<script>
// Prevent original image from loading before mask
aFade = 50, qFade = 0;
var mask = document.querySelector('#io-overlay>img');
function loaded() {
    var original = document.querySelector('#io-original');
    original.style.visibility = ""visible"";
}
if (mask === null || mask.complete) {
    loaded();
} else {
    mask.addEventListener('load', loaded);
}
</script>

// Toggle answer mask on clicking the image
var toggle = function() {
  var amask = document.getElementById('io-overlay');
  if (amask.style.display === 'block' || amask.style.display === '')
    amask.style.display = 'none';
  else
    amask.style.display = 'block'
}

  var amask = document.getElementById('io-overlay');
  if (amask.style.display === 'block' || amask.style.display === '')
    amask.style.display = 'none';

"Electrically <i>neutral </i>materials like plastic can <i>become</i> <u>charged</u> with excessive <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>friction</b></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>Chosen&nbsp;<u>arbitrarily</u>&nbsp;by physicists.</div></div><div><br></div><img src=""b1997557d6b1060427251b042bb3ac9d.png""></div>"
"The force on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>positive</u></span> charges point in&nbsp;the&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;same&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[opposite/same]</span> direction as the <b>electric field</b>."	"The force on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>positive</u></span> charges point in&nbsp;the&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i style="""">same</i></span> direction as the <b>electric field</b>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>Chosen&nbsp;<u>arbitrarily</u>&nbsp;by physicists.</div></div><div><br></div><img src=""b1997557d6b1060427251b042bb3ac9d.png""></div>"
"The electric force (E<sub>F</sub>) on <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;negative&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> charges point in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i style="""">opposite</i></span> direction as the <b>electric field</b>."	"The electric force (E<sub>F</sub>) on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>negative</u></span> charges point in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i style="""">opposite</i></span> direction as the <b>electric field</b>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The speed at which they move due to this electric field is its <u style=""font-weight: bold;"">drift velocity</u>&nbsp;(v<sub>d</sub>).</div><div><br></div><div>Chosen <u>arbitrarily</u> by physicists.</div><div><br></div><img src=""b1997557d6b1060427251b042bb3ac9d.png""></div>"
"The electric force (E<sub>F</sub>) on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>negative</u></span> charges point in the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;opposite&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[opposite/the same]</span> direction as the <b>electric field</b>."	"The electric force (E<sub>F</sub>) on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>negative</u></span> charges point in the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i style="""">opposite</i></span> direction as the <b>electric field</b>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The speed at which they move due to this electric field is its <u style=""font-weight: bold;"">drift velocity</u>&nbsp;(v<sub>d</sub>).</div><div><br></div><div>Chosen <u>arbitrarily</u> by physicists.</div><div><br></div><img src=""b1997557d6b1060427251b042bb3ac9d.png""></div>"
"<b>Electric field</b> lines <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> intersect."	"<b>Electric field</b> lines <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">cannot</i></span> intersect.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""d1478f2bcde5a0bbde63aef4530b1d4d.png""></div>"
"<b>Electric field lines</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""closer&amp;nbsp&#x3B;together"" data-ordinal=""1"">[closer/further]</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>stronger</i></u></span>."	"<b>Electric field lines</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">closer&nbsp;together</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>stronger</i></u></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""ff4e6b936f8ea9f74cb9f7dcc60c4363.png""></div>"
"If a test charge was placed on an <b>electric field's</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equipotential line</u></span>, it would experience <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""zero"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> net force."	"If a test charge was placed on an <b>electric field's</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equipotential line</u></span>, it would experience <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">zero</span> net force.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Voltage (electric potential) is the exact same at every point along a given equipotential line, meaning ΔV is zero.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""JLT_rl8xsUutkvzyD4pTWUFlXGT3mJ0yy5j5Avm9SYQccJFlMkMaieVcEVtbRfKz6MK6x7Zv7VXTqYqGJAa0E1IJjHRBa5kJF1Ib0phf_6RzkhuZm8d.png""><br></div></div>"
"If a test charge was placed on an <b>electric field's</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;equipotential&#x20;line&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, it would experience <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">zero</span> net force."	"If a test charge was placed on an <b>electric field's</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equipotential line</u></span>, it would experience <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">zero</span> net force.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Voltage (electric potential) is the exact same at every point along a given equipotential line, meaning ΔV is zero.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""JLT_rl8xsUutkvzyD4pTWUFlXGT3mJ0yy5j5Avm9SYQccJFlMkMaieVcEVtbRfKz6MK6x7Zv7VXTqYqGJAa0E1IJjHRBa5kJF1Ib0phf_6RzkhuZm8d.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Electric field lines</b> &amp; its <b>equipotential lines</b> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;perpendicular&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[parallel/perpendicular]</span> to each other."	"<b>Electric field lines</b> &amp; its <b>equipotential lines</b> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u><i>perpendicular</i></u></span> to each other.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""JLT_rl8xsUutkvzyD4pTWUFlXGT3mJ0yy5j5Avm9SYQccJFlMkMaieVcEVtbRfKz6MK6x7Zv7VXTqYqGJAa0E1IJjHRBa5kJF1Ib0phf_6RzkhuZm8d.png""><br></div></div>"
"Charges moving in the <i>opposite</i> direction as an <b>electric field</b> flow from a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;lower&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> potential to a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;&lt;u&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> potential."	"Charges moving in the <i>opposite</i> direction as an <b>electric field</b> flow from a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u><i>lower</i></u></span> potential to a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i><u>higher</u></i></span> potential.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>They're moving further away from the direction of the electric force, giving them more potential to do work.</div><div><br></div><div>Similar to how an object 10 feet about the ground has higher potential than one 2 feet above the ground.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""925fd16224268766c72c17c60aee7db3.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Voltage&#x20;&#x28;V&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">electric potential <i>difference</i></span> between points A &amp; B.<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Voltage (V)</b></span> represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">electric potential <i>difference</i></span> between points A &amp; B.<br></div>

"<div><b>Voltage</b>&nbsp;<b>(V)</b> is a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""scalar"" data-ordinal=""1"">[vector/scalar]</span> quantity.</div>"	"<div><b>Voltage</b>&nbsp;<b>(V)</b> is a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">scalar</span> quantity.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>To find the net voltage at a certain location due to multiple point charges, just <u><b>add all of them up</b></u> (<u>not</u> vector addition... regulat addition).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""53fc337aff0328f5acaf77d591a4b793.png""><br></div></div>"
"1 <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Volt&#x20;&#x28;V&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> = 1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Joule</span> / 1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Coulomb</span> (<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">J/C</span>)"	"1 <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Volt (V)</b></span> = 1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Joule</span> / 1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Coulomb</span> (<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">J/C</span>)

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Volt (V)</b></span> = 1 <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Joule"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> / 1 <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Coulomb"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> (<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""J&#x2F;C"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>)"	"1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Volt (V)</b></span> = 1 <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Joule</span> / 1 <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Coulomb</span> (<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">J/C</span>)

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""a7f8d7ff767a34973be00995acb0b059.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""What-is-Voltage.png""><br></div></div>"
"In a circuit, the <b>ground point</b> is the spot where the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""voltage&#x20;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>=</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""0"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In a circuit, the <b>ground point</b> is the spot where the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">voltage </span> <b>=</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">0</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""00070.png""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;dielectric&#x20;constant&#x20;&#x28;κ&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[constant]</span> represents the <i>difference</i> between the <u>electric force</u> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">vacuum</span> vs. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">actual medium</span> (i.e. solid)."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dielectric constant (κ)</b></span> represents the <i>difference</i> between the <u>electric force</u> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">vacuum</span> vs. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">actual medium</span> (i.e. solid).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Dielectric is the insulating material itself, but the constant represents how different its electric force is from a vacuum when it's inbetween the plates.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""cfd784030804de2f5cb8f05f6a9de34c.png""><br></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dielectric constant (κ)</b></span> represents the <i>difference</i> between the <u>electric force</u> in a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""vacuum"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> vs. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""actual&#x20;medium"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> (i.e. solid)."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dielectric constant (κ)</b></span> represents the <i>difference</i> between the <u>electric force</u> in a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">vacuum</span> vs. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">actual medium</span> (i.e. solid).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Dielectric is the insulating material itself, but the constant represents how different its electric force is from a vacuum when it's inbetween the plates.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""cfd784030804de2f5cb8f05f6a9de34c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b style="""">work</b>&nbsp;done <i>by</i> an <u style="""">electric field (E)</u></span> on a charge (+ or -) to move it from A → B:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;W&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x3D;&#x20;qEΔd"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b style="""">work</b>&nbsp;done <i>by</i> an <u style="""">electric field (E)</u></span> on a charge (+ or -) to move it from A → B:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b style="""">W</b>&nbsp;= qEΔd</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>True for both positive &amp; negative charges, so long as the electric field is constant.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div>ΔPE = -qEd</div><div><br></div><img src=""ba9449ac7aa69b6c0c1d2b5c026a62b5.png""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;work&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;done&#x20;&lt;i&gt;by&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;an&#x20;&lt;u&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;electric&#x20;field&#x20;&#x28;E&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> on a charge (+ or -) to move it from A → B:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b style="""">W</b>&nbsp;= qEΔd</span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b style="""">work</b>&nbsp;done <i>by</i> an <u style="""">electric field (E)</u></span> on a charge (+ or -) to move it from A → B:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b style="""">W</b>&nbsp;= qEΔd</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>True for both positive &amp; negative charges, so long as the electric field is constant.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div>ΔPE = -qEd</div><div><br></div><img src=""ba9449ac7aa69b6c0c1d2b5c026a62b5.png""></div>"
"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">positive</span> charge moves in the <i>same </i>direction as the <u>electric field</u>,&nbsp;<b>voltage</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;negative&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span>,&nbsp;<b>work </b>is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;negative&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> &amp; <b>ΔPE</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;negative&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span>."	"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">positive</span> charge moves in the <i>same </i>direction as the <u>electric field</u>,&nbsp;<b>voltage</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>negative</i></span>,&nbsp;<b>work </b>is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">negative</i></span> &amp; <b>ΔPE</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">negative</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Electric potential difference (ΔV) is defined as the amount of work needed to move a charge from point A → B.</div><div>- (-ΔV) → moving from high to low potential.</div><div>- (-Δwork) → must be negative since q is positive (according to the equation).</div><div>- (-ΔPE) → negative since work is negative (negative work signifies decreasing potential energy).</div><div><br></div><img src=""c9f10ebdc5c2a7735614f8b6ee57c5ae.png""><div></div></div>"
"When a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""positive"" data-ordinal=""2"">[positive/negative]</span> charge moves in the <i>same </i>direction as the <u>electric field</u>,&nbsp;<b>voltage</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>negative</i></span>,&nbsp;<b>work </b>is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">negative</i></span> &amp; <b>ΔPE</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">negative</i></span>."	"When a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">positive</span> charge moves in the <i>same </i>direction as the <u>electric field</u>,&nbsp;<b>voltage</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>negative</i></span>,&nbsp;<b>work </b>is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">negative</i></span> &amp; <b>ΔPE</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">negative</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Electric potential difference (ΔV) is defined as the amount of work needed to move a charge from point A → B.</div><div>- (-ΔV) → moving from high to low potential.</div><div>- (-Δwork) → must be negative since q is positive (according to the equation).</div><div>- (-ΔPE) → negative since work is negative (negative work signifies decreasing potential energy).</div><div><br></div><img src=""c9f10ebdc5c2a7735614f8b6ee57c5ae.png""><div></div></div>"
"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">positive</span> charge moves in the <i>opposite&nbsp;</i>direction as the <u>electric field</u>,&nbsp;<b>voltage</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span>&nbsp;<b>work </b>is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> &amp; <b>ΔPE</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span>."	"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">positive</span> charge moves in the <i>opposite&nbsp;</i>direction as the <u>electric field</u>,&nbsp;<b>voltage</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">positive</i></span>&nbsp;<b>work </b>is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">positive</i></span> &amp; <b>ΔPE</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">positive</i></span>.

"""Distance"" is a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;scalar&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[scalar/vector]</span> quantity &amp; ""displacement"" is a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;vector&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[scalar/vector]</span> quantity."	"""Distance"" is a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>scalar</u></span> quantity &amp; ""displacement"" is a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>vector</u></span> quantity.

"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Electric flux (φ<sub>E</sub>)</b></span> is the <i>number </i>of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electric field lines</u></span> <i>penetrating&nbsp;</i>through a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">closed surface</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"Electric <b>currents</b> point in the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;same&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[same/opposite]</span> direction as the <u>electric field</u>." <hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since both currents &amp; electric fields point in the direction positive charges <u>would</u> like to go.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""85686818e8b0d1bedab4a1d44b0700a1.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5afa317a59ee3e80f43f162f06264cc9.png""><br></div></div>"
"In conductors, electric <b>currents</b> flow from <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> electric potentials (V) to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> electric potentials (V)."	"In conductors, electric <b>currents</b> flow from <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> electric potentials (V) to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> electric potentials (V).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since they point in the <u>same direction as <b>positive charges</b></u> (which always flow from high → low electric potential).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""85686818e8b0d1bedab4a1d44b0700a1.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5afa317a59ee3e80f43f162f06264cc9.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Why do electric <b>currents </b>always flow from <i>high </i>electric potentials (V) to <i>low </i>electric potentials (V)?<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Currents&#x20;point&#x20;in&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;same&#x20;direction&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;as&#x20;&lt;u&gt;positive&#x20;charges&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x2C;&#x20;and&#x20;positive&#x20;charges&#x20;always&#x20;flow&#x20;from&#x20;&lt;i&gt;high&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;potential&#x20;→&#x20;&lt;i&gt;low&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;potential&#x20;in&#x20;electric&#x20;fields&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Why do electric <b>currents </b>always flow from <i>high </i>electric potentials (V) to <i>low </i>electric potentials (V)?<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Currents point in the <i>same direction</i> as <u>positive charges</u>, and positive charges always flow from <i>high </i>potential → <i>low </i>potential in electric fields.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""85686818e8b0d1bedab4a1d44b0700a1.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5afa317a59ee3e80f43f162f06264cc9.png""><br></div></div>"
"In <b>electric fields</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> charges spontaneously flow from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> electric potentials (V) to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> electric potentials (V)."	"In <b>electric fields</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>positive</u></span> charges spontaneously flow from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> electric potentials (V) to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> electric potentials (V).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>Similar to dropping an object from 10 feet above the ground. It's losing potential as it falls, since it's travelling in the same direction as the force (F<sub>g</sub>).<br></div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""925fd16224268766c72c17c60aee7db3.png""></div></div>"
"In <b>electric fields</b>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>positive</u></span> charges spontaneously flow from <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[high/low]</span> electric potentials (V) to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[high/low]</span> electric potentials (V)."	"In <b>electric fields</b>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>positive</u></span> charges spontaneously flow from <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> electric potentials (V) to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> electric potentials (V).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>Similar to dropping an object from 10 feet above the ground. It's losing potential as it falls, since it's travelling in the same direction as the force (F<sub>g</sub>).<br></div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""925fd16224268766c72c17c60aee7db3.png""></div></div>"
"In <b>electric fields</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;negative&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> charges flow from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> electric potentials (V) to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> electric potentials (V)."	"In <b>electric fields</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>negative</u></span> charges flow from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> electric potentials (V) to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> electric potentials (V).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>They're moving further away from the direction of the electric force, giving them more potential to do work.</div><div><br></div><div>Similar to how an object 10 feet about the ground has higher potential than one 2 feet above the ground.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""925fd16224268766c72c17c60aee7db3.png""></div></div>"
"In <b>electric fields</b>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>negative</u></span> charges flow from <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[high/low]</span> electric potentials (V) to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[high/low]</span> electric potentials (V)."	"In <b>electric fields</b>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>negative</u></span> charges flow from <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> electric potentials (V) to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> electric potentials (V).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>They're moving further away from the direction of the electric force, giving them more potential to do work.</div><div><br></div><div>Similar to how an object 10 feet about the ground has higher potential than one 2 feet above the ground.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""925fd16224268766c72c17c60aee7db3.png""></div></div>"
"In a conductor's <b>current</b>, <i>increasing</i> the <u>voltage</u> will <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increase&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increase/decrease]</span> the electrons' <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>drift velocities</u></span>."	"In a conductor's <b>current</b>, <i>increasing</i> the <u>voltage</u> will <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increase</i></span> the electrons' <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>drift velocities</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Also evident through Ohm's Law (I = V/R).</div><div><br></div><img src=""f270e1e457ac656a8ed2a7d0528359de.png""></div>"
"In a conductor's <b>current</b>, <i>increasing</i> the <u>voltage</u> will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increase</i></span> the electrons' <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;drift&#x20;velocities&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"In a conductor's <b>current</b>, <i>increasing</i> the <u>voltage</u> will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increase</i></span> the electrons' <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>drift velocities</u></span>.

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Voltage provides the potential difference that drives the movement of charge.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""85686818e8b0d1bedab4a1d44b0700a1.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5afa317a59ee3e80f43f162f06264cc9.png""><br></div></div>"
"Electrons move around <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>randomly</i></span> in a conductor when an <b>electric current</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;is&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[is/is not]</span> applied."	"Electrons move around <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>randomly</i></span> in a conductor when an <b>electric current</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i style="""">is not</i></span> applied.

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""8bc63df46102735696ac1983e1326ad2.png""></div>"
"In batteries, electrons <i>flow</i> from the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""anode"" data-ordinal=""1"">[anode/cath.]</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""cathode"" data-ordinal=""1"">[anode/cath.]</span>."	"In batteries, electrons <i>flow</i> from the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">anode</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">cathode</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Currents (I) point from the cathode → anode, but that's because current represents the direction positive charges would flow.</div><div><br></div><div>Anode (-): source of electrons (oxidation).</div><div>Cathode (+): receives the electrons (reduction).</div><div><br></div><img src=""8bc63df46102735696ac1983e1326ad2.png""></div>"
"The <i>symbol </i>for the <b>electromotive force</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ε"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"The <i>symbol </i>for the <b>electromotive force</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ε</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""922a862569bba589ee4c40ddd1f1fddc.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In <b>circuit diagrams</b>, <i>taller</i> lines represent <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""positive"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> terminals with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> potentials (V).</div><div><br></div><img src=""922a862569bba589ee4c40ddd1f1fddc.png"">"	"<div>In <b>circuit diagrams</b>, <i>taller</i> lines represent <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">positive</span> terminals with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> potentials (V).</div><div><br></div><img src=""922a862569bba589ee4c40ddd1f1fddc.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b><u>Cathode</u></b></div>"
"<div>In&nbsp;<b>circuit diagrams</b>,&nbsp;<i>shorter</i>&nbsp;lines represent <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""negative"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> terminals with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;lower&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> potentials (V).</div><div><br></div><img src=""922a862569bba589ee4c40ddd1f1fddc.png""><div></div>"	"<div>In&nbsp;<b>circuit diagrams</b>,&nbsp;<i>shorter</i>&nbsp;lines represent <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">negative</span> terminals with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>lower</i></span> potentials (V).</div><div><br></div><img src=""922a862569bba589ee4c40ddd1f1fddc.png""><div></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b style=""text-decoration-line: underline;"">Anode</b><div>- Direction of electron flow goes from anode → cathode.<br><div>- Direction of the circuit flows from the cathode → anode (since currents point in the opposite direction as the flow of electrons).</div></div></div>"
"<div>Good <u>insulators</u> have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> <b>resistivities (ρ)</b>.</div>"	"<div>Good <u>insulators</u> have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <b>resistivities (ρ)</b>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""524cb7ba2aefc97b9cbc7ffc9c58bb2c.png""></div>"
"<div>Good <u>conductors</u> have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> <b>resistivities (ρ)</b>.</div>"	"<div>Good <u>conductors</u> have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> <b>resistivities (ρ)</b>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""524cb7ba2aefc97b9cbc7ffc9c58bb2c.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Resistivity&#x20;&#x28;ρ&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is a constant given to a particular material that represents how <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>hard</i></span> it is for it to act as a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>conductor</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Resistivity (ρ)</b></span> is a constant given to a particular material that represents how <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>hard</i></span> it is for it to act as a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>conductor</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Also referred to as ""resistance per unit length.""</div><div><br></div><div>Low ρ = good conductor.</div><div>High ρ = good insulator.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""8aaca3f1d631fe67dab7915522392490.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""524cb7ba2aefc97b9cbc7ffc9c58bb2c.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Resistivity (ρ)</b></span> is a constant given to a particular material that represents how <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;hard&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> it is for it to act as a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;conductor&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[conductor/insulator]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Resistivity (ρ)</b></span> is a constant given to a particular material that represents how <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>hard</i></span> it is for it to act as a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>conductor</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Also referred to as ""resistance per unit length.""</div><div><br></div><div>Low ρ = good conductor.</div><div>High ρ = good insulator.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""8aaca3f1d631fe67dab7915522392490.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""524cb7ba2aefc97b9cbc7ffc9c58bb2c.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Current&#x20;density&#x20;&#x28;J&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>number&nbsp;</i>of charged molecules (+ <i>and</i> -)</span> that flow through a current."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Current density (J)</b></span> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>number&nbsp;</i>of charged molecules (+ <i>and</i> -)</span> that flow through a current.

<div><b>Δq</b> = (current density) x (1.6 x 10<sup>-19</sup> C)</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""85686818e8b0d1bedab4a1d44b0700a1.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5afa317a59ee3e80f43f162f06264cc9.png""></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Current density (J)</b></span> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;number&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;of&#x20;charged&#x20;molecules&#x20;&#x28;&#x2B;&#x20;&lt;i&gt;and&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&#x2D;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> that flow through a current."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Current density (J)</b></span> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>number&nbsp;</i>of charged molecules (+ <i>and</i> -)</span> that flow through a current.

"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;resistivity&#x20;&#x28;ρ&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of a material:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""8aaca3f1d631fe67dab7915522392490.png""></span><br>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>resistivity (ρ)</b></span> of a material:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""8aaca3f1d631fe67dab7915522392490.png""></span><br>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Low ρ = good conductor.</div><div>High ρ = good insulator.</div><div><br></div><img src=""524cb7ba2aefc97b9cbc7ffc9c58bb2c.png""></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Low ρ = good conductor.</div><div>High ρ = good insulator.</div><div><br></div><img src=""524cb7ba2aefc97b9cbc7ffc9c58bb2c.png""></div>"
"Electrons <b>drift </b>in the <i>opposite</i>&nbsp;direction<i> </i>of currents due to the applied <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""force&#x20;&#x28;F&lt;sub&gt;e&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <i>from </i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""electric&#x20;field&#x20;&#x28;E&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"Electrons <b>drift </b>in the <i>opposite</i>&nbsp;direction<i> </i>of currents due to the applied <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">force (F<sub>e</sub>)</span> <i>from </i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">electric field (E)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The electric force (F<sub>e</sub>) always points opposite to the direction of the electric field when acting on negative charges.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""f9e416b64d6bf3fdf62cc69c9d37fbb3.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5afa317a59ee3e80f43f162f06264cc9.png""></div><div></div></div>"
"<div>List the 3 separate equations for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">energy <i>dissipated</i> by a <u>resistor</u> (power)</span> in a circuit:<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;cc803dee7c71197b837c7ec69ee8a30f1c421ff3&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div>List the 3 separate equations for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">energy <i>dissipated</i> by a <u>resistor</u> (power)</span> in a circuit:<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-cc803dee7c71197b837c7ec69ee8a30f1c421ff3.jpg""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>P</b> = power; <b>I</b> = current; <b>V</b> = voltage; <b>R</b> = resistance.<div>- This is known as <b>power</b>, and it's released as heat.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-073412150b76e7ef9c982206f5ff995ab8bea3d5.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>List the 3 separate equations for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""energy&#x20;&lt;i&gt;dissipated&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;by&#x20;a&#x20;&lt;u&gt;resistor&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;power&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in a circuit:<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-cc803dee7c71197b837c7ec69ee8a30f1c421ff3.jpg""></span><br></div>"	"<div>List the 3 separate equations for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">energy <i>dissipated</i> by a <u>resistor</u> (power)</span> in a circuit:<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-cc803dee7c71197b837c7ec69ee8a30f1c421ff3.jpg""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>P</b> = power; <b>I</b> = current; <b>V</b> = voltage; <b>R</b> = resistance.<div>- This is known as <b>power</b>, and it's released as heat.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-073412150b76e7ef9c982206f5ff995ab8bea3d5.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"As a current (I) passes through a <b>resistor</b>, a <i>small</i> amount of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energy</u></span> from the electrons is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">dissipated as heat</span> - known as the resistor's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;power&#x20;&#x28;P&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"As a current (I) passes through a <b>resistor</b>, a <i>small</i> amount of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energy</u></span> from the electrons is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">dissipated as heat</span> - known as the resistor's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>power (P)</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The current is the thing delivering energy to the resistor (in the form of the electron's kinetic energy).</div><div>- Don't confuse this with the current being dissipated!<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-073412150b76e7ef9c982206f5ff995ab8bea3d5.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"As a current (I) passes through a <b>resistor</b>, a <i>small</i> amount of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;kinetic&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> from the electrons is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""dissipated&#x20;as&#x20;heat"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - known as the resistor's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>power (P)</b></span>."	"As a current (I) passes through a <b>resistor</b>, a <i>small</i> amount of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energy</u></span> from the electrons is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">dissipated as heat</span> - known as the resistor's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>power (P)</b></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Both left &amp; right sides of each capacitor are connected directly to the battery. This gives them each the <u>same</u> <b>potential difference</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""ea2ea731e78560b906723c2d6ea1bc10.png""><br></div>"

"<b>Capacitors</b> in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>series</u></span> are <i>not </i>connected directly to the battery - most are connected to <i>each other</i>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because the capacitors will have their maximum charges.</div><div><br></div><img src=""ea2ea731e78560b906723c2d6ea1bc10.png""></div>"
"<div>Total <i>amount</i> of&nbsp;<b>charge (q)</b> stored<i> </i>by two capacitors in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;parallel&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[parallel/series]</span> in a circuit:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><img src=""b4032d62d69c770f476c6e30fac0723a.png""></span><br>"	"<div>Total <i>amount</i> of&nbsp;<b>charge (q)</b> stored<i> </i>by two capacitors in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>parallel</u></span> in a circuit:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><img src=""b4032d62d69c770f476c6e30fac0723a.png""></span><br>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"The <i>total </i><b>capacitance (C)</b> of capacitors in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;parallel&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[parallel/series]</span> will always&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>greater</i></span> than the capacitance of one on its own."	"The <i>total </i><b>capacitance (C)</b> of capacitors in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>parallel</u></span> will always&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>greater</i></span> than the capacitance of one on its own.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""1503bf82589851867bf85b39f6d51303.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""ea2ea731e78560b906723c2d6ea1bc10.png""></div>"
"The <i>total </i><b>capacitance (C)</b> of capacitors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>parallel</u></span> will always&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;greater&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[greater/lower]</span> than the capacitance of one on its own."	"The <i>total </i><b>capacitance (C)</b> of capacitors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>parallel</u></span> will always&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>greater</i></span> than the capacitance of one on its own.

<div><img src=""1503bf82589851867bf85b39f6d51303.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""ea2ea731e78560b906723c2d6ea1bc10.png""></div>"
"The <i>total </i><b>capacitance (C)</b> of capacitors in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;series&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[parallel/series]</span> will always&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>lower</i></span> than the capacitance of one on its own."	"The <i>total </i><b>capacitance (C)</b> of capacitors in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>series</u></span> will always&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>lower</i></span> than the capacitance of one on its own.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Keep in mind that the equivalent capacitance of capacitors in series is essentially the same as one single capacitor with its plates spaced far apart. As you increase the distance between the plates of a capacitor, the capacitance decreases (according to the parallel plates capacitor equation).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6ef17646905d98b8aed70c7d164216b1.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""8298491c41c0f5f2c6d81480a01f8426.png""><br></div>"
"The <i>total </i><b>capacitance (C)</b> of capacitors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>series</u></span> will always&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;lower&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[greater/lower]</span> than the capacitance of one on its own."	"The <i>total </i><b>capacitance (C)</b> of capacitors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>series</u></span> will always&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>lower</i></span> than the capacitance of one on its own.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Keep in mind that the equivalent capacitance of capacitors in series is essentially the same as one single capacitor with its plates spaced far apart. As you increase the distance between the plates of a capacitor, the capacitance decreases (according to the parallel plates capacitor equation).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6ef17646905d98b8aed70c7d164216b1.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""8298491c41c0f5f2c6d81480a01f8426.png""><br></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;charges&#x20;&#x28;q&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <i>stored </i>on <b>capacitors</b> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">series</span> must always<i> </i>be <i>equal</i> in magnitude."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>charges (q)</u></span> <i>stored </i>on <b>capacitors</b> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">series</span> must always<i> </i>be <i>equal</i> in magnitude.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...due to the <b>conservation of charge</b> (considering they're all connected to each other).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6ef17646905d98b8aed70c7d164216b1.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""8298491c41c0f5f2c6d81480a01f8426.png""><br></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>charges (q)</u></span> <i>stored </i>on <b>capacitors</b> in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""series"" data-ordinal=""2"">[series/parallel]</span> must always<i> </i>be <i>equal</i> in magnitude."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>charges (q)</u></span> <i>stored </i>on <b>capacitors</b> in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">series</span> must always<i> </i>be <i>equal</i> in magnitude.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...due to the <b>conservation of charge</b> (considering they're all connected to each other).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6ef17646905d98b8aed70c7d164216b1.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""8298491c41c0f5f2c6d81480a01f8426.png""><br></div>"
"<div>For <b>capacitors </b>in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""series"" data-ordinal=""2"">[series/parallel]</span>, the battery's voltage (V) will be the <i>sum </i>of the voltages (V) of <i>each </i>capacitor.</div>"	"<div>For <b>capacitors </b>in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">series</span>, the battery's voltage (V) will be the <i>sum </i>of the voltages (V) of <i>each </i>capacitor.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Remember, capacitors in series will have different voltages but the same charges.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ea2f7a5d03c31a197dc5be3786e578c4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6ef17646905d98b8aed70c7d164216b1.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""8298491c41c0f5f2c6d81480a01f8426.png""><br></div>"

<div><img src=""8298491c41c0f5f2c6d81480a01f8426.png""><br></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""8298491c41c0f5f2c6d81480a01f8426.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Resistors&nbsp;</b>in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""series"" data-ordinal=""1"">[parallel/series]</span> have the&nbsp;<i>same&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>currents (I)</u></span> across them."	"<b>Resistors&nbsp;</b>in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span> have the&nbsp;<i>same&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>currents (I)</u></span> across them.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Resitors in series are ""one after the other."" Any amount of charge that flows through the first resistor will be the same in the second (where else would the flowing charge go otherwise?)</div><div>- Resistors in <u>parallel</u> are the ones with the same voltages (V).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e36c3b281f68e6a7a916ee87952f9292.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""63f47c687c85c091bb27a1791db49fbd.png""></div>"
"<b>Resistors&nbsp;</b>in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span> have the&nbsp;<i>same&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;currents&#x20;&#x28;I&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> across them."	"<b>Resistors&nbsp;</b>in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span> have the&nbsp;<i>same&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>currents (I)</u></span> across them.
<hr>

Any amount of charge that flows through the first resistor will be the same in the second (where else would the flowing charge go otherwise?)</div><div>- Resistors in <u>parallel</u> are the ones with the same voltages (V).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e36c3b281f68e6a7a916ee87952f9292.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""63f47c687c85c091bb27a1791db49fbd.png""></div>"

<div><b>Gauss (G)</b>: CGS unit for magnetic fields.</div><div><b>Tesla (T)</b>: SI unit for magnetic fields.</div><div><br></div><img src=""6cae4b2eefd087e5c6de5dcf5ed91f77.png""></div>"
"In the units for <u>magnetic fields</u>, 1 <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;gauss&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> = <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1 x 10<sup>-4</sup></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;T&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"In the units for <u>magnetic fields</u>, 1 <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>gauss</b></span> = <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1 x 10<sup>-4</sup></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>T</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Gauss (G)</b>: CGS unit for magnetic fields.</div><div><b>Tesla (T)</b>: SI unit for magnetic fields.</div><div><br></div><img src=""6cae4b2eefd087e5c6de5dcf5ed91f77.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Magnetic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>fields </b>are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>dipolar</i></span>, meaning they have a <u>north</u> &amp; <u>south</u> pole."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Magnetic</b></span> <b>fields </b>are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>dipolar</i></span>, meaning they have a <u>north</u> &amp; <u>south</u> pole.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""6cae4b2eefd087e5c6de5dcf5ed91f77.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Magnetic</b></span> <b>fields </b>are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;dipolar&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, meaning they have a <u>north</u> &amp; <u>south</u> pole."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Magnetic</b></span> <b>fields </b>are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>dipolar</i></span>, meaning they have a <u>north</u> &amp; <u>south</u> pole.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""6cae4b2eefd087e5c6de5dcf5ed91f77.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Magnetic field</b> lines <i>leave</i> from the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""north"" data-ordinal=""1"">[north/south]</span> pole &amp; <i>re-enter</i> through the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""south"" data-ordinal=""1"">[north/south]</span> pole."	"<b>Magnetic field</b> lines <i>leave</i> from the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">north</span> pole &amp; <i>re-enter</i> through the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">south</span> pole.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""6cae4b2eefd087e5c6de5dcf5ed91f77.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Magnetic fields</b>&nbsp;are denoted with the symbol <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""B"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Magnetic fields</b>&nbsp;are denoted with the symbol <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">B</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""6cae4b2eefd087e5c6de5dcf5ed91f77.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Magnetic fields</b> exert the <i>greatest</i> <u>force</u> on charges when their velocities are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""perpendicular"" data-ordinal=""1"">[parallel/perpendicular]</span> to the field lines."	"<b>Magnetic fields</b> exert the <i>greatest</i> <u>force</u> on charges when their velocities are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">perpendicular</span> to the field lines.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since a perpendicular angle would give you <b>sin(90)</b>, which is 1.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""f6296433cbb39d861660d81cd8ca30b9.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div><b>Magnetic fields</b> exert <i>zero</i> <u>force</u> on charges when their velocities are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""parallel"" data-ordinal=""1"">[parallel/perpendicular]</span> to the field lines.<br></div>"	"<div><b>Magnetic fields</b> exert <i>zero</i> <u>force</u> on charges when their velocities are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">parallel</span> to the field lines.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since a parallel angle would give you <b>sin(0)</b>, which is 0.</div><div>- Called the&nbsp;<b>magnetic</b>&nbsp;<b>Lorentz force</b>&nbsp;(which is a combination of the magnetic force &amp; electric field force on a charged particle).<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""f6296433cbb39d861660d81cd8ca30b9.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>The right-hand rule for <b>magnetic fields</b>&nbsp;on <i>positive </i>charges shows the <u>thumb</u> points in the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""magnetic&#x20;&lt;i&gt;force&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x28;F&lt;sub&gt;M&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>The right-hand rule for <b>magnetic fields</b>&nbsp;on <i>positive </i>charges shows the <u>thumb</u> points in the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">magnetic <i>force </i>(F<sub>M</sub>)</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""></div>"
"<div>For <b>magnetic fields</b> acting on <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""positive"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> charges, use the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">right</span>-hand rule.</div><div><br></div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br>"	"<div>For <b>magnetic fields</b> acting on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">positive</span> charges, use the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">right</span>-hand rule.</div><div><br></div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<div>The right-hand rule for <b>magnetic fields</b>&nbsp;on <i>positive</i> charges shows the <u>middle&nbsp;finger</u>&nbsp;point in the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""magnetic&#x20;&lt;i&gt;field&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x28;B&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>The right-hand rule for <b>magnetic fields</b>&nbsp;on <i>positive</i> charges shows the <u>middle&nbsp;finger</u>&nbsp;point in the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">magnetic <i>field</i>&nbsp;(B)</span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;magnetic&#x20;force&#x20;&#x28;F&lt;sub&gt;M&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> exerted&nbsp;<i>on</i> a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""moving&#x20;charge"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""f6296433cbb39d861660d81cd8ca30b9.png""></span><br>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>magnetic force (F<sub>M</sub>)</b></span> exerted&nbsp;<i>on</i> a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">moving charge</span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""f6296433cbb39d861660d81cd8ca30b9.png""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>F</b> = force; <b>q </b>= charge; <b>v</b> = velocity of the moving charge; <b>B</b> = magnetic field; <b>θ</b> = angle between the charge's velocity &amp; the magnetic field.</div><div>-&nbsp;<u>Right-hand rule</u> shows the direction of the <b>magnetic field (B)</b>, <b>magnetic force (F)</b>, and the <b>velocity (i)&nbsp;</b>of the charged particle.</div><div>- Called the <b>magnetic</b>&nbsp;<b>Lorentz force</b>&nbsp;(which is a combination of the magnetic force &amp; electric field force on a charged particle).</div><div><br></div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br></div>"

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>F</b> = force; <b>q </b>= charge; <b>v</b> = velocity of the moving charge; <b>B</b> = magnetic field; <b>θ</b> = angle between the charge's velocity &amp; the magnetic field.</div><div>-&nbsp;<u>Right-hand rule</u> shows the direction of the <b>magnetic field (B)</b>, <b>magnetic force (F)</b>, and the <b>velocity (i)&nbsp;</b>of the charged particle.</div><div>- Called the <b>magnetic</b>&nbsp;<b>Lorentz force</b>&nbsp;(which is a combination of the magnetic force &amp; electric field force on a charged particle).</div><div><br></div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br></div>"
"<div>For <b>magnetic fields</b> acting on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">positive</span> charges, use the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""right"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>-hand rule.</div><div><br></div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br>"	"<div>For <b>magnetic fields</b> acting on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">positive</span> charges, use the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">right</span>-hand rule.</div><div><br></div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br>

</div>"

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<div>In <b>magnetic fields</b>, the <u>charge's velocity</u> is <i>perpendicular </i>to the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""magnetic&#x20;&lt;i&gt;force&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x28;F&lt;sub&gt;M&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>In <b>magnetic fields</b>, the <u>charge's velocity</u> is <i>perpendicular </i>to the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">magnetic <i>force </i>(F<sub>M</sub>)</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is why charged particles enter into circular motion after being placed in a uniform magnetic field (initial velocity &amp; F<sub>M</sub> are always perpendicualr).</div><div>- Does <u>not</u> need to be exactly perpendicular to the magnetic field lines.</div><div><br></div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""></div>"
"<div>In <b>magnetic fields</b>, the <u>magnetic field <i>lines</i></u> are&nbsp;<i>perpendicular </i>to the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""magnetic&#x20;&lt;i&gt;force&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x28;F&lt;sub&gt;M&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>In <b>magnetic fields</b>, the <u>magnetic field <i>lines</i></u> are&nbsp;<i>perpendicular </i>to the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">magnetic <i>force </i>(F<sub>M</sub>)</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Does <u>not</u> need to be exactly perpendicular to the charge's velocity.</div><div><br></div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""></div>"
"<div>These show the <b>magnetic field lines</b> are directed <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;out&#x20;of&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> the page.</div><div><br></div><img src=""e63bff807016416c8667bfa1c9e7bfd6.png"">"	"<div>These show the <b>magnetic field lines</b> are directed <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>out of</i></span> the page.</div><div><br></div><img src=""e63bff807016416c8667bfa1c9e7bfd6.png"">

"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>potential&nbsp;energy <i style="""">stored</i></u></span> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>capacitor</b></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>potential&nbsp;energy <i style="""">stored</i></u></span> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>capacitor</b></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""></span></div>

-&nbsp;<b>C</b> = capacitance; <b>V</b> = voltage; <b>PE<sub>q</sub></b> = energy stored.<div>- The energy stored in the capacitor is the energy stored in the electric field between its plates.</div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;potential&amp;nbsp&#x3B;energy&#x20;&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;stored&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;capacitor&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""></span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>potential&nbsp;energy <i style="""">stored</i></u></span> in a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>capacitor</b></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""></span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>C</b> = capacitance; <b>V</b> = voltage; <b>PE<sub>q</sub></b> = energy stored.<div>- The energy stored in the capacitor is the energy stored in the electric field between its plates.</div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;electric&#x20;potential&#x20;&#x28;V&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>inside</i></span> a conducting sphere is <u>equal</u> to the potential on its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">surface</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>electric potential (V)</b></span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>inside</i></span> a conducting sphere is <u>equal</u> to the potential on its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">surface</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""Electric+Potential+Conducting+Sphere+Example.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>magnetic </b><span style=""font-weight: bold;"">force</span><b>&nbsp;</b><b style="""">(F</b><sub style=""font-weight: bold;"">M</sub><b style="""">)</b></span> <i>exerted on</i> a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>current-carrying wire</span></u>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;cc92e40e479e3fa12027e7c6cba20ec4&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>magnetic </b><span style=""font-weight: bold;"">force</span><b>&nbsp;</b><b style="""">(F</b><sub style=""font-weight: bold;"">M</sub><b style="""">)</b></span> <i>exerted on</i> a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>current-carrying wire</span></u>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""cc92e40e479e3fa12027e7c6cba20ec4.png""></span>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- When the magnetic field is directed into the page (x), the magnetic force is directed towards the left (using the right-hand rule).</div><div>- Force = 0 when current = 0, since the charges wouldn't have any velocity (magnetic forces are only exerted on charges in movement).</div><div>- Current and the magnetic field must be at right angles to each other.</div><div><br></div><img src=""7b48b1fa4a923b831c8bed3d5204e7a1.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;magnetic&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&lt;span&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;font&#x2D;weight&#x3A;&#x20;bold&#x3B;&quot;&gt;force&lt;&#x2F;span&gt;&lt;b&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&lt;b&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;&#x28;F&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&lt;sub&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;font&#x2D;weight&#x3A;&#x20;bold&#x3B;&quot;&gt;M&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;b&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <i>exerted on</i> a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;current&#x2D;carrying&#x20;wire"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></u>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""cc92e40e479e3fa12027e7c6cba20ec4.png""></span>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>magnetic </b><span style=""font-weight: bold;"">force</span><b>&nbsp;</b><b style="""">(F</b><sub style=""font-weight: bold;"">M</sub><b style="""">)</b></span> <i>exerted on</i> a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>current-carrying wire</span></u>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""cc92e40e479e3fa12027e7c6cba20ec4.png""></span>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- When the magnetic field is directed into the page (x), the magnetic force is directed towards the left (using the right-hand rule).</div><div>- Force = 0 when current = 0, since the charges wouldn't have any velocity (magnetic forces are only exerted on charges in movement).</div><div>- Current and the magnetic field must be at right angles to each other.</div><div><br></div><img src=""7b48b1fa4a923b831c8bed3d5204e7a1.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Use this version of the ""right-hand rule"" to determine the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""magnetic&#x20;field&#x20;&#x28;B&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <i>generated </i>by&nbsp;a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""current&#x2D;carrying&#x20;wire"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><img src=""c9952c43d4cb7e3a6ed4f08a784361f0.png""><br>"	"<div>Use this version of the ""right-hand rule"" to determine the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">magnetic field (B)</span> <i>generated </i>by&nbsp;a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">current-carrying wire</span>:</div><div><br></div><img src=""c9952c43d4cb7e3a6ed4f08a784361f0.png""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Magnetic field lines point tangent to the ""circle"" around the current (shown as being tangent to the blue line).<div><br></div><div><img src=""c28ee8b5003139c5291e25f9a469afae.png""><br></div></div>"
"Equation for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>magnetic <i>flux</i>&nbsp;(φ<sub>B</sub>)</b></span> passing through a surface:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;350757c93b05b85ebd90a31f8e119be4&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"Equation for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>magnetic <i>flux</i>&nbsp;(φ<sub>B</sub>)</b></span> passing through a surface:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""350757c93b05b85ebd90a31f8e119be4.png""></span><br></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is the <u>same</u> as <b>electric flux</b> passing through a surface.</div><div>- <b>θ</b> is the angle between the normal line and the magnetic field line passing through the surface.</div><div><br></div><img src=""43176d506adcf438a1b2ca640860b08e.png""><br></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is the <u>same</u> as <b>electric flux</b> passing through a surface.</div><div>- <b>θ</b> is the angle between the normal line and the magnetic field line passing through the surface.</div><div><br></div><img src=""43176d506adcf438a1b2ca640860b08e.png""><br></div>"
"<div>As the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;magnetic&#x20;flux&#x20;&#x28;φ&lt;sub&gt;B&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<i>changes&nbsp;</i>through a loop, the <u>induced<i> </i>emf</u> generates a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>current (I)</b></span> with a direction<i> </i>that gives a magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite</i></span> in sign.</div>"	"<div>As the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;<i>changes&nbsp;</i>through a loop, the <u>induced<i> </i>emf</u> generates a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>current (I)</b></span> with a direction<i> </i>that gives a magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite</i></span> in sign.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b><u>Lenz's Law</u></b>.</div><div>- Change in original magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>) could either increase or decrease - the induced current's magnetic flux will always be opposite in sign.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""05a07731e83c28cf350de95fb15c4332.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""f4a11fd7a41001b13ade6230ff2512d0.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2d5c79a3dd881007955d048fcdd3e668.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>As the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;<i>changes&nbsp;</i>through a loop, the <u>induced<i> </i>emf</u> generates a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>current (I)</b></span> with a direction<i> </i>that gives a magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;opposite&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[identical/opposite]</span> in sign.</div>"	"<div>As the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;<i>changes&nbsp;</i>through a loop, the <u>induced<i> </i>emf</u> generates a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>current (I)</b></span> with a direction<i> </i>that gives a magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite</i></span> in sign.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b><u>Lenz's Law</u></b>.</div><div>- Change in original magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>) could either increase or decrease - the induced current's magnetic flux will always be opposite in sign.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""05a07731e83c28cf350de95fb15c4332.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""f4a11fd7a41001b13ade6230ff2512d0.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2d5c79a3dd881007955d048fcdd3e668.png""><br></div></div>"

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>N</b> is the number of loops; <b>φ<sub>B</sub></b>&nbsp;is the magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>=BAcosθ).</div><div>- As the magnetic flux changes over time (due to the magnetic field moving closer/further away), a current is generated that produces a magnetic flux opposite in sign - Lenz's Law.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2d5c79a3dd881007955d048fcdd3e668.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;current&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>'s emf <i>induced </i>in a loop due to a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;changing&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;b&gt;magnetic&#x20;flux&#x20;&#x28;φ&lt;sub&gt;B&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""05a07731e83c28cf350de95fb15c4332.png""></span><br>"	"<div>Equation for a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>current</b></span>'s emf <i>induced </i>in a loop due to a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>changing </i><b>magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>)</b></span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""05a07731e83c28cf350de95fb15c4332.png""></span><br>

<div>- <b>N</b> is the number of loops; <b>φ<sub>B</sub></b>&nbsp;is the magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>=BAcosθ).</div><div>- As the magnetic flux changes over time (due to the magnetic field moving closer/further away), a current is generated that produces a magnetic flux opposite in sign - Lenz's Law.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2d5c79a3dd881007955d048fcdd3e668.png""><br></div></div>"

"As the <b>magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span> through a current-carrying loop, the <i><u>induced</u> </i><b>magnetic flux</b> points in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite</i></span> direction."	"As the <b>magnetic flux (φ<sub>B</sub>)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> through a current-carrying loop, the <i><u>induced</u> </i><b>magnetic flux</b> points in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite</i></span> direction.
