<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- Because of the angle of the reflected light off the mirror (below), it <u>appears</u> as if the light converges on a point behind the mirror.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Real</b>: light converges in front of the image (always inverted).</div><div><br></div><img src=""61111c6efea1d791bab3f6ed8848d2c0.png""><br></div></div>"

"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>virtual</b></span> <b>images</b>, the reflected image appears to be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>the same</i></span> <u>distance</u> from the mirror as the real object.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>virtual</b></span> <b>images</b>, the reflected image appears to be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;the&#x20;same&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[same/different]</span> <u>distance</u> from the mirror as the real object."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>virtual</b></span> <b>images</b>, the reflected image appears to be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>the same</i></span> <u>distance</u> from the mirror as the real object.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- Because of the angle of the reflected light off the mirror (below), it <u>appears</u> as if the light converges on a point behind the mirror.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Real</b>: light converges in front of the image (always inverted).</div><div><br></div><img src=""61111c6efea1d791bab3f6ed8848d2c0.png""><br></div></div>"

"<div><b>Sound </b><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;intensity&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is <i>directly </i>proportional to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">amplitude<sup>2</sup></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">frequency<sup>2</sup></span>.</div>"	"<div><b>Sound </b><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intensity</b></span> is <i>directly </i>proportional to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">amplitude<sup>2</sup></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">frequency<sup>2</sup></span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>I</b> = amplitude<sup>2</sup>.</div><div>- Doubling the intensity results in an amplitude 4x higher.</div>-&nbsp;<b>I</b> = intensity; <b>P</b> = power; <b>A</b> = area.<div><br></div><div><img src=""fd82ff4d9efba05c22b2f87bf9ff0cbd.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""z_Ug3KKiwneEg_myMfhR8uGT1wKOMnGnVSqK2FNRau_35A6ZuZ_PiZgV9eoxgN5dfOgBCx8Qa7_g4TpiptYlZH6eH-otWPo8rYxJuRaJtxUww5hflkt.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div><b>Sound </b><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intensity</b></span> is <i>directly </i>proportional to its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""amplitude&lt;sup&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[wave property]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""frequency&lt;sup&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div><b>Sound </b><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intensity</b></span> is <i>directly </i>proportional to its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">amplitude<sup>2</sup></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">frequency<sup>2</sup></span>.</div>

"<div><b>Sound </b><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;intensity&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <i>decreases </i>as the <u>distance</u><sup>2</sup> between the source &amp; observer <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span>.</div>"	"<div><b>Sound </b><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intensity</b></span> <i>decreases </i>as the <u>distance</u><sup>2</sup> between the source &amp; observer <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>β</b>&nbsp;= sound level;&nbsp;<b>I<sub>f</sub></b>&nbsp;=&nbsp;<u>final</u>&nbsp;intensity after the sound level was changed; I<sub>i</sub>&nbsp;=&nbsp;<u>initial</u>&nbsp;intensity before sound level was changed.<br></div><div><div>- To find just the first sound level alone (β<sub>i</sub>), use the below equation (I<sub>0</sub>&nbsp;= 1 x 10<sup>-12</sup>&nbsp;W/m<sup>2</sup>).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6f86409c4e713032b36aeb82f95a8cb8.png""></div></div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Sound&#x20;attenuation&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> occurs when the sound wave's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>amplitude</u></span> <i>diminishes </i>in magnitude over time due to air resistance, friction, etc.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Sound attenuation</b></span> occurs when the sound wave's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>amplitude</u></span> <i>diminishes </i>in magnitude over time due to air resistance, friction, etc.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Happens easier in softer materials.</div><div><br></div><img src=""reson-damp01.jpg""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Sound attenuation</b></span> occurs when the sound wave's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;amplitude&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <i>diminishes </i>in magnitude over time due to air resistance, friction, etc.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Sound attenuation</b></span> occurs when the sound wave's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>amplitude</u></span> <i>diminishes </i>in magnitude over time due to air resistance, friction, etc.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Happens easier in softer materials.</div><div><br></div><img src=""reson-damp01.jpg""></div>"
"<div><b>Sound dampening</b> is <i>also</i> called <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""attenuation"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div><b>Sound dampening</b> is <i>also</i> called <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">attenuation</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Amplitude decreases in magnitude over time due to air resistance, friction, etc.</div><div>- Happens the most in&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""reson-damp01.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-09f322a7b669c981097f55ea6cf42cbb46f011ef.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div><b>Sound dampening</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[does/does not]</span> affect the <u>frequency/pitch</u> of the sound.</div>"	"<div><b>Sound dampening</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does not</i></span> affect the <u>frequency/pitch</u> of the sound.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <u>Amplitude</u> decreases in magnitude over time due to air resistance, friction, etc.</div><div><br></div><img src=""reson-damp01.jpg""></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"<div>On a standing wave, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>fundamental frequency</b></span> is the frequency associated with the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;first&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;harmonic&#x20;&#x28;n&#x3D;1&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>On a standing wave, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>fundamental frequency</b></span> is the frequency associated with the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>first </i>harmonic (n=1)</span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Harmonics (n)</b>&nbsp;represent ""half wavelengths"" (look at the image... (a) n = 1, (b) n = 2, (c) n = 3)...</div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= harmonic number (n=1, 2, 3, etc.);&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= velocity of the wave;&nbsp;<b>L</b>&nbsp;= length of the string.</div><div>- Frequency (f)&nbsp;<u>associated</u>&nbsp;with a&nbsp;<b>given harmonic (n)</b>&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;<b>length (L)</b>. You could use this below equation to calculate what the frequency would be of the sound generated at n=2 in the image.</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""21fa3c5d5b9e821e6db500240c3c7b82.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""b91790e68b290665cdbbea8f7cc9b782.png""><div></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Antinodes"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are at the ends of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b style="""">open</b></span> <b style="""">pipes </b>in standing waves."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Antinodes</span> are at the ends of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b style="""">open</b></span> <b style="""">pipes </b>in standing waves.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Notice how both ends finish off with an antinode as opposed to a node...</div><div><br></div><img src=""55497ec48aba1aa5c0b232960e41eeca.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Antinodes</span> are at the ends of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;open&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[open/closed]</span> <b style="""">pipes </b>in standing waves."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Antinodes</span> are at the ends of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b style="""">open</b></span> <b style="""">pipes </b>in standing waves.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Notice how both ends finish off with an antinode as opposed to a node...</div><div><br></div><img src=""55497ec48aba1aa5c0b232960e41eeca.png""></div>"
"The standing waves in <b>strings</b> &amp; <b>open pipes</b>&nbsp;are essentially the same, except <u>strings</u> end in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""nodes"" data-ordinal=""1"">[node/antinode]</span> &amp; <u>open pipes</u> end in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""antinodes"" data-ordinal=""1"">[node/antinode]</span>."	"The standing waves in <b>strings</b> &amp; <b>open pipes</b>&nbsp;are essentially the same, except <u>strings</u> end in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">nodes</span> &amp; <u>open pipes</u> end in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">antinodes</span>.

<div>- Harmonic (n) refers to the number of ""half wavelengths"" associated with the wave. Look at the image below for clarification.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= harmonic number (n=1, 2, 3, etc.);&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= velocity of the wave;&nbsp;<b>L</b>&nbsp;= length of the string.</div><div>- Frequency (f)&nbsp;<u>associated</u>&nbsp;with a&nbsp;<b>given harmonic (n)</b>&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;<b>length (L)</b>. "<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>Nodes</i></span> are at the ends of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>closed</b></span>&nbsp;<b>pipes&nbsp;</b>in standing waves.

"In closed pipes,&nbsp;<b>harmonics (n)</b>&nbsp;are the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;number&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;of&#x20;&quot;&lt;u&gt;quarter&#x20;wavelengths&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&quot;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;of the oscillating wave."	"In closed pipes,&nbsp;<b>harmonics (n)</b>&nbsp;are the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>number&nbsp;</i>of ""<u>quarter wavelengths</u>""</span>&nbsp;of the oscillating wave.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- Harmonic (n) refers to the number of ""quarter wavelengths"" associated with the wave. Look at the image below for clarification.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= harmonic number (n=1, 3, 5, etc.);&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= velocity of the wave;&nbsp;<b>L</b>&nbsp;= length of the string.</div><div>- Frequency (f)&nbsp;<u>associated</u>&nbsp;with a&nbsp;<b>given harmonic (n)</b>&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;<b>length (L)</b>. You could use this below equation to calculate what the frequency would be of the sound generated at n=3 in the image.</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""39ce5536ab9a0ceef7c4e97979a73545.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""961947c849639c439d6a8e7f58ec2fae.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""204a1cbbfc5c3d6d19e10f21860a7df8.png""><div><div></div></div><div><div></div></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Ultrasounds&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> work by generating sound waves, <i>bouncing it</i> off an object, and measuring how long it takes for that sound wave to return."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Ultrasounds</b></span> work by generating sound waves, <i>bouncing it</i> off an object, and measuring how long it takes for that sound wave to return.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Sound waves that return quickly tell you part of the object (ex: nose) is closer than other waves that take slightly longer to return.</div><div><br></div><img src=""7f50eb768c5a2d7d1fb46bb70f9b0f92.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Doppler&#x20;ultrasounds&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are used to determine the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>rate </i>of blood flow</u></span>&nbsp;based on the noise's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>frequency</i> shift</u></span> (doppler effect) as it moves closer/further away."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Doppler ultrasounds</b></span> are used to determine the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>rate </i>of blood flow</u></span>&nbsp;based on the noise's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>frequency</i> shift</u></span> (doppler effect) as it moves closer/further away.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""7f50eb768c5a2d7d1fb46bb70f9b0f92.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Doppler ultrasounds</b></span> are used to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;rate&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;of&#x20;blood&#x20;flow&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;based on the noise's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;frequency&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;shift&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> (doppler effect) as it moves closer/further away."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Doppler ultrasounds</b></span> are used to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>rate </i>of blood flow</u></span>&nbsp;based on the noise's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>frequency</i> shift</u></span> (doppler effect) as it moves closer/further away.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""7f50eb768c5a2d7d1fb46bb70f9b0f92.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Gauge&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> represents the <i>difference </i>between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>absolute pressure</u> (P<sub>0</sub> + ρgh)</span> experienced by a substance submerged in a liquid &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>atmospheric pressure</u>&nbsp;(P<sub>atm</sub>)</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Gauge pressure</b></span> represents the <i>difference </i>between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>absolute pressure</u> (P<sub>0</sub> + ρgh)</span> experienced by a substance submerged in a liquid &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>atmospheric pressure</u>&nbsp;(P<sub>atm</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""2f80ab39e757a1d30b6119d5acce7227.png""><div><br></div><div>- (P<sub>0</sub> + ρgh) represents the <b>absolute pressure</b> (the total amount of pressure experienced by something submerged in water).<br></div><div>- P<sub>0</sub>&nbsp;is the pressure exerted on the surface of the fluid (most of the time it's the same as atmospheric pressure (1 x 10<sup>5</sup>&nbsp;Pa).<br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Gauge pressure</b></span> represents the <i>difference </i>between the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;absolute&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;P&lt;sub&gt;0&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x2B;&#x20;ρgh&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> experienced by a substance submerged in a liquid &amp; the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;atmospheric&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x28;P&lt;sub&gt;atm&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Gauge pressure</b></span> represents the <i>difference </i>between the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>absolute pressure</u> (P<sub>0</sub> + ρgh)</span> experienced by a substance submerged in a liquid &amp; the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>atmospheric pressure</u>&nbsp;(P<sub>atm</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""2f80ab39e757a1d30b6119d5acce7227.png""><div><br></div><div>- (P<sub>0</sub> + ρgh) represents the <b>absolute pressure</b> (the total amount of pressure experienced by something submerged in water).<br></div><div>- P<sub>0</sub>&nbsp;is the pressure exerted on the surface of the fluid (most of the time it's the same as atmospheric pressure (1 x 10<sup>5</sup>&nbsp;Pa).<br></div></div>"
"<div><b>Gauge pressure</b> is<i> equal</i> to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ρgh"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>ambient pressure (P</u><sub style="""">0</sub><u>)</u></span> <i>equals</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>atmospheric pressure (P</u><sub style="""">atm</sub><u>)</u></span>.</div>"	"<div><b>Gauge pressure</b> is<i> equal</i> to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ρgh</span> when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>ambient pressure (P</u><sub style="""">0</sub><u>)</u></span> <i>equals</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>atmospheric pressure (P</u><sub style="""">atm</sub><u>)</u></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""2f80ab39e757a1d30b6119d5acce7227.png""><div><br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Gauge pressure</b>&nbsp;represents the difference between the absolute pressure (i.e. total pressure experienced by something in a fluid) versus the outside atmospheric pressure.<br></div><div>- P<sub>0</sub>&nbsp;is the pressure exerted on the surface of the fluid (most of the time it's the same as atmospheric pressure (1 x 10<sup>5</sup>&nbsp;Pa).</div></div>"
"<div><b>Gauge pressure</b> is<i> equal</i> to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ρgh</span> when <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;ambient&#x20;pressure&#x20;&#x28;P&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;sub&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;0&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;u&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <i>equals</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;atmospheric&#x20;pressure&#x20;&#x28;P&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;sub&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;atm&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;u&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div><b>Gauge pressure</b> is<i> equal</i> to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ρgh</span> when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>ambient pressure (P</u><sub style="""">0</sub><u>)</u></span> <i>equals</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>atmospheric pressure (P</u><sub style="""">atm</sub><u>)</u></span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""2f80ab39e757a1d30b6119d5acce7227.png""><div><br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Gauge pressure</b>&nbsp;represents the difference between the absolute pressure (i.e. total pressure experienced by something in a fluid) versus the outside atmospheric pressure.<br></div><div>- P<sub>0</sub>&nbsp;is the pressure exerted on the surface of the fluid (most of the time it's the same as atmospheric pressure (1 x 10<sup>5</sup>&nbsp;Pa).</div></div>"
"<b>Pascal's principle</b> <i>only </i>applies to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""incompressible"" data-ordinal=""1"">[compress./incompress.]</span> fluids."	"<b>Pascal's principle</b> <i>only </i>applies to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">incompressible</span> fluids.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Pascal's principle</b> says that changes in pressure will be dispersed evenly throughout the entire fluid.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d861da263dde5bd34b79de924455b279.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""361c4621de6414ed911ab61bb153e132.png""><div><br></div></div>"

<div><img src=""d861da263dde5bd34b79de924455b279.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""361c4621de6414ed911ab61bb153e132.png""><div><br></div></div>"
"In hydrostatics, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;volume&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> can be measured by <i>multiplying </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>cross-sectional area (A)</u></span> times the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>distance (d)</u></span> the fluid moved."	"In hydrostatics, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>volume</b></span> can be measured by <i>multiplying </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>cross-sectional area (A)</u></span> times the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>distance (d)</u></span> the fluid moved.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Pascal's principle</b>&nbsp;says that changes in pressure will be dispersed evenly throughout the entire fluid.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d861da263dde5bd34b79de924455b279.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""361c4621de6414ed911ab61bb153e132.png""><div><br></div></div>"
"In hydrostatics, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>volume</b></span> can be measured by <i>multiplying </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;cross&#x2D;sectional&#x20;area&#x20;&#x28;A&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> times the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;distance&#x20;&#x28;d&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> the fluid moved."	"In hydrostatics, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>volume</b></span> can be measured by <i>multiplying </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>cross-sectional area (A)</u></span> times the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>distance (d)</u></span> the fluid moved.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Pascal's principle</b>&nbsp;says that changes in pressure will be dispersed evenly throughout the entire fluid.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d861da263dde5bd34b79de924455b279.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""361c4621de6414ed911ab61bb153e132.png""><div><br></div></div>"
"<div>In a hydraulic system, an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""applied&#x20;pressure"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> to <i>one </i>side of the system must <i>equal </i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pressure&#x20;applied"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> to the <i>other</i> end of the system.</div>"	"<div>In a hydraulic system, an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">applied pressure</span> to <i>one </i>side of the system must <i>equal </i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">pressure applied</span> to the <i>other</i> end of the system.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Because the pressures need to be <u>equal to each other</u> on both sides, a larger force needs to be exerted since its cross-sectional area is also larger (P = F/A).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Pascal's principle</b>&nbsp;says that changes in pressure will be dispersed evenly throughout the entire fluid.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d861da263dde5bd34b79de924455b279.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""361c4621de6414ed911ab61bb153e132.png""><div><br></div></div>"
"<div><b>Hydrostatic equation</b> that relates the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;applied&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> on <i>one </i>side to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;resulting&#x20;applied&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> on the <i>other </i>side:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""d861da263dde5bd34b79de924455b279.png""></span><br></div>"	"<div><b>Hydrostatic equation</b> that relates the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>applied pressure</u></span> on <i>one </i>side to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>resulting applied pressure</u></span> on the <i>other </i>side:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""d861da263dde5bd34b79de924455b279.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Pressures need to be the exact same on both sides (<u>not</u> force).</div><div>- Because the pressures need to be <u>equal to each other</u> on both sides, a larger force needs to be exerted since its cross-sectional area is also larger (P = F/A).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Pascal's principle</b>&nbsp;says that changes in pressure will be dispersed evenly throughout the entire fluid.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d861da263dde5bd34b79de924455b279.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b0a9a972be5d473c73f5498cd20dab9be95088a0.jpg""><br></div>"
"<div><b>Hydrostatic equation</b> that relates the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>applied pressure</u></span> on <i>one </i>side to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>resulting applied pressure</u></span> on the <i>other </i>side:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;d861da263dde5bd34b79de924455b279&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div><b>Hydrostatic equation</b> that relates the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>applied pressure</u></span> on <i>one </i>side to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>resulting applied pressure</u></span> on the <i>other </i>side:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""d861da263dde5bd34b79de924455b279.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Pressures need to be the exact same on both sides (<u>not</u> force).</div><div>- Because the pressures need to be <u>equal to each other</u> on both sides, a larger force needs to be exerted since its cross-sectional area is also larger (P = F/A).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Pascal's principle</b>&nbsp;says that changes in pressure will be dispersed evenly throughout the entire fluid.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d861da263dde5bd34b79de924455b279.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b0a9a972be5d473c73f5498cd20dab9be95088a0.jpg""><br></div>"
"In <u>hydraulic systems</u>, the <b>volume displaced</b> from the inital applied pressure is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;the&#x20;same&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[the same/different]</span> as the volume displaced on the other end."	"In <u>hydraulic systems</u>, the <b>volume displaced</b> from the inital applied pressure is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>the same</i></span> as the volume displaced on the other end.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Pressures need to be the exact same on both sides.</div><div>- Because the pressures need to be <u>equal to each other</u> on both sides, a larger force needs to be exerted since its cross-sectional area is also larger (P = F/A).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Pascal's principle</b>&nbsp;says that changes in pressure will be dispersed evenly throughout the entire fluid.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""adb413e852b81f49dae032801b58408d.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""361c4621de6414ed911ab61bb153e132.png""></div>"
"<div><u>Hydrostatics equation</u> that shows how the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b><i>initial </i>volume displaced</b></span>&nbsp;is <i>equal </i>to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">volume displaced</span> on the <i>other</i> end:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;adb413e852b81f49dae032801b58408d&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div><u>Hydrostatics equation</u> that shows how the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b><i>initial </i>volume displaced</b></span>&nbsp;is <i>equal </i>to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">volume displaced</span> on the <i>other</i> end:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""adb413e852b81f49dae032801b58408d.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Pressures need to be the exact same on both sides.</div><div>- Because the pressures need to be <u>equal to each other</u> on both sides, a larger force needs to be exerted since its cross-sectional area is also larger (P = F/A).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Pascal's principle</b>&nbsp;says that changes in pressure will be dispersed evenly throughout the entire fluid.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""adb413e852b81f49dae032801b58408d.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""361c4621de6414ed911ab61bb153e132.png""></div>"
"<div><u>Hydrostatics equation</u> that shows how the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;&lt;i&gt;initial&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;volume&#x20;displaced&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is <i>equal </i>to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""volume&#x20;displaced"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> on the <i>other</i> end:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""adb413e852b81f49dae032801b58408d.png""></span><br></div>"	"<div><u>Hydrostatics equation</u> that shows how the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b><i>initial </i>volume displaced</b></span>&nbsp;is <i>equal </i>to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">volume displaced</span> on the <i>other</i> end:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""adb413e852b81f49dae032801b58408d.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Pressures need to be the exact same on both sides.</div><div>- Because the pressures need to be <u>equal to each other</u> on both sides, a larger force needs to be exerted since its cross-sectional area is also larger (P = F/A).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Pascal's principle</b>&nbsp;says that changes in pressure will be dispersed evenly throughout the entire fluid.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""adb413e852b81f49dae032801b58408d.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""361c4621de6414ed911ab61bb153e132.png""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">amount of <b>work</b> done</span> in a <u>hydrostatic system</u> when a pressure is exerted on one end:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;f927644cc68f83be4331c24ffe1d0441&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">amount of <b>work</b> done</span> in a <u>hydrostatic system</u> when a pressure is exerted on one end:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""f927644cc68f83be4331c24ffe1d0441.png""></span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Same as the regular work equation (W = Fdcosθ), except θ = 0 in hydrostatics.</div><div>- Pressures need to be the exact same on both sides.</div><div>- Because the pressures need to be <u>equal to each other</u> on both sides, a larger force needs to be exerted since its cross-sectional area is also larger (P = F/A).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Pascal's principle</b>&nbsp;says that changes in pressure will be dispersed evenly throughout the entire fluid.</div><div><br></div><img src=""361c4621de6414ed911ab61bb153e132.png""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""amount&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;b&gt;work&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x20;done"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in a <u>hydrostatic system</u> when a pressure is exerted on one end:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""f927644cc68f83be4331c24ffe1d0441.png""></span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">amount of <b>work</b> done</span> in a <u>hydrostatic system</u> when a pressure is exerted on one end:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""f927644cc68f83be4331c24ffe1d0441.png""></span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Same as the regular work equation (W = Fdcosθ), except θ = 0 in hydrostatics.</div><div>- Pressures need to be the exact same on both sides.</div><div>- Because the pressures need to be <u>equal to each other</u> on both sides, a larger force needs to be exerted since its cross-sectional area is also larger (P = F/A).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Pascal's principle</b>&nbsp;says that changes in pressure will be dispersed evenly throughout the entire fluid.</div><div><br></div><img src=""361c4621de6414ed911ab61bb153e132.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Archimede&#x27;s&#x20;principle&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> says that the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>buoyant force (F</u><sub style="""">buoy</sub><u>)</u></span> exerted on an object in a liquid is <i>equal </i>to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>weight of the displaced liquid (mg)</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Archimede's principle</b></span> says that the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>buoyant force (F</u><sub style="""">buoy</sub><u>)</u></span> exerted on an object in a liquid is <i>equal </i>to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>weight of the displaced liquid (mg)</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Weight of the displaced liquid can be calculated as&nbsp;<b>ρvg</b>, since (ρv) is equal to mass and g is the gravitational constant (same as F = mg).</div><div><br></div><img src=""9d4102481b06b1915961d88f256893ab.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-245e972dc9644350291cb04f80409d7c6b2d18e8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Archimede's principle</b></span> says that the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;buoyant&#x20;force&#x20;&#x28;F&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;sub&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;buoy&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;u&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> exerted on an object in a liquid is <i>equal </i>to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;weight&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;displaced&#x20;liquid&#x20;&#x28;mg&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Archimede's principle</b></span> says that the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>buoyant force (F</u><sub style="""">buoy</sub><u>)</u></span> exerted on an object in a liquid is <i>equal </i>to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>weight of the displaced liquid (mg)</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Weight of the displaced liquid can be calculated as&nbsp;<b>ρvg</b>, since (ρv) is equal to mass and g is the gravitational constant (same as F = mg).</div><div><br></div><img src=""9d4102481b06b1915961d88f256893ab.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-245e972dc9644350291cb04f80409d7c6b2d18e8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>When an object is <u>floating</u> in water at its <i>equilibrium</i> point, its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""buoyant&#x20;force&#x20;&#x28;F&lt;sub&gt;buoyant&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> = its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""weight&#x20;&#x28;mg&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - <i>regardless</i> of the depth of the object.</div>"	"<div>When an object is <u>floating</u> in water at its <i>equilibrium</i> point, its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">buoyant force (F<sub>buoyant</sub>)</span> = its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">weight (mg)</span> - <i>regardless</i> of the depth of the object.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Force exerted by the weight of the object downards is equal to the buoyant force exerted upwards.</div><div>- Weight of the displaced liquid can be calculated as&nbsp;<b>ρvg</b>, since (ρv) is equal to mass and g is the gravitational constant (same as F = mg).</div><div><br></div><img src=""9d4102481b06b1915961d88f256893ab.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-51b4a6717359aeccb89e89bb010145cea85d3157.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"To determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;percent&#x20;of&#x20;an&#x20;object&lt;i&gt;&#x20;submerged&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> in a fluid, <u>divide</u><i> </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">(object's density)</span> / <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">(fluid's density)</span>."	"To determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>percent of an object<i> submerged</i></b></span> in a fluid, <u>divide</u><i> </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">(object's density)</span> / <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">(fluid's density)</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Critical speed</b>&nbsp;depends on the physical properties of the liquid (viscosity/diameter).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>v<sub>c</sub></b>&nbsp;= critical speed;&nbsp;<b>N<sub>R</sub></b>&nbsp;= Reynolds number;&nbsp;<b>η</b>&nbsp;= viscosity;&nbsp;<b>ρ</b>&nbsp;= fluid's density;&nbsp;<b>D</b>&nbsp;= tube diameter.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c822006aad85af2a36588f3fc5cde6fd.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""6619d3bec2252163ab90984fb251c3ee.png""></div>"
"Laminar flow <i>transitions </i>into <b>turbulent flow</b> when the velocity of fluid molecules <i>exceed </i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""critical&#x20;speed&#x20;&#x28;v&lt;sub&gt;c&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"Laminar flow <i>transitions </i>into <b>turbulent flow</b> when the velocity of fluid molecules <i>exceed </i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">critical speed (v<sub>c</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Critical speed</b> depends on the physical properties of the liquid (viscosity/diameter).</div><div>- <b>v<sub>c</sub></b> = critical speed; <b>N<sub>R</sub></b> = Reynolds number; <b>η</b> = viscosity; <b>ρ</b> = fluid's density; <b>D</b> = tube diameter.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c822006aad85af2a36588f3fc5cde6fd.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""6619d3bec2252163ab90984fb251c3ee.png""></div>"
"In turbulent flow, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;boundary&#x20;layer&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is the <i>only</i> place where <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>laminar flow</u></span> still takes place."	"In turbulent flow, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>boundary layer</b></span>&nbsp;is the <i>only</i> place where <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>laminar flow</u></span> still takes place.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Critical speed</b> depends on the physical properties of the liquid (viscosity/diameter).</div><div>- <b>v<sub>c</sub></b> = critical speed; <b>N<sub>R</sub></b> = Reynolds number; <b>η</b> = viscosity; <b>ρ</b> = fluid's density; <b>D</b> = tube diameter.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c822006aad85af2a36588f3fc5cde6fd.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""velocity-profiles.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""6619d3bec2252163ab90984fb251c3ee.png""></div>"
"In turbulent flow, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>boundary layer</b></span>&nbsp;is the <i>only</i> place where <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;laminar&#x20;flow&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> still takes place." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>As the tube widens, the <b>streamlines </b>space further apart.</div><div><br></div><img src=""fc70e0e1c8a95053aea91eb9547e4335.png""></div>"
"As a fluid travels through two ends of a tube with <i>unequal </i>widths, the fluid's <b>flow rate (Q)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[does/does not]</span> change."	"As a fluid travels through two ends of a tube with <i>unequal </i>widths, the fluid's <b>flow rate (Q)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does not</i></span> change.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Flow rate</b> is the volume of fluid that flows per unit of time (volume/s).</div><div>- Flow rate (Q) is equal to the <b>linear speed</b> multiplied by the tube's <b>cross-sectional area</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""fc70e0e1c8a95053aea91eb9547e4335.png""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;flow&#x20;rate&#x20;&#x28;Q&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of a fluid in a tube represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>volume </i>of liquid</u></span> passing through a point&nbsp;<i>per</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>unit of time</u></span>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>flow rate (Q)</b></span> of a fluid in a tube represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>volume </i>of liquid</u></span> passing through a point&nbsp;<i>per</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>unit of time</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Volume/time</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Flow rate </b>always remains <u>constant</u> in a tube (linear speed might change, however).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""fc70e0e1c8a95053aea91eb9547e4335.png""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>flow rate (Q)</b></span> of a fluid in a tube represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;volume&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;of&#x20;liquid&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> passing through a point&nbsp;<i>per</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;unit&#x20;of&#x20;time&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>flow rate (Q)</b></span> of a fluid in a tube represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>volume </i>of liquid</u></span> passing through a point&nbsp;<i>per</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>unit of time</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Volume/time</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Flow rate </b>always remains <u>constant</u> in a tube (linear speed might change, however).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""fc70e0e1c8a95053aea91eb9547e4335.png""></div>"
"<div>Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;flow&#x20;rate&#x20;&#x28;Q&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of a fluid in a tube:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""></span><br>"	"<div>Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>flow rate (Q)</b></span> of a fluid in a tube:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Called the <b>continuity equation</b>.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Q</b>&nbsp;= flow rate;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= linear speed of a fluid molecule;&nbsp;<b>A</b>&nbsp;= cross-sectional area.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Flow rate&nbsp;</b>always remains&nbsp;<u>constant</u>&nbsp;in a tube (volume/time), but linear speed (v) might change.</div><div>- Since flow rate is always constant, v and A work inversely proportional to each other to ensure Q is always the same.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""fc70e0e1c8a95053aea91eb9547e4335.png""></div>"
"<div>Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>flow rate (Q)</b></span> of a fluid in a tube:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>flow rate (Q)</b></span> of a fluid in a tube:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Called the <b>continuity equation</b>.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Q</b>&nbsp;= flow rate;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= linear speed of a fluid molecule;&nbsp;<b>A</b>&nbsp;= cross-sectional area.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Flow rate&nbsp;</b>always remains&nbsp;<u>constant</u>&nbsp;in a tube (volume/time), but linear speed (v) might change.</div><div>- Since flow rate is always constant, v and A work inversely proportional to each other to ensure Q is always the same.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""fc70e0e1c8a95053aea91eb9547e4335.png""></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Q</b>&nbsp;= flow rate;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= linear speed of a fluid molecule;&nbsp;<b>A</b>&nbsp;= cross-sectional area.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Flow rate&nbsp;</b>always remains&nbsp;<u>constant</u>&nbsp;in a tube (volume/time), but linear speed (v) might change.</div><div>- Since flow rate is always constant, v and A work inversely proportional to each other to ensure Q is always the same.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""fc70e0e1c8a95053aea91eb9547e4335.png""></div>"
"<div>In the equation for the <u>flow rate (Q)</u> of a fluid through a tube, <b>A</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;tube&#x27;s&#x20;cross&#x2D;sectional&#x20;area&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""><br>"	"<div>In the equation for the <u>flow rate (Q)</u> of a fluid through a tube, <b>A</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>tube's cross-sectional area</u></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Q</b>&nbsp;= flow rate;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= linear speed of a fluid molecule;&nbsp;<b>A</b>&nbsp;= cross-sectional area.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Flow rate&nbsp;</b>always remains&nbsp;<u>constant</u>&nbsp;in a tube (volume/time), but linear speed (v) might change.</div><div>- Since flow rate is always constant, v and A work inversely proportional to each other to ensure Q is always the same.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ccbef1455067ec640ae1fdfba2333a31.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""fc70e0e1c8a95053aea91eb9547e4335.png""></div>"
"Fluids flow through <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""narrow"" data-ordinal=""1"">[narrow/wide]</span> tubes at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <u>speeds</u> in order to compensate for the reduced cross-sectional area."	"Fluids flow through <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">narrow</span> tubes at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <u>speeds</u> in order to compensate for the reduced cross-sectional area.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""e40c6b1d97c08f843fb7051295533c03.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3153ecf0144e3d1c62b3057678241192.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In Bernoulli's equation for the conservation of energy in a fluid, <b>P</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pressure&#x20;energy"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c819af59bec0a807500f656bbb10f319.png""><br>"	"<div>In Bernoulli's equation for the conservation of energy in a fluid, <b>P</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">pressure energy</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c819af59bec0a807500f656bbb10f319.png""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""e40c6b1d97c08f843fb7051295533c03.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3153ecf0144e3d1c62b3057678241192.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In <u>Bernoulli's equation</u> for the conservation of <i>energy </i>in a fluid, <b>ρgh</b>&nbsp;represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""fluid&#x27;s&#x20;potential&#x20;energy"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c819af59bec0a807500f656bbb10f319.png""><br>"	"<div>In <u>Bernoulli's equation</u> for the conservation of <i>energy </i>in a fluid, <b>ρgh</b>&nbsp;represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">fluid's potential energy</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c819af59bec0a807500f656bbb10f319.png""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>P</b> = pressure energy; <b>ρgh</b> = potential energy (using fluid's density and height of the tube); <b>1/2ρv<sup>2</sup></b> = kinetic energy (using density).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e40c6b1d97c08f843fb7051295533c03.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3153ecf0144e3d1c62b3057678241192.png""><br></div><div><br></div></div>"
"<div>In <u>Bernoulli's equation</u> for the conservation of <i>energy </i>in a fluid, <b>1/2ρv<sup>2</sup></b>&nbsp;represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""fluid&#x27;s&#x20;&lt;u&gt;dynamic&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;kinetic&#x20;energy&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c819af59bec0a807500f656bbb10f319.png""><br>"	"<div>In <u>Bernoulli's equation</u> for the conservation of <i>energy </i>in a fluid, <b>1/2ρv<sup>2</sup></b>&nbsp;represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">fluid's <u>dynamic pressure</u> (kinetic energy)</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c819af59bec0a807500f656bbb10f319.png""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>P</b> = pressure energy; <b>ρgh</b> = potential energy (using fluid's density and height of the tube); <b>1/2ρv<sup>2</sup></b> = kinetic energy (using density).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e40c6b1d97c08f843fb7051295533c03.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3153ecf0144e3d1c62b3057678241192.png""><br></div><div><br></div></div>"
"<div>In&nbsp;<u>Bernoulli's equation</u>&nbsp;for the conservation of&nbsp;<i>energy&nbsp;</i>in a fluid,<b>&nbsp;P +&nbsp;ρgh</b>&nbsp;represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""fluid&#x27;s&amp;nbsp&#x3B;static&#x20;pressure"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c819af59bec0a807500f656bbb10f319.png"">"	"<div>In&nbsp;<u>Bernoulli's equation</u>&nbsp;for the conservation of&nbsp;<i>energy&nbsp;</i>in a fluid,<b>&nbsp;P +&nbsp;ρgh</b>&nbsp;represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">fluid's&nbsp;static pressure</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c819af59bec0a807500f656bbb10f319.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>P</b> = pressure energy; <b>ρgh</b> = potential energy (using fluid's density and height of the tube); <b>1/2ρv<sup>2</sup></b> = kinetic energy (using density).</div><div>- <b>Static pressure</b> is the same as <u>absolute pressure</u> (P<sub>0</sub> + ρgh).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e40c6b1d97c08f843fb7051295533c03.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3153ecf0144e3d1c62b3057678241192.png""><br></div><div><br></div></div>"
"According to <b>Bernoulli's equation</b>, the <i>sum </i>of a fluid's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""static&#x20;pressure&#x20;&#x28;P&#x20;&#x2B;&#x20;ρgh&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> and <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""dynamic&#x20;pressure&#x20;&#x28;1&#x2F;2ρv&lt;sup&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> will <i>always&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">remain <u>constant</u></span>&nbsp;in a tube."	"According to <b>Bernoulli's equation</b>, the <i>sum </i>of a fluid's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">static pressure (P + ρgh)</span> and <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">dynamic pressure (1/2ρv<sup>2</sup>)</span> will <i>always&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">remain <u>constant</u></span>&nbsp;in a tube.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Bernoulli's equation describes energy conservation within a fluid.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>P</b> = pressure energy; <b>ρgh</b> = potential energy (using fluid's density and height of the tube); <b>1/2ρv<sup>2</sup></b> = kinetic energy (using density).</div><div>- <b>Static pressure</b> is the same as <u>absolute pressure</u> (P<sub>0</sub> + ρgh).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e40c6b1d97c08f843fb7051295533c03.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3153ecf0144e3d1c62b3057678241192.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><br></div></div>"
"According to <b>Bernoulli's equation</b>, the <i>sum </i>of a fluid's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">static pressure (P + ρgh)</span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">dynamic pressure (1/2ρv<sup>2</sup>)</span> will <i>always&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""remain&#x20;&lt;u&gt;constant&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;in a tube."	"According to <b>Bernoulli's equation</b>, the <i>sum </i>of a fluid's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">static pressure (P + ρgh)</span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">dynamic pressure (1/2ρv<sup>2</sup>)</span> will <i>always&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">remain <u>constant</u></span>&nbsp;in a tube.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Bernoulli's equation describes energy conservation within a fluid.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>P</b> = pressure energy; <b>ρgh</b> = potential energy (using fluid's density and height of the tube); <b>1/2ρv<sup>2</sup></b> = kinetic energy (using density).</div><div>- <b>Static pressure</b> is the same as <u>absolute pressure</u> (P<sub>0</sub> + ρgh).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e40c6b1d97c08f843fb7051295533c03.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3153ecf0144e3d1c62b3057678241192.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><br></div></div>"
"<i>Individual</i> blood vessels <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;furthest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[closest/furthest]</span> away from the heart have the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>greatest</i></span> amount of <u>vascular resistance</u>."	"<i>Individual</i> blood vessels <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>furthest</i></span> away from the heart have the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>greatest</i></span> amount of <u>vascular resistance</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

They break up into multiple <u>smaller vessels</u> (like capillaries) in parallel though, so <b>total resistance&nbsp;(R<sub>eq</sub>)</b>&nbsp;decreases (1/R<sub>eq</sub> = 1/R<sub>1</sub> + 1/R<sub>2</sub> + ...).</div>"
"<i>Individual</i> blood vessels <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>furthest</i></span> away from the heart have the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;greatest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[lowest/greatest]</span> amount of <u>vascular resistance</u>."	"<i>Individual</i> blood vessels <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>furthest</i></span> away from the heart have the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>greatest</i></span> amount of <u>vascular resistance</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

They break up into multiple <u>smaller vessels</u> (like capillaries) in parallel though, so <b>total resistance&nbsp;(R<sub>eq</sub>)</b>&nbsp;decreases (1/R<sub>eq</sub> = 1/R<sub>1</sub> + 1/R<sub>2</sub> + ...).</div>"
"<div>Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>positioning</i> of the focal point, image, and object</span>&nbsp;in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">converging lens</span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;7f5bff09da3aa1fb015eecda60a98e7d4248aa5b&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>positioning</i> of the focal point, image, and object</span>&nbsp;in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">converging lens</span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-7f5bff09da3aa1fb015eecda60a98e7d4248aa5b.jpg""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>f</b> = focal length (distance between focal point &amp; mirror); <b>o</b> = object (thing you're looking at); <b>i</b> = image (what you're actually seeing).<div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg</a><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-34b940337312e49d940a8d5feb45f14db06db9d0.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"<div>In the lens equation, <b>o</b> represents the <i>distance</i> between the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""lens"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> and the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""object"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7f5bff09da3aa1fb015eecda60a98e7d4248aa5b.jpg""><br>"	"<div>In the lens equation, <b>o</b> represents the <i>distance</i> between the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">lens</span> and the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">object</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7f5bff09da3aa1fb015eecda60a98e7d4248aa5b.jpg""><br>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>f</b> = focal length (distance between focal point &amp; mirror); <b>o</b> = object (thing you're looking at); <b>i</b> = image (what you're actually seeing).<div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg</a><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-34b940337312e49d940a8d5feb45f14db06db9d0.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>f</b> = focal length (distance between focal point &amp; mirror); <b>o</b> = object (thing you're looking at); <b>i</b> = image (what you're actually seeing).<div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg</a><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-34b940337312e49d940a8d5feb45f14db06db9d0.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"<div>In the lens equation, <b>f</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;focal&#x20;length&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;distance&#x20;from&#x20;lens&#x20;→&#x20;focal&#x20;point&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7f5bff09da3aa1fb015eecda60a98e7d4248aa5b.jpg""><br>"	"<div>In the lens equation, <b>f</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>focal length</u> (distance from lens → focal point)</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7f5bff09da3aa1fb015eecda60a98e7d4248aa5b.jpg""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>f</b> = focal length (distance between focal point &amp; mirror); <b>o</b> = object (thing you're looking at); <b>i</b> = image (what you're actually seeing).<div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg</a><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-34b940337312e49d940a8d5feb45f14db06db9d0.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"<div>In geometric optics, <u>convex lenses</u> &amp; <u>concave mirrors</u> have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> <b>focal lengths (f)</b>.<br></div>"	"<div>In geometric optics, <u>convex lenses</u> &amp; <u>concave mirrors</u> have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>positive</i></span> <b>focal lengths (f)</b>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Convex lenses &amp; concave mirrors are both considered converging systems.</div><div>- Focal point (spot where they light rays meet up or diverge apart from) will always be <u>away</u> from the convex lens.</div>- <b>f</b> = focal length (distance between focal point &amp; mirror); <b>o</b> = object (thing you're looking at); <b>i</b> = image (what you're actually seeing).<div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg</a><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-34b940337312e49d940a8d5feb45f14db06db9d0.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"<div>In geometric optics,&nbsp;<u>concave lenses</u>&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;<u>convex mirrors</u>&nbsp;have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;negative&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span>&nbsp;<b>focal lengths (f)</b>.<br></div>"	"<div>In geometric optics,&nbsp;<u>concave lenses</u>&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;<u>convex mirrors</u>&nbsp;have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>negative</i></span>&nbsp;<b>focal lengths (f)</b>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Concave lenses &amp; convex mirrors are both <b>diverging systems</b>.</div><div>- Symbolizes that light rays <u>converge</u> together at some point.</div>- <b>f</b> = focal length (distance between focal point &amp; mirror); <b>o</b> = object (thing you're looking at); <b>i</b> = image (what you're actually seeing).<div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg</a><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-34b940337312e49d940a8d5feb45f14db06db9d0.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"Using the lens equation, <b>o</b> is <i>always</i> a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> value."	"Using the lens equation, <b>o</b> is <i>always</i> a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>positive</i></span> value.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Chosen arbitrarily. Makes more sense to designate the object's distance as positive and then work around the image relative to the object.</div>- <b>f</b> = focal length (distance between focal point &amp; mirror); <b>o</b> = object (thing you're looking at); <b>i</b> = image (what you're actually seeing).<div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg</a><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-34b940337312e49d940a8d5feb45f14db06db9d0.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"Using the lens equation, <b>i</b>&nbsp;is always a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> value when it's on the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite</i></span> side of the <u>object</u> from the lens."	"Using the lens equation, <b>i</b>&nbsp;is always a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>positive</i></span> value when it's on the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite</i></span> side of the <u>object</u> from the lens.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>f</b> = focal length (distance between focal point &amp; mirror); <b>o</b> = object (thing you're looking at); <b>i</b> = image (what you're actually seeing).<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg</a><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-34b940337312e49d940a8d5feb45f14db06db9d0.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"Using the lens equation, <b>i</b>&nbsp;is always a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>positive</i></span> value when it's on the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;opposite&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[same/opposite]</span> side of the <u>object</u> from the lens."	"Using the lens equation, <b>i</b>&nbsp;is always a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>positive</i></span> value when it's on the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite</i></span> side of the <u>object</u> from the lens.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>r</b> represents the radius of the lens' curvature (i.e. the radius of the lens itself).</div><div>- <b>f</b> = focal length (distance between focal point &amp; mirror); <b>o</b> = object (thing you're looking at); <b>i</b> = image (what you're actually seeing).<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg</a><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-1e54f2f0d29538ba74aa3001ec2e0c3943ff750a.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"According to the lens equation, a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> <u>image distance (i)</u> represents a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>real</b></span> <b>image</b>."	"According to the lens equation, a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>positive</i></span> <u>image distance (i)</u> represents a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>real</b></span> <b>image</b>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because a positive i means it's on the <u>opposite</u> side of the object (<u>in front</u> of the mirror).<br></div><div>- Think of wearing a contact lens. When the image is behind the lens (opposite side from o), it's real. <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>i</b> = distance from the lens to the image; <b>o</b> = distance from the lens to the object.</div><div>- When m is <u>negative</u>, the image is <b>inverted</b>&nbsp;(indicates a +i).</div><div>- When m is <u>positive</u>, the image is <b>upright&nbsp;</b>(indicates a -i).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7f5bff09da3aa1fb015eecda60a98e7d4248aa5b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-34b940337312e49d940a8d5feb45f14db06db9d0.jpg""></div>"

"Mneumonic for images in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>converging</b></span> systems in geometric optics:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""RIS&#x2D;E&#x20;yo&#x20;RIMs&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;RIDe&#x20;dat&#x20;oVUM"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Mneumonic for images in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>converging</b></span> systems in geometric optics:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">RIS-E yo RIMs &amp; RIDe dat oVUM</span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Object is <u>past</u> C → <b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.<b>S</b>.</div><div>Object is <u>at</u> C → <b>E</b>.<b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.</div><div>Object is <u>between</u> C &amp; F → <b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.<b>M</b>. (magnified as in large).</div><div>Object is <u>at</u> F → <b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.<b>D</b>. (disappears as in you can't see it).</div><div>Object is between F &amp; the lens → <b>V</b>.<b>U</b>.<b>M</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-4ba2eefa622c699849a710976aeed487b3e30f0e.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Mneumonic for images in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;converging&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[converging/diverging]</span> systems in geometric optics:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">RIS-E yo RIMs &amp; RIDe dat oVUM</span></div>"	"Mneumonic for images in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>converging</b></span> systems in geometric optics:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">RIS-E yo RIMs &amp; RIDe dat oVUM</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Object is <u>past</u> C → <b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.<b>S</b>.</div><div>Object is <u>at</u> C → <b>E</b>.<b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.</div><div>Object is <u>between</u> C &amp; F → <b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.<b>M</b>. (magnified as in large).</div><div>Object is <u>at</u> F → <b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.<b>D</b>. (disappears as in you can't see it).</div><div>Object is between F &amp; the lens → <b>V</b>.<b>U</b>.<b>M</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-4ba2eefa622c699849a710976aeed487b3e30f0e.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In converging systems, the point <b>F</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""focal&#x20;point"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, &amp; point <b>C</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""len&#x27;s&#x20;curvature&#x20;center"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In converging systems, the point <b>F</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">focal point</span>, &amp; point <b>C</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">len's curvature center</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>To visualize C, pretend like light rays from the top &amp; bottom of the lens are converging together. In reality, no light strikes this point.</div><div><br></div><div>Object is <u>past</u> C → <b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.<b>S</b>.</div><div>Object is <u>at</u> C → <b>E</b>.<b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.</div><div>Object is <u>between</u> C &amp; F → <b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.<b>M</b>. (magnified as in large).</div><div>Object is <u>at</u> F → <b>R</b>.<b>I</b>.<b>D</b>. (disappears as in you can't see it).</div><div>Object is between F &amp; the lens → <b>V</b>.<b>U</b>.<b>M</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-53c80f04c7772d2d58404216e6501ef5e899a959.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-4ba2eefa622c699849a710976aeed487b3e30f0e.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In <b>converging</b> systems, images located <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;past&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;point&#x20;C"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>real</u></span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>inverted</u></span>, and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>small</u></span>."	"In <b>converging</b> systems, images located <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>past</i> point C</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>real</u></span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>inverted</u></span>, and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>small</u></span>.

"Light <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;refraction&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reflection/refraction]</span> is when light passes from one medium to another - <i>bending</i> itself and <i>slowing</i> down."	"Light <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>refraction</b></span> is when light passes from one medium to another - <i>bending</i> itself and <i>slowing</i> down.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Snell's law (below) describes this.</div><div>- <b>n</b> = refractive index; <b>c</b> = 3 x 10<sup>8</sup>; <b>v</b> = speed of light passing through the medium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b6d3ad51a2ff0c2e12f309e0135ffd51cc5ff70d.jpg""><br></div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;refractive&#x20;index&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;of&#x20;light"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<i>passing through</i> a medium:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""paste-3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f.jpg""></span></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>refractive index </b>of light</span>&nbsp;<i>passing through</i> a medium:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""paste-3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f.jpg""></span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= refractive index;&nbsp;<b>c</b>&nbsp;= 3 x 10<sup>8</sup>;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= speed of light passing through the medium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c71800da0671c109f24fbb0eb24a457b3b836d08.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b1cfc6fbc9b9cd601c46901020761ffdfce3d404.jpg""></div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>refractive index </b>of light</span>&nbsp;<i>passing through</i> a medium:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>refractive index </b>of light</span>&nbsp;<i>passing through</i> a medium:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""paste-3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f.jpg""></span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= refractive index;&nbsp;<b>c</b>&nbsp;= 3 x 10<sup>8</sup>;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= speed of light passing through the medium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c71800da0671c109f24fbb0eb24a457b3b836d08.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b1cfc6fbc9b9cd601c46901020761ffdfce3d404.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In the equation for the <u>refractive index</u> of light <i>passing through</i> a medium, <b>c</b> represents <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""3&#x20;x&#x20;10&lt;sup&gt;8&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;&#x28;speed&#x20;of&#x20;light&#x20;in&#x20;a&#x20;vacuum&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f.jpg""><br>"	"<div>In the equation for the <u>refractive index</u> of light <i>passing through</i> a medium, <b>c</b> represents <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">3 x 10<sup>8</sup> (speed of light in a vacuum)</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f.jpg""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= refractive index;&nbsp;<b>c</b>&nbsp;= 3 x 10<sup>8</sup>;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= speed of light passing through the medium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c71800da0671c109f24fbb0eb24a457b3b836d08.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b1cfc6fbc9b9cd601c46901020761ffdfce3d404.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In the equation for the <u>refractive index</u> of light <i>passing through</i> a medium, <b>v</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""light&#x27;s&#x20;velocity&#x20;in&#x20;the&#x20;medium"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f.jpg""><br>"	"<div>In the equation for the <u>refractive index</u> of light <i>passing through</i> a medium, <b>v</b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">light's velocity in the medium</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f.jpg""><br>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= refractive index;&nbsp;<b>c</b>&nbsp;= 3 x 10<sup>8</sup>;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= speed of light passing through the medium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c71800da0671c109f24fbb0eb24a457b3b836d08.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b1cfc6fbc9b9cd601c46901020761ffdfce3d404.jpg""></div>"
"The <b>refractive index (n)</b> for light passing through a <u>vacuum</u> is <i>always</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span>."	"The <b>refractive index (n)</b> for light passing through a <u>vacuum</u> is <i>always</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- 1 because the refractive index equation is a ratio of light in a vacuum to light in a medium. Vacuum/vacuum = 1.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= refractive index;&nbsp;<b>c</b>&nbsp;= 3 x 10<sup>8</sup>;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= speed of light passing through the medium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c71800da0671c109f24fbb0eb24a457b3b836d08.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b1cfc6fbc9b9cd601c46901020761ffdfce3d404.jpg""></div>"
"The <b>refractive index (n)</b> for light passing through some <u>medium</u> is <i>always</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""greater"" data-ordinal=""1"">[less/greater]</span> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span>."	"The <b>refractive index (n)</b> for light passing through some <u>medium</u> is <i>always</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">greater</span> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Greater than 1 because dividing a large number (light in a vacuum) by a smaller number (light in a medium) yields a number greater than 1.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= refractive index;&nbsp;<b>c</b>&nbsp;= 3 x 10<sup>8</sup>;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= speed of light passing through the medium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c71800da0671c109f24fbb0eb24a457b3b836d08.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b1cfc6fbc9b9cd601c46901020761ffdfce3d404.jpg""></div>"
