<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-72de92a9b69b14ca173828b54567fbcabc5db4e5.jpg""></div>"
"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dielectric material</b></span> is placed between the plates of a capacitor <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;within&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;a"" data-ordinal=""4"">[within/isolated]</span>&nbsp;circuit, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>charge (Q)</u></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span> while voltage remains constant (emf source)."	"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dielectric material</b></span> is placed between the plates of a capacitor <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>within</i>&nbsp;a</span>&nbsp;circuit, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>charge (Q)</u></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span> while voltage remains constant (emf source).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is how dielectric materials increase the capacitance of capacitors in circuits.</div><div>- Adding dielectric materials increase capacitance, because they act as insulators and can help ""hold"" onto the charge.</div><div>- <b>C'</b> = new capacitance after dielectric is added;&nbsp;<span style=""font-weight: 700;"">κ</span>&nbsp;= dielectric constant; <b>C</b> = original capacitance.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-830f5153c7cf413cad4f8afa64379fe1a4ec7cd8.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-b53426a16f24caf062ca39522b78c347442df4ed.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dielectric material</b></span> is placed between the plates of a capacitor <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;isolated&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;from"" data-ordinal=""5"">[within/isolated]</span>&nbsp;a circuit, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>voltage (V)</u></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span> because it <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">shields the charges from each other</span>."	"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dielectric material</b></span> is placed between the plates of a capacitor <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""5""><i>isolated</i> from</span>&nbsp;a circuit, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>voltage (V)</u></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span> because it <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">shields the charges from each other</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- According to C = Q/V, this results in an increased capacitance (C).</div><div>- Adding dielectric materials increase capacitance, because they act as insulators and can help ""hold"" onto the charge.</div><div>- <b>C'</b> = new capacitance after dielectric is added;&nbsp;<span style=""font-weight: 700;"">κ</span>&nbsp;= dielectric constant; <b>C</b> = original capacitance.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-830f5153c7cf413cad4f8afa64379fe1a4ec7cd8.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""cfd784030804de2f5cb8f05f6a9de34c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;electric&#x20;field&#x20;&#x28;E&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <i>generated</i> by a source charge:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">E = (kQ)/r<sup>2</sup></span>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>electric field (E)</b></span> <i>generated</i> by a source charge:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">E = (kQ)/r<sup>2</sup></span>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>E</b> = electric field; <b>k</b> = 9 x 10<sup>9</sup>; <b>Q</b> = charge of the source charge; <b>r</b> = distance away from the source chargel</div>- Both equations mean the same thing, except the first equation requires the presence of a test charge and the second equation doesn't.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-43064db87ca3f7eeefbba493e1d484166de893ee.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>electric field (E)</b></span> <i>generated</i> by a source charge:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""E&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;&#x28;kQ&#x29;&#x2F;r&lt;sup&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>electric field (E)</b></span> <i>generated</i> by a source charge:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">E = (kQ)/r<sup>2</sup></span>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>E</b> = electric field; <b>k</b> = 9 x 10<sup>9</sup>; <b>Q</b> = charge of the source charge; <b>r</b> = distance away from the source chargel</div>- Both equations mean the same thing, except the first equation requires the presence of a test charge and the second equation doesn't.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-43064db87ca3f7eeefbba493e1d484166de893ee.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<i>All </i><u>charges</u>&nbsp;(moving or stationary) generate their <i>own</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""electric&#x20;fields"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, even if they're isolated from other charges."	"<i>All </i><u>charges</u>&nbsp;(moving or stationary) generate their <i>own</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">electric fields</span>, even if they're isolated from other charges.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>kQ/r<sup>2</sup>&nbsp;is the equation used to determine the strength of the electric field generated by one single source charge.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-43064db87ca3f7eeefbba493e1d484166de893ee.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-2eb14e16be68ff6186f597e52e013a43435037ab.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"To find the <b>electric potential (V)</b>&nbsp;due to <u>multiple charges</u> at a point, <i>add up</i> the electric potentials (V) from <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;each&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;nearby&#x20;charge"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> acting on that location."	"To find the <b>electric potential (V)</b>&nbsp;due to <u>multiple charges</u> at a point, <i>add up</i> the electric potentials (V) from <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>each</i> nearby charge</span> acting on that location.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Electric potential (V)</b> is a <u>scalar</u> quantity.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""53fc337aff0328f5acaf77d591a4b793.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;electric&#x20;potential&#x20;&lt;i&gt;difference&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&#x28;ΔV&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> between points A &amp; B...</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-87a7e4ecd9fc1c458bd34042219a781658f529a3.jpg""></span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>electric potential <i>difference</i> (ΔV)</b></span> between points A &amp; B...</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-87a7e4ecd9fc1c458bd34042219a781658f529a3.jpg""></span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Equal to the amount of work needed to move a charge from point A → B.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""What-is-Voltage.png""><br></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;electric&#x20;potential&#x20;difference&#x20;&#x28;ΔV&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> between points A &amp; B is defined as the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>amount of work</u></span> needed to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>move</i> a charge</u> from A → B</span> through the electric field."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>electric potential difference (ΔV)</b></span> between points A &amp; B is defined as the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>amount of work</u></span> needed to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>move</i> a charge</u> from A → B</span> through the electric field.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Can also be written as ΔPE/q (ΔPE = PE<sub>B</sub> - PE<sub>A</sub>).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-83013adc0a8fe04788ec3982b3a365598b36d286.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>electric potential difference (ΔV)</b></span> between points A &amp; B is defined as the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;amount&#x20;of&#x20;work&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> needed to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;move&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;a&#x20;charge&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;from&#x20;A&#x20;→&#x20;B"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> through the electric field."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>electric potential difference (ΔV)</b></span> between points A &amp; B is defined as the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>amount of work</u></span> needed to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>move</i> a charge</u> from A → B</span> through the electric field.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Can also be written as ΔPE/q (ΔPE = PE<sub>B</sub> - PE<sub>A</sub>).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-83013adc0a8fe04788ec3982b3a365598b36d286.jpg""></div>"
"An <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;equipotential&#x20;line&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is a line around a charge at which the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electric potential (V)</u></span> is the <i>same</i> at every point."	"An <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equipotential line</b></span> is a line around a charge at which the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electric potential (V)</u></span> is the <i>same</i> at every point.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The potential difference (ΔV) between the charges placed on the same equipotential line is zero.</div><div><br></div><img src=""JLT_rl8xsUutkvzyD4pTWUFlXGT3mJ0yy5j5Avm9SYQccJFlMkMaieVcEVtbRfKz6MK6x7Zv7VXTqYqGJAa0E1IJjHRBa5kJF1Ib0phf_6RzkhuZm8d.png""></div>"
"An <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equipotential line</b></span> is a line around a charge at which the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;electric&#x20;potential&#x20;&#x28;V&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is the <i>same</i> at every point."	"An <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equipotential line</b></span> is a line around a charge at which the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electric potential (V)</u></span> is the <i>same</i> at every point.

"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;electric&#x20;field&#x20;&#x28;E&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <i>exerted on</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;perpendicular&#x20;bisector&#x20;of&#x20;a&#x20;dipole&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">E = (kp)/r<sup>3</sup></span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>electric field (E)</b></span> <i>exerted on</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>perpendicular bisector of a dipole</u></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">E = (kp)/r<sup>3</sup></span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- <b>k</b> = 9 x 10<sup>9</sup>; <b>p</b> = dipole moment; <b>r</b> = radius (distance) between the two charges and the point.</div><div>- Perpendicualr bisector is shown as the line perpendicular between the two charges.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8f5b13f3675a5efdc41254c8c18f187213fbf73f.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"<div>In the equation for the <b>electric field (E)</b>&nbsp;on the <u>perpendicular bisector of a dipole</u>, the <i>direction</i> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>electric field's</b> <b>vector</b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;opposite&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[same/opposite]</span> to the direction of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">dipole moment</span>.</div><div><br></div><div>E = (kp)/r<sup>3</sup></div>"	"<div>In the equation for the <b>electric field (E)</b>&nbsp;on the <u>perpendicular bisector of a dipole</u>, the <i>direction</i> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>electric field's</b> <b>vector</b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> to the direction of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">dipole moment</span>.</div><div><br></div><div>E = (kp)/r<sup>3</sup></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- <b>k</b> = 9 x 10<sup>9</sup>; <b>p</b> = dipole moment; <b>r</b> = radius (distance) between the two charges and the point.</div><div>- Perpendicualr bisector is shown as the line perpendicular between the two charges.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8f5b13f3675a5efdc41254c8c18f187213fbf73f.jpg""></div></div></div>"

"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Lorentz&#x20;force&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the <i>sum</i> of the forces from an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electric field</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>magnetic field</u></span> acting on a single test charge."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Lorentz force</b></span> is the <i>sum</i> of the forces from an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electric field</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>magnetic field</u></span> acting on a single test charge.

"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;change&#x20;in&#x20;&lt;i&gt;length&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;a&#x20;solid&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> as a result of a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""temperature&#x20;change"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-6894133a0219dc189fffc1bc4383c2894fadb41b.jpg""></span><br>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>change in <i>length</i> of a solid</u></span> as a result of a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">temperature change</span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-6894133a0219dc189fffc1bc4383c2894fadb41b.jpg""></span><br>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>ΔL</b> = change in length; <b>α</b> = coefficient of linear expansion (given); <b>L</b> = original length; <b>ΔT</b> = change in temperature.</div><div><br></div>- Higher temperatures increase the length.<div>- Lower temperature decrease the length.</div></div>"

-&nbsp;<b>ΔV</b> = change in volume; <b>β</b> = coefficient of volumetric expansion (given); <b>V</b> = original volume; <b>ΔT</b> = change in temperature.<div>- <b>β </b>is <u>3 times larger than α</u> for the same material (liquids experience greater changes in volume than solids with changes in length).</div></div>"

"The <b>coefficient of volumetric expansion (β)</b> in liquids is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">3 times <i>larger</i></span> than the <b>coefficient of linear expansion (α)</b> in solids.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Convection</b></span> is the transfer of heat from the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>motion of </u><span style=""text-decoration-line: underline;"">fluids</span> over distances</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Radiation</b></span> is the transfer of heat through <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;electromagnetic&#x20;waves&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Radiation</b></span> is the transfer of heat through <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electromagnetic waves</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Capable of transferring heat through a vacuum (no particles).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-507bca1ddd29f5942368088d5a9b455e467e9b6b.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Radiation&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is the <i>only</i> method of <u>heat transfer</u> that can take place in the <i>absence</i> of other molecules (vacuum)."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Radiation</b></span> is the <i>only</i> method of <u>heat transfer</u> that can take place in the <i>absence</i> of other molecules (vacuum).

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"The <b>specific heat (c)</b> of a substance can <i>change</i> after the substance undergoes a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""phase&#x20;change"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"The <b>specific heat (c)</b> of a substance can <i>change</i> after the substance undergoes a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">phase change</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-5a77c41f46b4fbb8f3a1440a23551fe387197a55.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In thermodynamics, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;microstates&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are the different <i>possible</i> free movements available to molecules."	"In thermodynamics, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>microstates</b></span> are the different <i>possible</i> free movements available to molecules.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Melting ice increases its number of microstates since it's able to move around more freely.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e429513d81aae4ebf7018c33a2504ee8597091c1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"When water transitions from a solid to a liquid, its number of <b>microstates</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span>."	"When water transitions from a solid to a liquid, its number of <b>microstates</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Microstates</b>: different possible paths (movements) a molecule could take.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e429513d81aae4ebf7018c33a2504ee8597091c1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"When water transitions from a liquid to a gas, its number of <b>microstates</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span>."	"When water transitions from a liquid to a gas, its number of <b>microstates</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Microstates</b>: different possible paths (movements) a molecule could take.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e429513d81aae4ebf7018c33a2504ee8597091c1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"When water transitions from a liquid to a solid, its number of <b>microstates</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span>."	"When water transitions from a liquid to a solid, its number of <b>microstates</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreases</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Microstates</b>: different possible paths (movements) a molecule could take.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e429513d81aae4ebf7018c33a2504ee8597091c1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"During a <u>phase change</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;kinetic&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[kinetic/potential]</span> of the particles <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">remains <i>constant</i></span>, by definition of temperature."	"During a <u>phase change</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic energy</b></span> of the particles <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">remains <i>constant</i></span>, by definition of temperature.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Temperature</b>&nbsp;(measure of kinetic energy)&nbsp;<u>does not change</u> during a phase change.</div><div>- <b>Liquid</b> water at 0°C must have the <u>same kinetic energy</u> as <b>solid</b> water at 0°C (by definition of temperature).</div><div>- Molecules might be able to ""rock back and forth"" more after they overcome some intermolecular forces, but they then become restricted in ""side to side"" motion - thereby keeping temperature &amp; kinetic energy constant.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e429513d81aae4ebf7018c33a2504ee8597091c1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"During a <u>phase change</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic energy</b></span> of the particles <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""remains&#x20;&lt;i&gt;constant&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[inc/dec/constant]</span>, by definition of temperature."	"During a <u>phase change</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic energy</b></span> of the particles <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">remains <i>constant</i></span>, by definition of temperature.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Temperature</b>&nbsp;(measure of kinetic energy)&nbsp;<u>does not change</u> during a phase change.</div><div>- <b>Liquid</b> water at 0°C must have the <u>same kinetic energy</u> as <b>solid</b> water at 0°C (by definition of temperature).</div><div>- Molecules might be able to ""rock back and forth"" more after they overcome some intermolecular forces, but they then become restricted in ""side to side"" motion - thereby keeping temperature &amp; kinetic energy constant.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e429513d81aae4ebf7018c33a2504ee8597091c1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"During a <u>phase change</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;potential&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[kinetic/potential]</span> of the particles <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> by way of allowing them more space to move around."	"During a <u>phase change</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>potential energy</b></span> of the particles <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> by way of allowing them more space to move around.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Temperature</b>&nbsp;(measure of kinetic energy)&nbsp;<u>does not change</u> during a phase change.</div><div>- <b>Liquid</b> water at 0°C must have the <u>same kinetic energy</u> as <b>solid</b> water at 0°C (by definition of temperature).</div><div>- Molecules might be able to ""rock back and forth"" more after they overcome some intermolecular forces, but they then become restricted in ""side to side"" motion - thereby keeping temperature &amp; kinetic energy constant.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e429513d81aae4ebf7018c33a2504ee8597091c1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"During a <u>phase change</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>potential energy</b></span> of the particles <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[inc/dec/constant]</span> by way of allowing them more space to move around."	"During a <u>phase change</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>potential energy</b></span> of the particles <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> by way of allowing them more space to move around.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Temperature</b>&nbsp;(measure of kinetic energy)&nbsp;<u>does not change</u> during a phase change.</div><div>- <b>Liquid</b> water at 0°C must have the <u>same kinetic energy</u> as <b>solid</b> water at 0°C (by definition of temperature).</div><div>- Molecules might be able to ""rock back and forth"" more after they overcome some intermolecular forces, but they then become restricted in ""side to side"" motion - thereby keeping temperature &amp; kinetic energy constant.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e429513d81aae4ebf7018c33a2504ee8597091c1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Parallel-plate <b>capacitors</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;store&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> through the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electric field</u></span> established between their plates when a voltage is applied.</div>"	"<div>Parallel-plate <b>capacitors</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>store energy</i></span> through the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electric field</u></span> established between their plates when a voltage is applied.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>rate of heat transfer</u></span> via <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>conduction</b></span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;d5e2a69b123069861a47e621d0b93e9a744cf23c&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>rate of heat transfer</u></span> via <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>conduction</b></span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-d5e2a69b123069861a47e621d0b93e9a744cf23c.jpg""></span><br>

<div>-&nbsp;<b>H</b>&nbsp;= rate of heat transfer;&nbsp;<b>A</b>&nbsp;= conductor's cross-sectional area;&nbsp;<b>Th</b>,&nbsp;<b>Tc</b>&nbsp;= temperature of hot/cold object;&nbsp;<b>L</b>&nbsp;= conductor length/thickness.</div><div>- Actual equation shown below. <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>H</b>&nbsp;= rate of heat transfer;&nbsp;<b>A</b>&nbsp;= conductor's cross-sectional area;&nbsp;<b>Th</b>,&nbsp;<b>Tc</b>&nbsp;= temperature of hot/cold object;&nbsp;<b>L</b>&nbsp;= conductor length/thickness.</div><div>- Actual equation shown below. The above equation only relates the proportionality.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-caf1a22441dcb258bba54fcaf5d2c0bc5547306e.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-4c1a48b04637fe50df00c8c18056b2e3f200e7b7.jpg""><div></div></div>"
"<div>In the equation for the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;with&nbsp;a potential difference (ΔV), <b>d</b> is measured in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""meters"" data-ordinal=""1"">[units]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg"">"	"<div>In the equation for the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;with&nbsp;a potential difference (ΔV), <b>d</b> is measured in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">meters</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>E</b> = electric field, <b>V</b> = voltage, <b>d</b> = distance between the separation of charge.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ea30f57247930aac41e3be4dc201a56746128b79.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Lorentz force</b></span> (combination of forces from an electric field &amp; magnetic field):</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;658e5901abd85f094830ee901eb5a2e7e45ba2cd&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Lorentz force</b></span> (combination of forces from an electric field &amp; magnetic field):</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-658e5901abd85f094830ee901eb5a2e7e45ba2cd.jpg""></span><br>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>q</b> = charge; <b>E</b> = electric field; <b>v</b> = velocity; <b>B</b> = magnetic field.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-a9691759730bed0076a2dd901fd7896c4ad828f1.jpg""></div>"

<div>- <b>q</b> = charge; <b>E</b> = electric field; <b>v</b> = velocity; <b>B</b> = magnetic field.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-a9691759730bed0076a2dd901fd7896c4ad828f1.jpg""></div>"
"In the different types of <u>nuclear decay</u>, atomic changes happen <i>simultaneously</i> with the emission of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""electromagnetic&#x20;waves"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> as radiation."	"In the different types of <u>nuclear decay</u>, atomic changes happen <i>simultaneously</i> with the emission of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">electromagnetic waves</span> as radiation.

"In standing waves, pipes with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[less/more]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>harmonic numbers (n)</u></span>&nbsp;have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>shorter</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>wavelengths (λ)</b></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>frequencies (f)</b></span>."	"In standing waves, pipes with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>harmonic numbers (n)</u></span>&nbsp;have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>shorter</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>wavelengths (λ)</b></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>frequencies (f)</b></span>.

"In standing waves, pipes with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>harmonic numbers (n)</u></span>&nbsp;have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;shorter&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[shorter/longer]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>wavelengths (λ)</b></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[lower/higher]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>frequencies (f)</b></span>."	"In standing waves, pipes with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>harmonic numbers (n)</u></span>&nbsp;have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>shorter</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>wavelengths (λ)</b></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>frequencies (f)</b></span>.

"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>circular polarized light</b></span>, the two <u>transverse waves</u> are <i>out of phase</i> by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""90"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>° - allowing for interference that results in a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""45"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>° resultant wave (appearing to turn)."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>circular polarized light</b></span>, the two <u>transverse waves</u> are <i>out of phase</i> by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">90</span>° - allowing for interference that results in a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">45</span>° resultant wave (appearing to turn).

<div>- Being at an angle of 90° from each other allows them to add to a 45° combined oscillation - facilitating turning.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-bf35e569edd49b8083fe8d4c582bf544504154de.jpg""></div>"

"In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;longitudinal&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[long./transverse]</span> <b>waves</b>, areas of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>low</i></span>&nbsp;pressure are called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>rarefactions</u></span>."	"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>longitudinal</b></span> <b>waves</b>, areas of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>low</i></span>&nbsp;pressure are called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>rarefactions</u></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-d8e97f69d872dadf1fd94303865545f16fa18ead.jpg""></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>longitudinal</b></span> <b>waves</b>, areas of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[low/high]</span>&nbsp;pressure are called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>rarefactions</u></span>."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>longitudinal</b></span> <b>waves</b>, areas of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>low</i></span>&nbsp;pressure are called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>rarefactions</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-d8e97f69d872dadf1fd94303865545f16fa18ead.jpg""></div>"
"In the <b>Doppler effect</b>, the velocities of individual sound waves <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;do&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[do/do not]</span> change - successive waves are only perceived to be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>produced</i> at slower/faster rates</u></span> depending on relative velocities."	"In the <b>Doppler effect</b>, the velocities of individual sound waves <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>do not</i></span> change - successive waves are only perceived to be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>produced</i> at slower/faster rates</u></span> depending on relative velocities.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<u>None</u> of the sound waves actually change their velocities!</div><div>- Crests/troughs are perceived to be closer together or further apart depending on whether the source is moving towards or away from the observer.</div><div>- Notice how the man perceives the ambulance to be producing more sound waves per second when it's moving towards him.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-c82370dbcc23ea6731f5157a0e77d1e946201b66.jpg""></div>"
"In the <b>Doppler effect</b>, the velocities of individual sound waves <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>do not</i></span> change - successive waves are only perceived to be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;produced&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;at&#x20;slower&#x2F;faster&#x20;rates&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> depending on relative velocities."	"In the <b>Doppler effect</b>, the velocities of individual sound waves <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>do not</i></span> change - successive waves are only perceived to be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>produced</i> at slower/faster rates</u></span> depending on relative velocities.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<u>None</u> of the sound waves actually change their velocities!</div><div>- Crests/troughs are perceived to be closer together or further apart depending on whether the source is moving towards or away from the observer.</div><div>- Notice how the man perceives the ambulance to be producing more sound waves per second when it's moving towards him.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-c82370dbcc23ea6731f5157a0e77d1e946201b66.jpg""></div>"
"The <b>Doppler effect</b>&nbsp;can occur with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;both&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;longitudinal&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;transverse&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[long./transv./both]</span> waves."	"The <b>Doppler effect</b>&nbsp;can occur with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>both</i> <u>longitudinal</u> &amp; <u>transverse</u></span> waves.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The&nbsp;<b>Doppler effect</b>&nbsp;<i>can</i>&nbsp;occur with both longitudinal &amp; transverse waves.</div><div>-&nbsp;<u>None</u> of the sound waves actually change their velocities!</div><div>- Notice how the man perceives the ambulance to be producing more sound waves per second when it's moving towards him.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-c82370dbcc23ea6731f5157a0e77d1e946201b66.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Lens power</b> is sometimes <i>referred to</i> as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""lens&#x20;strength"" data-ordinal=""1"">[name]</span>, denoted by the symbol <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""S"" data-ordinal=""1"">[symbol]</span>."	"<b>Lens power</b> is sometimes <i>referred to</i> as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">lens strength</span>, denoted by the symbol <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">S</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-153dc861cc4fde84a067591005c8255fbecaeba0.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-96030572fd5cac43380911d08e5b531eda84eac1.jpg""></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>single</u></span><u>-slit light diffraction</u>, light waves that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;differ&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;in&#x20;wavelength"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;1λ&#x2F;2&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[magnitude]</span> add <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>destructively</i></span> - resulting in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">dark</span> band."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>single</u></span><u>-slit light diffraction</u>, light waves that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>differ</i> in wavelength</span> by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>1λ/2</b></span> add <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>destructively</i></span> - resulting in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">dark</span> band.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This <u>integer difference</u> is indicated by differences in <b>n</b> (nλ/2 → 1λ/2, 2λ/2, 3λ/2, 4λ/2, etc.)</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-63ec36cf4451cb3f497232ddfa49ac5ed4f2e351.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e762ed6efa7895dc287bcbfc126a0b7533e9e541.jpg""></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>single</u></span><u>-slit light diffraction</u>, light waves that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>differ</i> in wavelength</span> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>1λ/2</b></span> add <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;destructively&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[const./destruct.]</span> - resulting in a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""dark"" data-ordinal=""2"">[light/dark]</span> band."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>single</u></span><u>-slit light diffraction</u>, light waves that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>differ</i> in wavelength</span> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>1λ/2</b></span> add <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>destructively</i></span> - resulting in a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">dark</span> band.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This <u>integer difference</u> is indicated by differences in <b>n</b> (nλ/2 → 1λ/2, 2λ/2, 3λ/2, 4λ/2, etc.)</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-63ec36cf4451cb3f497232ddfa49ac5ed4f2e351.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e762ed6efa7895dc287bcbfc126a0b7533e9e541.jpg""></div>"
"In single-slit light diffraction, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>widening</i></span> the <b>length of the slit (a)</b> results in a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;narrow&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[wide/narrow]</span> <u>band pattern</u>."	"In single-slit light diffraction, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>widening</i></span> the <b>length of the slit (a)</b> results in a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>narrow</i></span> <u>band pattern</u>.

"In single-slit light diffraction, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;widening&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[shortening/widening]</span> the <b>length of the slit (a)</b> results in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>narrow</i></span> <u>band pattern</u>."	"In single-slit light diffraction, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>widening</i></span> the <b>length of the slit (a)</b> results in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>narrow</i></span> <u>band pattern</u>.

<div><img src=""paste-fae1b482b42045108b7d6a20732a5391b586faba.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e762ed6efa7895dc287bcbfc126a0b7533e9e541.jpg""></div>"
"In single-slit light diffraction, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>shortening</i></span> the <b>length of the slit (a)</b> results in a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;wide&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[wide/narrow]</span> <u>band pattern</u>." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This effect becomes even more pronounced as you shorten the slit to a width smaller than that of the incident light's wavelength (λ).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-fae1b482b42045108b7d6a20732a5391b586faba.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e762ed6efa7895dc287bcbfc126a0b7533e9e541.jpg""></div>"

"In single-slit light diffraction, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>shortening</i></span> the <b>length of the slit (a)</b> results in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>wide</i></span> <u>band pattern</u>.

"<b>Plane polarimeters</b> are <i>only</i> able to polarize <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""transverse"" data-ordinal=""1"">[longit./transv.]</span> waves."	"<b>Plane polarimeters</b> are <i>only</i> able to polarize <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">transverse</span> waves.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-6b348e3f0c66212e81f891935ed54e74e252aae0.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Plane polarimeters</b>&nbsp;<i>only</i> polarize the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""electric&#x20;field&#x20;vector"" data-ordinal=""1"">[magnetic/electric]</span> component of <u>electromagnetic waves</u> - the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""magnetic&#x20;field&#x20;vectors"" data-ordinal=""1"">[magnetic/electric]</span> are instead <i>absorbed</i>.&nbsp;"	"<b>Plane polarimeters</b>&nbsp;<i>only</i> polarize the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">electric field vector</span> component of <u>electromagnetic waves</u> - the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">magnetic field vectors</span> are instead <i>absorbed</i>.&nbsp;
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The only light that gets polarized by polarimeters is the electric field component.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-6b348e3f0c66212e81f891935ed54e74e252aae0.jpg""></div>"
"<i>Before</i> <u>electromagnetic light</u> is passed through a <b>plane-polarimeter</b>, its light waves are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;scattered&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;in&#x20;every&#x20;direction"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<i>Before</i> <u>electromagnetic light</u> is passed through a <b>plane-polarimeter</b>, its light waves are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>scattered</i> in every direction</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Plane polarimeters only allow the electric field component of electromagnetic waves to pass through oriented along one axis though - everything else is absorbed.</div><div>- The only light that gets polarized by polarimeters is the electric field component.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-6b348e3f0c66212e81f891935ed54e74e252aae0.jpg""></div>"
"Electromagnetic light passed through a <b>plane-polarimeter</b> usually <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;loses&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[gains/loses]</span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""50"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>% of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>light intensity</u></span>, since <i>some</i> of the electric field vectors perpendicular to the axis are absorbed."	"Electromagnetic light passed through a <b>plane-polarimeter</b> usually <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>loses</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">50</span>% of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>light intensity</u></span>, since <i>some</i> of the electric field vectors perpendicular to the axis are absorbed.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Plane polarimeters <u>only</u> allow the <b>electric field</b> component of electromagnetic waves to pass through oriented along one axis though - everything else is absorbed.</div><div>- The only light that gets polarized by polarimeters is the electric field component.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-6b348e3f0c66212e81f891935ed54e74e252aae0.jpg""></div>"
"Electromagnetic light passed through a <b>plane-polarimeter</b> usually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>loses</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">50</span>% of its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;light&#x20;intensity&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[property]</span>, since <i>some</i> of the electric field vectors perpendicular to the axis are absorbed."	"Electromagnetic light passed through a <b>plane-polarimeter</b> usually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>loses</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">50</span>% of its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>light intensity</u></span>, since <i>some</i> of the electric field vectors perpendicular to the axis are absorbed.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Plane polarimeters <u>only</u> allow the <b>electric field</b> component of electromagnetic waves to pass through oriented along one axis though - everything else is absorbed.</div><div>- The only light that gets polarized by polarimeters is the electric field component.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-6b348e3f0c66212e81f891935ed54e74e252aae0.jpg""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Diffraction grating</b></span> is a type of light diffraction where <i>many</i> small slits are lined up - resulting in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&quot;color&#x20;splitting&quot;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> due to the <u>interference</u> of the diffracted light from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>non-adjacent</i> slits</span>.<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Diffraction grating</b></span> is a type of light diffraction where <i>many</i> small slits are lined up - resulting in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">""color splitting""</span> due to the <u>interference</u> of the diffracted light from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>non-adjacent</i> slits</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Color splitting only works with <b>polychromatic light</b> (like white light).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-69aef0bc800439bb2c1d7e822f98a6f7ada812bc.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"""Color splitting"" associated with <u>diffraction grating</u> or <u>light refraction</u>&nbsp;<i>only</i> happens with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;poly&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><b>chromatic light</b>.&nbsp;"	"""Color splitting"" associated with <u>diffraction grating</u> or <u>light refraction</u>&nbsp;<i>only</i> happens with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>poly</b></span><b>chromatic light</b>.&nbsp;
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Polychromatic</b> light is light composed of ""multiple colors"" (many different wavelengths).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-6dd7bbafd3a1acc115d03891ab6698064e0d8869.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Thin&#x2D;film&#x20;interference&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[phenomenon]</span> passes light through a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">semitransparent medium</span> - causing ""color splitting"" due to the <u>interference</u>&nbsp;between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>bottom</i> layer's reflected light</span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>top</i> layer's reflected light</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Thin-film interference</b></span> passes light through a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">semitransparent medium</span> - causing ""color splitting"" due to the <u>interference</u>&nbsp;between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>bottom</i> layer's reflected light</span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>top</i> layer's reflected light</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Part of the light reflects off the top surface, other part passes through and reflects off the bottom surface. Wavelengths bend slightly and interfere with each other (resulting in ""color splitting.""</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ba965a60fb23abb49ad5b3685d02e47ae1b57340.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-23dac7d156d4c54f9e02d52d648e323dbb98b165.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Thin-film interference</b></span> passes light through a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""semitransparent&#x20;medium"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - causing ""color splitting"" due to the <u>interference</u>&nbsp;between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>bottom</i> layer's reflected light</span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>top</i> layer's reflected light</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Thin-film interference</b></span> passes light through a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">semitransparent medium</span> - causing ""color splitting"" due to the <u>interference</u>&nbsp;between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>bottom</i> layer's reflected light</span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>top</i> layer's reflected light</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Part of the light reflects off the top surface, other part passes through and reflects off the bottom surface. Wavelengths bend slightly and interfere with each other (resulting in ""color splitting.""</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ba965a60fb23abb49ad5b3685d02e47ae1b57340.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-23dac7d156d4c54f9e02d52d648e323dbb98b165.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Thin-film interference</b></span> passes light through a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">semitransparent medium</span> - causing ""color splitting"" due to the <u>interference</u>&nbsp;between the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;bottom&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;layer&#x27;s&#x20;reflected&#x20;light"" data-ordinal=""4"">[...]</span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;top&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;layer&#x27;s&#x20;reflected&#x20;light"" data-ordinal=""4"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Thin-film interference</b></span> passes light through a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">semitransparent medium</span> - causing ""color splitting"" due to the <u>interference</u>&nbsp;between the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>bottom</i> layer's reflected light</span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>top</i> layer's reflected light</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Part of the light reflects off the top surface, other part passes through and reflects off the bottom surface. Wavelengths bend slightly and interfere with each other (resulting in ""color splitting.""</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ba965a60fb23abb49ad5b3685d02e47ae1b57340.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-23dac7d156d4c54f9e02d52d648e323dbb98b165.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"The degree of ""color splitting"" associated with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;thin&#x2D;film&#x20;interference&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[phenomenon]</span> is influenced by the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>thickness</i></span> of the <u>semitransparent medium</u>, since it can change the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">wavelength</span> of the light - changing <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">interference patterns</span>."	"The degree of ""color splitting"" associated with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>thin-film interference</b></span> is influenced by the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>thickness</i></span> of the <u>semitransparent medium</u>, since it can change the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">wavelength</span> of the light - changing <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">interference patterns</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The multi-colored array generated depends on the pattern of constructive &amp; destructive interference.<br></div><div>- Part of the light reflects off the top surface, other part passes through and reflects off the bottom surface. Wavelengths bend slightly and interfere with each other (resulting in ""color splitting.""</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ba965a60fb23abb49ad5b3685d02e47ae1b57340.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-23dac7d156d4c54f9e02d52d648e323dbb98b165.jpg""><br></div></div>"

"The degree of ""color splitting"" associated with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>thin-film interference</b></span> is influenced by the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>thickness</i></span> of the <u>semitransparent medium</u>, since it can change the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">wavelength</span> of the light - changing <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">interference patterns</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because more optically dense mediums (like water) have more atoms that absorb &amp; re-emit the light - slowing it down.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-eb0a500b565960f12493865dbbb9663639e3e642.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Light travels the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>fastest</i></span> in mediums with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[low/high]</span> <u>refractive indices (n)</u>."	"Light travels the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>fastest</i></span> in mediums with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> <u>refractive indices (n)</u>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because more optically dense mediums (like water) have more atoms that absorb &amp; re-emit the light - slowing it down.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-eb0a500b565960f12493865dbbb9663639e3e642.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;intensity&#x20;&#x28;amplitude&lt;sup&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[property]</span> of a wave is related to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>loudness</u></span> (sound) or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>brightness</u></span> (light)."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intensity (amplitude<sup>2</sup>)</b></span> of a wave is related to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>loudness</u></span> (sound) or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>brightness</u></span> (light).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Intensity = amplitude<sup>2</sup>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-673a550c742e342965d11b5d357125a23ac46e2d.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a5dab2a41d7f3ba572fbce759a546b4a47f59e1a.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intensity (amplitude<sup>2</sup>)</b></span> of a wave is related to its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;loudness&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> (sound) or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;brightness&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> (light)."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intensity (amplitude<sup>2</sup>)</b></span> of a wave is related to its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>loudness</u></span> (sound) or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>brightness</u></span> (light).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Intensity = amplitude<sup>2</sup>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-673a550c742e342965d11b5d357125a23ac46e2d.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a5dab2a41d7f3ba572fbce759a546b4a47f59e1a.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Simple&#x20;fluid&#x20;barometers&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are devices used to measure <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">atmospheric pressure (P<sub>atm</sub>)</span>&nbsp;by measuring the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>height of water in a tube</u></span>&nbsp;with one end <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>immersed</i> in the container</span>." <div>The buoyant force applied on an object is the same at 100m of depth as it is at a depth of 10m.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-d6d354709831046562bbd41e1252ee712ff38dd3.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;absolute&#x20;temperature&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of a substance refers to the <i>average</i> kinetic energy of its particles <i>relative</i> to absolute zero."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>absolute temperature</b></span> of a substance refers to the <i>average</i> kinetic energy of its particles <i>relative</i> to absolute zero.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Measured with the <b>Kelvin</b> scale.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-02a8381e6f34230842ff008819d2437e5e9a7962.jpg""></div>"
"The <b>molar heat capacity (C)</b> of gas with a constant <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;C&lt;sub&gt;P&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[property]</span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> than the molar heat capacity (C) of a gas at a constant <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;volume&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;C&lt;sub&gt;V&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[property]</span>."	"The <b>molar heat capacity (C)</b> of gas with a constant <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>pressure</u> (C<sub>P</sub>)</span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> than the molar heat capacity (C) of a gas at a constant <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>volume</u> (C<sub>V</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because heat is lost as work as it enters the container.<br></div><div>- <b>C<sub>P</sub>&nbsp;- C<sub>V</sub>&nbsp;= R</b><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-187ddf6bbb05e9d8f8be8fc66a40d2867aaea807.jpg""></div>"
"The <b>molar heat capacity (C)</b> of gas with a constant <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>pressure</u> (C<sub>P</sub>)</span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[lower/higher]</span> than the molar heat capacity (C) of a gas at a constant <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>volume</u> (C<sub>V</sub>)</span>."	"The <b>molar heat capacity (C)</b> of gas with a constant <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>pressure</u> (C<sub>P</sub>)</span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> than the molar heat capacity (C) of a gas at a constant <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>volume</u> (C<sub>V</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because heat is lost as work as it enters the container.<br></div><div>- <b>C<sub>P</sub>&nbsp;- C<sub>V</sub>&nbsp;= R</b><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-187ddf6bbb05e9d8f8be8fc66a40d2867aaea807.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>molar heat capacity (C)</b></span> of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>ideal gas</u></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;3b3cbcae93a408c6be2d14b8a58529547a0073e9&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>molar heat capacity (C)</b></span> of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>ideal gas</u></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-3b3cbcae93a408c6be2d14b8a58529547a0073e9.jpg""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>C</b> = molar heat capacity; <b>Q</b> = heat added/removed from system (U = Q - W); <b>n</b> = number of moles of gas; <b>ΔT</b> = change in temperature.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>C<sub>P</sub>&nbsp;- C<sub>V</sub>&nbsp;= R</b><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-187ddf6bbb05e9d8f8be8fc66a40d2867aaea807.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;molar&#x20;heat&#x20;capacity&#x20;&#x28;C&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;ideal&#x20;gas&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-3b3cbcae93a408c6be2d14b8a58529547a0073e9.jpg""></span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>molar heat capacity (C)</b></span> of an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>ideal gas</u></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-3b3cbcae93a408c6be2d14b8a58529547a0073e9.jpg""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>C</b> = molar heat capacity; <b>Q</b> = heat added/removed from system (U = Q - W); <b>n</b> = number of moles of gas; <b>ΔT</b> = change in temperature.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>C<sub>P</sub>&nbsp;- C<sub>V</sub>&nbsp;= R</b><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-187ddf6bbb05e9d8f8be8fc66a40d2867aaea807.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat capacity (C)</b></span> of a monoatomic ideal gas at a constant <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">volume (C<sub>V</sub>)</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""C&lt;sub&gt;V&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;1&#x2E;5&#x28;n&#x29;&#x28;R&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat capacity (C)</b></span> of a monoatomic ideal gas at a constant <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">volume (C<sub>V</sub>)</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">C<sub>V</sub> = 1.5(n)(R)</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>C</b> = molar heat capacity; <b>n</b> = total number of molecules;&nbsp;<b>k<sub>B</sub></b> = Boltzmann's constant (1.4 x 10<sup>-23</sup>); <b>R</b> = ideal gas constant.</div><div>- <u>Do not confuse this</u> with the <b>molar</b> heat capacity at a constant volume (C<sub>V</sub>). This is the same, except there's <u>no</u> 'n.'</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>C<sub>P</sub>&nbsp;- C<sub>V</sub>&nbsp;= R</b></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-3b3cbcae93a408c6be2d14b8a58529547a0073e9.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-187ddf6bbb05e9d8f8be8fc66a40d2867aaea807.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;heat&#x20;capacity&#x20;&#x28;C&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of a monoatomic ideal gas at a constant <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""volume&#x20;&#x28;C&lt;sub&gt;V&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[property]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">C<sub>V</sub> = 1.5(n)(R)</span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat capacity (C)</b></span> of a monoatomic ideal gas at a constant <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">volume (C<sub>V</sub>)</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">C<sub>V</sub> = 1.5(n)(R)</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>C</b> = molar heat capacity; <b>n</b> = total number of molecules;&nbsp;<b>k<sub>B</sub></b> = Boltzmann's constant (1.4 x 10<sup>-23</sup>); <b>R</b> = ideal gas constant.</div><div>- <u>Do not confuse this</u> with the <b>molar</b> heat capacity at a constant volume (C<sub>V</sub>). This is the same, except there's <u>no</u> 'n.'</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>C<sub>P</sub>&nbsp;- C<sub>V</sub>&nbsp;= R</b></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-3b3cbcae93a408c6be2d14b8a58529547a0073e9.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-187ddf6bbb05e9d8f8be8fc66a40d2867aaea807.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat capacity (C)</b></span> of a monoatomic ideal gas at a constant <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pressure (C<sub>P</sub>)</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""C&lt;sub&gt;P&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x3D;&#x20;2&#x2E;5&#x28;n&#x29;&#x28;R&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat capacity (C)</b></span> of a monoatomic ideal gas at a constant <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pressure (C<sub>P</sub>)</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">C<sub>P</sub>&nbsp;= 2.5(n)(R)</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>C</b>&nbsp;= molar heat capacity;&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= total number of molecules;&nbsp;<b>k<sub>B</sub></b>&nbsp;= Boltzmann's constant (1.4 x 10<sup>-23</sup>);&nbsp;<b>R</b>&nbsp;= ideal gas constant.</div><div>-&nbsp;<u>Do not confuse this</u>&nbsp;with the&nbsp;<b>molar</b>&nbsp;heat capacity at a constant pressure (C<sub>P</sub>). This is the same, except there's&nbsp;<u>no</u>&nbsp;'n.'</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>C<sub>P</sub>&nbsp;- C<sub>V</sub>&nbsp;= R</b></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-3b3cbcae93a408c6be2d14b8a58529547a0073e9.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-187ddf6bbb05e9d8f8be8fc66a40d2867aaea807.jpg""><div></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;heat&#x20;capacity&#x20;&#x28;C&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of a monoatomic ideal gas at a constant <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pressure&#x20;&#x28;C&lt;sub&gt;P&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[property]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">C<sub>P</sub>&nbsp;= 2.5(n)(R)</span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat capacity (C)</b></span> of a monoatomic ideal gas at a constant <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">pressure (C<sub>P</sub>)</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">C<sub>P</sub>&nbsp;= 2.5(n)(R)</span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>C</b>&nbsp;= molar heat capacity;&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= total number of molecules;&nbsp;<b>k<sub>B</sub></b>&nbsp;= Boltzmann's constant (1.4 x 10<sup>-23</sup>);&nbsp;<b>R</b>&nbsp;= ideal gas constant.</div><div>-&nbsp;<u>Do not confuse this</u>&nbsp;with the&nbsp;<b>molar</b>&nbsp;heat capacity at a constant pressure (C<sub>P</sub>). This is the same, except there's&nbsp;<u>no</u>&nbsp;'n.'</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>C<sub>P</sub>&nbsp;- C<sub>V</sub>&nbsp;= R</b></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-3b3cbcae93a408c6be2d14b8a58529547a0073e9.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-187ddf6bbb05e9d8f8be8fc66a40d2867aaea807.jpg""><div></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <i>relationship</i> between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat capacities (C)</b> of a gas</span>&nbsp;at a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;constant&#x20;pressure&#x20;&#x28;C&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;sub&gt;P&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;u&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;and at a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;constant&#x20;volume&#x20;&#x28;C&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;sub&gt;V&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;u&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">C<sub>P</sub> - C<sub>V</sub> = R (ideal gas constant)</span></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <i>relationship</i> between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat capacities (C)</b> of a gas</span>&nbsp;at a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>constant pressure (C</u><sub>P</sub><u>)</u></span>&nbsp;and at a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>constant volume (C</u><sub>V</sub><u>)</u></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">C<sub>P</sub> - C<sub>V</sub> = R (ideal gas constant)</span></div>

"<u>Magnetic forces (F</u><sub>M</sub><u>)</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> do <b>work</b> on charges, because they point <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""exactly&#x20;&lt;i&gt;perpendicular&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x28;90&#xB0;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> to their velocities."	"<u>Magnetic forces (F</u><sub>M</sub><u>)</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>cannot</i></span> do <b>work</b> on charges, because they point <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">exactly <i>perpendicular</i>&nbsp;(90°)</span> to their velocities.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The F<sub>M</sub> and velocity of the charge are <u>perpendicular</u> to each other, so W=Fdcos(90) → W=0.</div><div><br></div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""></div>"

<div>- <b>Q</b> = flow rate; <b>ΔP</b> = pressure difference; <b>R</b> = resistance.</div><div>- This equation is used in&nbsp;<u>Poiseuille's law</u>&nbsp;(below).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c3f72dd7b09c60138d631dc135d5110b87242dd2.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-5b48699ed376c70c77186b5701447120ba5b7d1e.jpg""><br></div></div>"

<div>- <b>Q</b> = flow rate; <b>ΔP</b> = pressure difference; <b>R</b> = resistance.</div><div>- This equation is used in&nbsp;<u>Poiseuille's law</u>&nbsp;(below).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c3f72dd7b09c60138d631dc135d5110b87242dd2.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-5b48699ed376c70c77186b5701447120ba5b7d1e.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"According to <u>Pascal's principle</u>, the <b>force</b> applied on one end of the system <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;is&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[is/is not]</span> equal to the force on the other end when the area is different - <i>only</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pressures"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> must be equal."	"According to <u>Pascal's principle</u>, the <b>force</b> applied on one end of the system <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>is not</i></span> equal to the force on the other end when the area is different - <i>only</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">pressures</span> must be equal.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>In order to keep the pressures on both ends equal, the force must change if the area changes.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-b0a9a972be5d473c73f5498cd20dab9be95088a0.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation that relates the <b>force</b> applied on one end of a <u>hydraulic system</u> to the <i>resultant</i> force on the other end:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;41bc9731f680a38789bbc5d5da8a8de02169a60a&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Equation that relates the <b>force</b> applied on one end of a <u>hydraulic system</u> to the <i>resultant</i> force on the other end:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-41bc9731f680a38789bbc5d5da8a8de02169a60a.jpg""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <u>Only</u> the <b>pressures</b> must be <u>equal on both sides</u> (P = F/area).</div><div>- In order to keep the pressures on both ends equal, the force must change if the area changes.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-b0a9a972be5d473c73f5498cd20dab9be95088a0.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Venturi&#x20;effect&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> describes how fluids with&nbsp;<i>higher</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>velocities</u></span> exert <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>lower</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>pressures</u></span> on the sides of a tube."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Venturi effect</b></span> describes how fluids with&nbsp;<i>higher</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>velocities</u></span> exert <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>lower</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>pressures</u></span> on the sides of a tube.
<hr>

"As the <u>angle</u> of an inclined plane <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[inc./dec.]</span>, the <b>kinetic friction (F<sub>fk</sub>)</b> of the sliding object <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span>, due to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smaller</i> normal force (F<sub>N</sub>)</span>."	"As the <u>angle</u> of an inclined plane <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span>, the <b>kinetic friction (F<sub>fk</sub>)</b> of the sliding object <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span>, due to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smaller</i> normal force (F<sub>N</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Normal force (F<sub>N</sub>)</b> only depends on <u>mass</u>, <u>gravity</u>, and other component vectors.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-c273d5fb4c0698cd090852f430fded5d9a307aac.jpg""></div>"
"Electromagnetic waves with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[lower/higher]</span> <b>energies</b> have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>hotter</i></span> <u>temperatures</u>."	"Electromagnetic waves with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <b>energies</b> have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>hotter</i></span> <u>temperatures</u>.

<div><u>Don't</u> confuse this with the red/violet coloring of the visible light spectrum!</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-f627095838449336e7ef20c522925fc85e7f6e9c.jpg""></div>"
"Electromagnetic waves with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <b>energies</b> have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;hotter&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[colder/hotter]</span> <u>temperatures</u>."	"Electromagnetic waves with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <b>energies</b> have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>hotter</i></span> <u>temperatures</u>.

"For every <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">10 dB</span> <i style="""">increase</i> in a <u>sound</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;intensity&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[property]</span> changes by a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>multiple</i> of 10</span>."	"For every <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">10 dB</span> <i style="""">increase</i> in a <u>sound</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intensity</b></span> changes by a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>multiple</i> of 10</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>60 dB → 70 dB = intensity is 10x greater.</div><div>60 dB → 80 dB = intensity is 100x greater.</div><div>60 dB → 90 dB = intensity is 1000x greater.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-906fe153ff259ce8291d07a9411bf70c11d2cdd6.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-101b563191479aafc304f232e99622e81dc53eb7.jpg""></div></div>"

"<div>In the below equation for the <u>elastic potential energy</u> stored in a spring, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;spring&#x20;constant&#x20;&#x28;k&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[variable]</span>&nbsp;is a measure of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">spring's elasticity</span>.</div><div><br></div><div>PE = 1/2kx<sup>2</sup></div>"	"<div>In the below equation for the <u>elastic potential energy</u> stored in a spring, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>spring constant (k)</b></span>&nbsp;is a measure of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">spring's elasticity</span>.</div><div><br></div><div>PE = 1/2kx<sup>2</sup></div>

If there was an upward force equal to the weight of the hanging object, it wouldn't be falling.</div>"

"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>momentum</b></span> of an object:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;b25c9b010dab2434644690e987d8690f8f1d5118&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>momentum</b></span> of an object:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-b25c9b010dab2434644690e987d8690f8f1d5118.jpg""></span><br></div>
