"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;momentum&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of an object:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-b25c9b010dab2434644690e987d8690f8f1d5118.jpg""></span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>momentum</b></span> of an object:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-b25c9b010dab2434644690e987d8690f8f1d5118.jpg""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>p</b> = momentum; <b>m</b> = mass; <b>v</b> = velocity.</div>"
"<div>When an object is in motion, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>inertia</b></span> is equivalent to its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;mass&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>When an object is in motion, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>inertia</b></span> is equivalent to its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>mass</u></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Velocity has <u>no effect</u> on the inertia (will affect the momentum, though).</div>- 5 objects (of equal mass) travelling at different speeds will have the <u>same inertia</u>, since they each still have the same mass.</div>"
"In order for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;total&#x20;internal&#x20;reflection&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> to occur, the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>first</i></span>&nbsp;medium must be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>larger</i></span> (<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">slower</span>) than the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>second</i></span> medium (<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">faster</span>)."	"In order for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>total internal reflection</b></span> to occur, the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>first</i></span>&nbsp;medium must be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>larger</i></span> (<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">slower</span>) than the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>second</i></span> medium (<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">faster</span>).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Happens when the incident angle is greater than the&nbsp;<b>critical angle (θ<sub>c</sub>)</b>.</div><div>- When light is refracted out of a slow medium (water) into a faster medium (air), the refracted angle is larger than the angle it hit at. If the incident angle is big enough, eventually the refracted angle is so big that it never leaves the medium. This is the&nbsp;<b>critical angle (θ<sub>c</sub>)</b>.</div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WRuatAcd2WY"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WRuatAcd2WY</a></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-18081221999daf6b4ce117107334970db27b170c.jpg""></div>"
"In order for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>total internal reflection</b></span> to occur, the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;first&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[first/second]</span>&nbsp;medium must be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>larger</i></span> (<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">slower</span>) than the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;second&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[first/second]</span> medium (<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">faster</span>)."	"In order for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>total internal reflection</b></span> to occur, the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>first</i></span>&nbsp;medium must be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>larger</i></span> (<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">slower</span>) than the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>second</i></span> medium (<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">faster</span>).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Happens when the incident angle is greater than the&nbsp;<b>critical angle (θ<sub>c</sub>)</b>.</div><div>- When light is refracted out of a slow medium (water) into a faster medium (air), the refracted angle is larger than the angle it hit at. If the incident angle is big enough, eventually the refracted angle is so big that it never leaves the medium. This is the&nbsp;<b>critical angle (θ<sub>c</sub>)</b>.</div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WRuatAcd2WY"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WRuatAcd2WY</a></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-18081221999daf6b4ce117107334970db27b170c.jpg""></div>"
"In order for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>total internal reflection</b></span> to occur, the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>first</i></span>&nbsp;medium must be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;larger&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[smaller/larger]</span> (<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""slower"" data-ordinal=""3"">[slower/faster]</span>) than the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>second</i></span> medium (<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""faster"" data-ordinal=""3"">[slower/faster]</span>)."	"In order for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>total internal reflection</b></span> to occur, the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>first</i></span>&nbsp;medium must be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>larger</i></span> (<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">slower</span>) than the <u>refractive index (n)</u> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>second</i></span> medium (<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">faster</span>).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Happens when the incident angle is greater than the&nbsp;<b>critical angle (θ<sub>c</sub>)</b>.</div><div>- When light is refracted out of a slow medium (water) into a faster medium (air), the refracted angle is larger than the angle it hit at. If the incident angle is big enough, eventually the refracted angle is so big that it never leaves the medium. This is the&nbsp;<b>critical angle (θ<sub>c</sub>)</b>.</div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WRuatAcd2WY"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WRuatAcd2WY</a></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-18081221999daf6b4ce117107334970db27b170c.jpg""></div>"
"<u>Currents (I)</u><b> </b><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;are&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[are/are not]</span> allowed to move in <b>insulators</b>."	"<u>Currents (I)</u><b> </b><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>are not</i></span> allowed to move in <b>insulators</b>.
<hr>

"On a concave mirror, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;focal&#x20;length&#x20;&#x28;f&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> can be <i>calculated</i>&nbsp;with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">f = r/2</span>."	"On a concave mirror, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>focal length (f)</b></span> can be <i>calculated</i>&nbsp;with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">f = r/2</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>r</b> represents the radius of the lens' curvature (i.e. the radius of the lens itself).</div><div>- <b>f</b> = focal length (distance between focal point &amp; mirror); <b>o</b> = object (thing you're looking at); <b>i</b> = image (what you're actually seeing).<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GV1UZSTNJg</a><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-1e54f2f0d29538ba74aa3001ec2e0c3943ff750a.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"In a converging lens, as you move the <u>object</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;closer&#x20;to&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[closer to/further from]</span> the <b>lens</b>, the <u>image</u> moves <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>further away </i>from</span> the lens."	"In a converging lens, as you move the <u>object</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>closer to</i></span> the <b>lens</b>, the <u>image</u> moves <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>further away </i>from</span> the lens.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>1/o &amp; 1/i are <b>inversely proportional</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-359098d5b90058833dccc0a41fbcd1c51dea0261.jpg""></div>"
"In a converging lens, as you move the <u>object</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>closer to</i></span> the <b>lens</b>, the <u>image</u> moves <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;further&#x20;away&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;from"" data-ordinal=""2"">[closer to/further away]</span> the lens."	"In a converging lens, as you move the <u>object</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>closer to</i></span> the <b>lens</b>, the <u>image</u> moves <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>further away </i>from</span> the lens.

"In a converging lens, as you move the <u>object</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;further&#x20;away&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;from"" data-ordinal=""1"">[closer to/further from]</span> the <b>lens</b>, the <u>image</u> moves <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>closer to</i></span> the lens." <div>1/o &amp; 1/i are <b>inversely proportional</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-359098d5b90058833dccc0a41fbcd1c51dea0261.jpg""></div>"
"In a converging lens, as you move the <u>object</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>further away</i>&nbsp;from</span> the <b>lens</b>, the <u>image</u> moves <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;closer&#x20;to&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[closer to/further away]</span> the lens."	"In a converging lens, as you move the <u>object</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>further away</i>&nbsp;from</span> the <b>lens</b>, the <u>image</u> moves <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>closer to</i></span> the lens.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>1/o &amp; 1/i are <b>inversely proportional</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-359098d5b90058833dccc0a41fbcd1c51dea0261.jpg""></div>"
"For resistors in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""parallel"" data-ordinal=""1"">[series/parallel]</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span> <u>resistance</u> will have the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>highest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b> output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>largest</i>&nbsp;portion of the current (I)</span> flowing through it."	"For resistors in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">parallel</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span> <u>resistance</u> will have the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>highest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b> output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>largest</i>&nbsp;portion of the current (I)</span> flowing through it.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Power (P) </b>in terms of resistors refers to the <u>amount of heat dissipated</u>... smaller resistors in parallel dissipate more heat.<br></div><div>- <b>Currents</b> always choose the <u>path of least resistance</u>, so the weakest resistor will have the greatest current fllowing through it.</div><div>- This <u>only</u> applies to resistors in parallel, since resistors in series have equal currents flowing across them.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9e49495c9af85d9dd27719a30946094b8045b38b.jpg""></div>"
"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">parallel</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span> <u>resistance</u> will have the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;highest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[smallest/highest]</span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b> output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>largest</i>&nbsp;portion of the current (I)</span> flowing through it."	"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">parallel</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span> <u>resistance</u> will have the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>highest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b> output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>largest</i>&nbsp;portion of the current (I)</span> flowing through it.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Power (P) </b>in terms of resistors refers to the <u>amount of heat dissipated</u>... smaller resistors in parallel dissipate more heat.<br></div><div>- <b>Currents</b> always choose the <u>path of least resistance</u>, so the weakest resistor will have the greatest current fllowing through it.</div><div>- This <u>only</u> applies to resistors in parallel, since resistors in series have equal currents flowing across them.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9e49495c9af85d9dd27719a30946094b8045b38b.jpg""></div>"

"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">parallel</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span> <u>resistance</u> will have the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>highest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b> output since it has the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>largest</i>&nbsp;portion of the current (I)</span> flowing through it.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Power (P) </b>in terms of resistors refers to the <u>amount of heat dissipated</u>... smaller resistors in parallel dissipate more heat.<br></div><div>- <b>Currents</b> always choose the <u>path of least resistance</u>, so the weakest resistor will have the greatest current fllowing through it.</div><div>- This <u>only</u> applies to resistors in parallel, since resistors in series have equal currents flowing across them.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9e49495c9af85d9dd27719a30946094b8045b38b.jpg""></div>"
"For resistors in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""series"" data-ordinal=""1"">[series/parallel]</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>largest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>highest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>highest</i> voltage drop (ΔV)</span>."	"For resistors in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>largest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>highest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>highest</i> voltage drop (ΔV)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Power (P)&nbsp;</b>in terms of resistors refers to the&nbsp;<u>amount of heat dissipated</u>. P = I<sup>2</sup>R → resistors in series have the same current, so ↑R = ↑P.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Currents</b>&nbsp;always choose the&nbsp;<u>path of least resistance</u>. When resistors are in series and have equal currents, the larger resistor dissipates more heat.</div><div>- For resistors in parallel, the smallest resistor has the greatest power output since the majority of the current prefers to take the path of least resistance.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-9e49495c9af85d9dd27719a30946094b8045b38b.jpg""></div></div>"
"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>largest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;highest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[smallest/highest]</span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>highest</i> voltage drop (ΔV)</span>."	"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>largest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>highest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>highest</i> voltage drop (ΔV)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Power (P)&nbsp;</b>in terms of resistors refers to the&nbsp;<u>amount of heat dissipated</u>. P = I<sup>2</sup>R → resistors in series have the same current, so ↑R = ↑P.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Currents</b>&nbsp;always choose the&nbsp;<u>path of least resistance</u>. <div>-&nbsp;<b>Power (P)&nbsp;</b>in terms of resistors refers to the&nbsp;<u>amount of heat dissipated</u>. P = I<sup>2</sup>R → resistors in series have the same current, so ↑R = ↑P.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Currents</b>&nbsp;always choose the&nbsp;<u>path of least resistance</u>. "For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>largest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>highest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>highest</i> voltage drop (ΔV)</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Power (P)&nbsp;</b>in terms of resistors refers to the&nbsp;<u>amount of heat dissipated</u>. P = I<sup>2</sup>R → resistors in series have the same current, so ↑R = ↑P.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Currents</b>&nbsp;always choose the&nbsp;<u>path of least resistance</u>. When resistors are in series and have equal currents, the larger resistor dissipates more heat.</div><div>- For resistors in parallel, the smallest resistor has the greatest power output since the majority of the current prefers to take the path of least resistance.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-9e49495c9af85d9dd27719a30946094b8045b38b.jpg""></div></div>"
"<div>As a <u>sound wave</u> passes from one medium to another, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>frequency</b></span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[does/does not]</span> change.<br></div>"	"<div>As a <u>sound wave</u> passes from one medium to another, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>frequency</b></span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does not</i></span> change.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Its velocity will change depending on the medium, according to the equation for the speed of sound below.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""90fb590216e39b3ddef373ec34676b8d.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-09f322a7b669c981097f55ea6cf42cbb46f011ef.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Beat&#x20;frequencies&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> occur when two <u>sound waves</u> of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i style="""">different</i> frequencies</span> are overlapped - resulting in periods of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">constructive</span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">destructive</span>, and then <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">constructive</span> interference."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Beat frequencies</b></span> occur when two <u>sound waves</u> of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i style="""">different</i> frequencies</span> are overlapped - resulting in periods of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">constructive</span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">destructive</span>, and then <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">constructive</span> interference.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Produces a ""wobble"" sound.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-08d197aac603fd82c539b2d145d8f3b950bf79c6.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-8874241f88fbd7582d6b61e404269dc0e9e0af18.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Beat frequencies</b></span> occur when two <u>sound waves</u> of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;different&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;frequencies"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> are overlapped - resulting in periods of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">constructive</span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">destructive</span>, and then <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">constructive</span> interference."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Beat frequencies</b></span> occur when two <u>sound waves</u> of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i style="""">different</i> frequencies</span> are overlapped - resulting in periods of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">constructive</span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">destructive</span>, and then <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">constructive</span> interference.

"<b>Ultrasounds</b> can <i>only</i> detect the presence of objects with sizes greater than or equal to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""sound&#x20;wave&#x27;s&#x20;&lt;u&gt;wavelength&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;λ&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Ultrasounds</b> can <i>only</i> detect the presence of objects with sizes greater than or equal to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">sound wave's <u>wavelength</u> (λ)</span>.

"<div>Equation for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Poiseuille's law</b></span>, which calculates the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>laminar</u></span> flow of a liquid through a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pipe"" data-ordinal=""4"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><img src=""paste-c3f72dd7b09c60138d631dc135d5110b87242dd2.jpg""></span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Poiseuille's law</b></span>, which calculates the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>laminar</u></span> flow of a liquid through a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4"">pipe</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><img src=""paste-c3f72dd7b09c60138d631dc135d5110b87242dd2.jpg""></span><br></div>

- Q = flow rate (volume flowing per time)<div>- r = tube's radius</div><div>- L = length of the pipe</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-0af1f0d77b0c7947887921c37ce6e9a4efd178b3.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>When an object is in motion, its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;inertia&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is equivalent to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>mass</u></span>.<br></div>"	"<div>When an object is in motion, its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>inertia</b></span> is equivalent to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>mass</u></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Velocity has <u>no effect</u> on the inertia (will affect the momentum, though).</div>- 5 objects (of equal mass) travelling at different speeds will have the <u>same inertia</u>, since they each still have the same mass.</div>"
"For resistors in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""parallel"" data-ordinal=""1"">[series/parallel]</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>largest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;will have the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>smallest</i>&nbsp;portion of the current (I)</span> flowing through it."	"For resistors in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">parallel</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>largest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;will have the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>smallest</i>&nbsp;portion of the current (I)</span> flowing through it.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Power (P)&nbsp;</b>in terms of resistors refers to the&nbsp;<u>amount of heat dissipated</u>... smaller resistors in parallel dissipate more heat.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Currents</b>&nbsp;always choose the&nbsp;<u>path of least resistance</u>, so the weakest resistor will have the greatest current fllowing through it.</div><div>- This&nbsp;<u>only</u>&nbsp;applies to resistors in parallel, since resistors in series have equal currents flowing across them.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9e49495c9af85d9dd27719a30946094b8045b38b.jpg""></div>"
"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">parallel</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>largest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;will have the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;smallest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[smallest/highest]</span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>smallest</i>&nbsp;portion of the current (I)</span> flowing through it."	"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">parallel</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>largest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;will have the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>smallest</i>&nbsp;portion of the current (I)</span> flowing through it.

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Power (P)&nbsp;</b>in terms of resistors refers to the&nbsp;<u>amount of heat dissipated</u>... smaller resistors in parallel dissipate more heat.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Currents</b>&nbsp;always choose the&nbsp;<u>path of least resistance</u>, so the weakest resistor will have the greatest current fllowing through it.</div><div>- This&nbsp;<u>only</u>&nbsp;applies to resistors in parallel, since resistors in series have equal currents flowing across them.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9e49495c9af85d9dd27719a30946094b8045b38b.jpg""></div>"
"For resistors in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""series"" data-ordinal=""1"">[series/parallel]</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>lowest</i>&nbsp;voltage drop (ΔV)</span>."	"For resistors in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>lowest</i>&nbsp;voltage drop (ΔV)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Power (P)&nbsp;</b>in terms of resistors refers to the&nbsp;<u>amount of heat dissipated</u>. P = I<sup>2</sup>R → resistors in series have the same current, so ↑R = ↑P.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Currents</b>&nbsp;always choose the&nbsp;<u>path of least resistance</u>. When resistors are in series and have equal currents, the larger resistor dissipates more heat.</div><div>- For resistors in parallel, the smallest resistor has the greatest power output since the majority of the current prefers to take the path of least resistance.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-9e49495c9af85d9dd27719a30946094b8045b38b.jpg""></div></div>"
"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;smallest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[smallest/highest]</span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>lowest</i>&nbsp;voltage drop (ΔV)</span>."	"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>lowest</i>&nbsp;voltage drop (ΔV)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Power (P)&nbsp;</b>in terms of resistors refers to the&nbsp;<u>amount of heat dissipated</u>. P = I<sup>2</sup>R → resistors in series have the same current, so ↑R = ↑P.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Currents</b>&nbsp;always choose the&nbsp;<u>path of least resistance</u>. "For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;smallest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[smallest/largest]</span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>lowest</i>&nbsp;voltage drop (ΔV)</span>."	"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>lowest</i>&nbsp;voltage drop (ΔV)</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Power (P)&nbsp;</b>in terms of resistors refers to the&nbsp;<u>amount of heat dissipated</u>. P = I<sup>2</sup>R → resistors in series have the same current, so ↑R = ↑P.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Currents</b>&nbsp;always choose the&nbsp;<u>path of least resistance</u>. When resistors are in series and have equal currents, the larger resistor dissipates more heat.</div><div>- For resistors in parallel, the smallest resistor has the greatest power output since the majority of the current prefers to take the path of least resistance.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-9e49495c9af85d9dd27719a30946094b8045b38b.jpg""></div></div>"
"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;lowest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;voltage&#x20;drop&#x20;&#x28;ΔV&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""4"">[...]</span>."	"For resistors in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">series</span>, the resistor with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<u>resistance</u>&nbsp;has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smallest</i></span>&nbsp;<b>power (P)</b>&nbsp;output since it has the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>lowest</i>&nbsp;voltage drop (ΔV)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Power (P)&nbsp;</b>in terms of resistors refers to the&nbsp;<u>amount of heat dissipated</u>. P = I<sup>2</sup>R → resistors in series have the same current, so ↑R = ↑P.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Currents</b>&nbsp;always choose the&nbsp;<u>path of least resistance</u>. When resistors are in series and have equal currents, the larger resistor dissipates more heat.</div><div>- For resistors in parallel, the smallest resistor has the greatest power output since the majority of the current prefers to take the path of least resistance.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-9e49495c9af85d9dd27719a30946094b8045b38b.jpg""></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Brewster&#x27;s&#x20;Law&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[law]</span> describes an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>angle</i> of incident light</span> that both <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reflects</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>refracts</u></span> light off a surface at a 90° angle from each other - <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">reflecting</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>fully</i> polarized light</span> in one direction."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Brewster's Law</b></span> describes an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>angle</i> of incident light</span> that both <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reflects</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>refracts</u></span> light off a surface at a 90° angle from each other - <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">reflecting</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>fully</i> polarized light</span> in one direction.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Angle of the incident light: <b>tan(θ) = n<sub>2</sub>/n<sub>1</sub></b></div><div>- The light that passed through the medium (refracted) was only partially polarized.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-468cbec8b1944078c2166c055ddcd30463fa847c.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Brewster's Law</b></span> describes an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;angle&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;incident&#x20;light"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> that both <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;reflects&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;refracts&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> light off a surface at a 90° angle from each other - <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">reflecting</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>fully</i> polarized light</span> in one direction."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Brewster's Law</b></span> describes an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>angle</i> of incident light</span> that both <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reflects</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>refracts</u></span> light off a surface at a 90° angle from each other - <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">reflecting</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>fully</i> polarized light</span> in one direction.

"<div>In terms of radioactive decay, the <b><i>rate</i> of decay</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>faster</i></span> towards the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;beginning&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[beginning/later]</span> stage of the nucleus' decay than at a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""later"" data-ordinal=""2"">[beginning/later]</span> stage.</div>"	"<div>In terms of radioactive decay, the <b><i>rate</i> of decay</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>faster</i></span> towards the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>beginning</u></span> stage of the nucleus' decay than at a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">later</span> stage.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Aka as time goes on and less nuclei remain, decay will be slower&nbsp;<div><br></div><div>Think: first half life gets rid of half whereas second half life only gets rid of 1/4 of the original total. These half lives are the same amount of time. So decay rate must have been fastest at the beginning since more decayed in an equal amount of time.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e7debbc58abc464a4621a09b214a4a46.png""><br></div></div>"
"In terms of radioactive decay, the&nbsp;<b><i>rate</i>&nbsp;of decay</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;faster&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[slower/faster]</span> towards the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>beginning</u></span> stage of the nucleus' decay than at a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">later</span> stage."	"In terms of radioactive decay, the&nbsp;<b><i>rate</i>&nbsp;of decay</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>faster</i></span> towards the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>beginning</u></span> stage of the nucleus' decay than at a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">later</span> stage.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Aka as time goes on and less nuclei remain, decay will be slower&nbsp;<div><br></div><div>Think: first half life gets rid of half whereas second half life only gets rid of 1/4 of the original total. These half lives are the same amount of time. So decay rate must have been fastest at the beginning since more decayed in an equal amount of time&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e7debbc58abc464a4621a09b214a4a46.png""><br></div></div>"
"In terms of radioactive decay, the&nbsp;<b><i>rate</i>&nbsp;of decay</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>faster</i></span> towards the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;beginning&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[beginning/later]</span> stage of the nucleus' decay than at a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""later"" data-ordinal=""2"">[beginning/later]</span> stage."	"In terms of radioactive decay, the&nbsp;<b><i>rate</i>&nbsp;of decay</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>faster</i></span> towards the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>beginning</u></span> stage of the nucleus' decay than at a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">later</span> stage.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Aka as time goes on and less nuclei remain, decay will be slower&nbsp;<div><br></div><div>Think: first half life gets rid of half whereas second half life only gets rid of 1/4 of the original total. These half lives are the same amount of time. So decay rate must have been fastest at the beginning since more decayed in an equal amount of time&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e7debbc58abc464a4621a09b214a4a46.png""><br></div></div>"
"In terms of radioactive decay, the&nbsp;<b><i>rate</i>&nbsp;of decay</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;slower&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[slower/faster]</span> towards the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>later</u></span> stage of the nucleus' decay than at a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">beginning</span> stage."	"In terms of radioactive decay, the&nbsp;<b><i>rate</i>&nbsp;of decay</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>slower</i></span> towards the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>later</u></span> stage of the nucleus' decay than at a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">beginning</span> stage.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Aka as time goes on and less nuclei remain, decay will be slower&nbsp;<div><br></div><div>Think: first half life gets rid of half whereas second half life only gets rid of 1/4 of the original total. These half lives are the same amount of time. So decay rate must have been fastest at the beginning since more decayed in an equal amount of time&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e7debbc58abc464a4621a09b214a4a46.png""><br></div></div>"
"In terms of radioactive decay, the&nbsp;<b><i>rate</i>&nbsp;of decay</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>slower</i></span> towards the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;later&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[beginning/later]</span> stage of the nucleus' decay than at a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""beginning"" data-ordinal=""2"">[beginning/later]</span> stage."	"In terms of radioactive decay, the&nbsp;<b><i>rate</i>&nbsp;of decay</b>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>slower</i></span> towards the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>later</u></span> stage of the nucleus' decay than at a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">beginning</span> stage.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Aka as time goes on and less nuclei remain, decay will be slower&nbsp;<div><br></div><div>Think: first half life gets rid of half whereas second half life only gets rid of 1/4 of the original total. These half lives are the same amount of time. So decay rate must have been fastest at the beginning since more decayed in an equal amount of time&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e7debbc58abc464a4621a09b214a4a46.png""><br></div></div>"
"As the strength of an <u>electric field (E)</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> between the plates of a&nbsp;<b>capacitor</b>, the capacitance (C) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[increases/decreases]</span>."	"As the strength of an <u>electric field (E)</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> between the plates of a&nbsp;<b>capacitor</b>, the capacitance (C) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is because capacitance is inversely related to voltage, and voltage is directly proportional to the electric field (E = ΔV/d).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-34f8957cc24ce906b582457cf244152d1a8189fa.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"As the strength of an <u>electric field (E)</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span> between the plates of a&nbsp;<b>capacitor</b>, the capacitance (C) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span>."	"As the strength of an <u>electric field (E)</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> between the plates of a&nbsp;<b>capacitor</b>, the capacitance (C) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span>.

"As the strength of an <u>electric field (E)</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreases</i></span> between the plates of a&nbsp;<b>capacitor</b>, the capacitance (C) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[increases/decreases]</span>."	"As the strength of an <u>electric field (E)</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreases</i></span> between the plates of a&nbsp;<b>capacitor</b>, the capacitance (C) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is because capacitance is inversely related to voltage, and voltage is directly proportional to the electric field.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-34f8957cc24ce906b582457cf244152d1a8189fa.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>A nucleus with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <b>binding energy</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[less/more]</span> <u>stable</u>, since a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[smaller/higher]</span> amount of energy is needed to separate its nuclear components.</div>"	"<div>A nucleus with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <b>binding energy</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>more</i></span> <u>stable</u>, since a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> amount of energy is needed to separate its nuclear components.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>E</b> = binding energy needed to hold together the nuclear components; <b>m</b> = mass defect; <b>c</b> = speed of light.</div><div>- The <u>mass defect</u> represents the amount of mass ""missing"" from a real atom versus its theoretical, calculated weight. This mass is actually converted into energy to hold the nucleus together.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""dbc75c411fc548e419b09b77bbdc2f51.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-9de4dcb952f95b5ee3374687e24cc516b8be0215.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>A nucleus with a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[low/high]</span> <b>binding energy</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>more</i></span> <u>stable</u>, since a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> amount of energy is needed to separate its nuclear components.</div>"	"<div>A nucleus with a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <b>binding energy</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>more</i></span> <u>stable</u>, since a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> amount of energy is needed to separate its nuclear components.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"<div>A nucleus with a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[low/high]</span> <b>binding energy</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span> <u>stable</u>, since a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smaller</i></span> amount of energy is capable of separating its nuclear components.</div>"	"<div>A nucleus with a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> <b>binding energy</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span> <u>stable</u>, since a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smaller</i></span> amount of energy is capable of separating its nuclear components.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>E</b> = binding energy needed to hold together the nuclear components; <b>m</b> = mass defect; <b>c</b> = speed of light.</div><div>- The <u>mass defect</u> represents the amount of mass ""missing"" from a real atom versus its theoretical, calculated weight. This mass is actually converted into energy to hold the nucleus together.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""dbc75c411fc548e419b09b77bbdc2f51.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-9de4dcb952f95b5ee3374687e24cc516b8be0215.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>A <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> sign for a system's <b>internal energy (ΔU)</b> signifies that its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u> is <i>increasing</i></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-05c555c58cc0dae697b20f69b60c578a6d9959c9.jpg"">"	"<div>A <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>positive</i></span> sign for a system's <b>internal energy (ΔU)</b> signifies that its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u> is <i>increasing</i></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-05c555c58cc0dae697b20f69b60c578a6d9959c9.jpg"">

Having a big pot of lukewarm water has higher thermal energy than a small pot of hot water.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-0b6df5ac3c3d18281d42e2ca1a7fc9199b9164ff.jpg""><br></div></div>"

"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>thermal energy</b></span> of a material takes into account the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>amount</i> of substance</span> when describing its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Having a big pot of lukewarm water has higher thermal energy than a small pot of hot water.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-0b6df5ac3c3d18281d42e2ca1a7fc9199b9164ff.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"A <i>large</i> pot of lukewarm water has <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[less/more]</span> <b>thermal energy</b> than a <i>small</i> pot of hot water."	"A <i>large</i> pot of lukewarm water has <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <b>thermal energy</b> than a <i>small</i> pot of hot water.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>Thermal energy</b> is different from temperature in the sense that it takes the <u>amount of the substance</u> into account.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-0b6df5ac3c3d18281d42e2ca1a7fc9199b9164ff.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Dipole&#x20;moments&#x20;&#x28;p&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> can be <i>measured</i> in the units <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>debyes</u> (C*m)</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Dipole moments (p)</b></span> can be <i>measured</i> in the units <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>debyes</u> (C*m)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""274502475240d334c65296d8b0125da9.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-fc3cd2f3ac441ac1e139678e765a3a3abedc8bb3.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Dipole moments (p)</b></span> can be <i>measured</i> in the units <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;debyes&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;C&#x2A;m&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[units]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Dipole moments (p)</b></span> can be <i>measured</i> in the units <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>debyes</u> (C*m)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""274502475240d334c65296d8b0125da9.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-fc3cd2f3ac441ac1e139678e765a3a3abedc8bb3.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Nodes and antinodes are <i>only</i> found in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""standing"" data-ordinal=""1"">[standing/traveling]</span> waves, while troughs and crests are <i>only </i>found in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""traveling"" data-ordinal=""1"">[standing/traveling]</span> waves."	"Nodes and antinodes are <i>only</i> found in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">standing</span> waves, while troughs and crests are <i>only </i>found in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">traveling</span> waves.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Nodes and antinodes are&nbsp;<u>only found in standing waves</u>&nbsp;- the analogous points on travelling waves are called crests and troughs.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""674f100155a53630ef29fab436439068.png""></div></div>"
"Average <b>velocities</b> (m/s) of <u>light</u> &amp; <u>sound waves</u> in air...<div><br></div><div>Light: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""3&#x20;x&#x20;10&lt;sup&gt;8&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;m/s</div><div>Sound: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""342"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> m/s</div>"	"Average <b>velocities</b> (m/s) of <u>light</u> &amp; <u>sound waves</u> in air...<div><br></div><div>Light: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">3 x 10<sup>8</sup></span>&nbsp;m/s</div><div>Sound: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">342</span> m/s</div>

"<div>On a displacement vs. time graph, <i>one</i>&nbsp;<b>harmonic</b>&nbsp;in a <u>sound wave</u>&nbsp;can be <i>visualized</i> as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""one&#x20;complete&#x20;cycle"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> along the graph.</div>"	"<div>On a displacement vs. time graph, <i>one</i>&nbsp;<b>harmonic</b>&nbsp;in a <u>sound wave</u>&nbsp;can be <i>visualized</i> as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">one complete cycle</span> along the graph.</div>

<div>The solid black line shows 1 harmonic, the longer dashed line shows 2 harmonics, and the small dashed line shows 3 harmonics.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-f06a4fe4663b2a196ace42ac5a3faa86590e3cc6.jpg""></div>"
"<div>The <i>intersection</i> of these 3 <b>sound waves</b> are considered <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;out&#x20;of&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[in/out of]</span>-<u>phase</u> with each other, since they're <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""traveling&#x20;in&#x20;different&#x20;&lt;i&gt;directions&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-890a1b23621ad4f4c070c35a32290b6e19140872.jpg"">"	"<div>The <i>intersection</i> of these 3 <b>sound waves</b> are considered <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>out of</u></span>-<u>phase</u> with each other, since they're <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">traveling in different <i>directions</i></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-890a1b23621ad4f4c070c35a32290b6e19140872.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Sound waves must be travelling in the same direction (as well as being able to add constructively) to be considered in-phase.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-c83b45a1791a254ba29166840f55adb72c107f9b.jpg""></div>"
"In order for 2 <u>sound waves</u> to be considered <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;in&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[in/out of]</span>&nbsp;<b>phase</b>, they must...<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Have a 0° phase difference.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Travel in the same direction.</span></div>"	"In order for 2 <u>sound waves</u> to be considered <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>in</b></span>&nbsp;<b>phase</b>, they must...<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Have a 0° phase difference.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Travel in the same direction.</span></div>

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-c83b45a1791a254ba29166840f55adb72c107f9b.jpg""></div>"
"Are the frequency differences between these <b>doppler shift</b> examples equivalent? Why?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""No&#x2C;&#x20;because&#x20;this&#x20;would&#x20;result&#x20;in&#x20;an&#x20;even&#x20;larger&#x20;frequency&#x20;shift&#x20;than&#x20;the&#x20;original&#x2E;&#x20;Their&#x20;relative&#x20;velocities&#x20;would&#x20;be&#x20;larger&#x20;than&#x20;the&#x20;original&#x2C;&#x20;since&#x20;they&#x27;re&#x20;both&#x20;rapidly&#x20;moving&#x20;away&#x20;from&#x20;each&#x20;other&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><div>- Stationary man and a plane travelling at 300 m/s away from him.</div><div>- Man moving 300 m/s in the opposite direction of a plane moving 600 m/s away from him.</div>"	"Are the frequency differences between these <b>doppler shift</b> examples equivalent? Why?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">No, because this would result in an even larger frequency shift than the original. Their relative velocities would be larger than the original, since they're both rapidly moving away from each other.</span></div><div><br></div><div>- Stationary man and a plane travelling at 300 m/s away from him.</div><div>- Man moving 300 m/s in the opposite direction of a plane moving 600 m/s away from him.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>- Δf&nbsp;</b>= frequency shift;&nbsp;<b>f</b>&nbsp;= original frequency;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= velocity between the source &amp; observer;&nbsp;<b>c</b>&nbsp;= speed of the wave in the medium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c82370dbcc23ea6731f5157a0e77d1e946201b66.jpg""></div></div>"
"Are the frequency differences between these <b>doppler shift</b> examples equivalent? Why?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Yes&#x2C;&#x20;because&#x20;they&#x27;re&#x20;still&#x20;travelling&#x20;in&#x20;the&#x20;same&#x20;direction&#x20;&#x2D;&#x20;there&#x27;s&#x20;still&#x20;only&#x20;a&#x20;300&#x20;m&#x2F;s&#x20;difference&#x20;between&#x20;their&#x20;relative&#x20;velocities&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><div>- Stationary man and a plane travelling at 300 m/s away from him.</div><div>- Man moving 300 m/s in the same direction as a plane moving 600 m/s away from him.</div>"	"Are the frequency differences between these <b>doppler shift</b> examples equivalent? Why?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Yes, because they're still travelling in the same direction - there's still only a 300 m/s difference between their relative velocities.</span></div><div><br></div><div>- Stationary man and a plane travelling at 300 m/s away from him.</div><div>- Man moving 300 m/s in the same direction as a plane moving 600 m/s away from him.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>- Δf&nbsp;</b>= frequency shift;&nbsp;<b>f</b>&nbsp;= original frequency;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= velocity between the source &amp; observer;&nbsp;<b>c</b>&nbsp;= speed of the wave in the medium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c82370dbcc23ea6731f5157a0e77d1e946201b66.jpg""></div></div>"
"Due to the <b>doppler effect</b>, a sound source moving <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;away&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[towards/away]</span> from you with an&nbsp;<i>increasing</i> velocity produces a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>longer</i></span> <u>wavelength (λ)</u>."	"Due to the <b>doppler effect</b>, a sound source moving <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>away</i></span> from you with an&nbsp;<i>increasing</i> velocity produces a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>longer</i></span> <u>wavelength (λ)</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Sound wave is ""compressed"" (↓λ, ↑f)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>f'</b>&nbsp;= observer's perceived frequency;&nbsp;<b>f</b>&nbsp;= real frequency;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= speed of sound (343 m/s in 20°C);&nbsp;<b>v<sub>D</sub></b>&nbsp;= detector's (observer's) speed;&nbsp;<b>v<sub>S</sub></b>&nbsp;= speed of moving source.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-972e257dfae45d0aa145163f5fb4deba7cd343c0.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c82370dbcc23ea6731f5157a0e77d1e946201b66.jpg""></div></div>"
"Due to the <b>doppler effect</b>, a sound source moving <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>away</i></span> from you with an&nbsp;<i>increasing</i> velocity produces a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;longer&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[shorter/longer]</span> <u>wavelength (λ)</u>."	"Due to the <b>doppler effect</b>, a sound source moving <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>away</i></span> from you with an&nbsp;<i>increasing</i> velocity produces a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>longer</i></span> <u>wavelength (λ)</u>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Sound wave is ""compressed"" (↓λ, ↑f)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>f'</b>&nbsp;= observer's perceived frequency;&nbsp;<b>f</b>&nbsp;= real frequency;&nbsp;<b>v</b>&nbsp;= speed of sound (343 m/s in 20°C);&nbsp;<b>v<sub>D</sub></b>&nbsp;= detector's (observer's) speed;&nbsp;<b>v<sub>S</sub></b>&nbsp;= speed of moving source.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-972e257dfae45d0aa145163f5fb4deba7cd343c0.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c82370dbcc23ea6731f5157a0e77d1e946201b66.jpg""></div></div>"

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Hydrostatic&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the pressure that is exerted by the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>weight of the fluid molecules</u></span> <i>above</i> some arbitrary point in a fluid."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hydrostatic pressure</b></span> is the pressure that is exerted by the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>weight of the fluid molecules</u></span> <i>above</i> some arbitrary point in a fluid.

"According to Newton's 3<sup>rd</sup> law, <b>gravity</b> and <b>normal force (F<sub>N</sub>)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;do&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[do/do not]</span> form typical <u>action-reaction pairs</u>, since they are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""forces&#x20;exerted&#x20;by&#x20;&lt;i&gt;different&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;objects"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"According to Newton's 3<sup>rd</sup> law, <b>gravity</b> and <b>normal force (F<sub>N</sub>)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>do not</i></span> form typical <u>action-reaction pairs</u>, since they are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">forces exerted by <i>different</i> objects</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Force of gravity → generated by the earth.</div><div>- Normal force → generated by the surface an object is resting on.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-bc048ee068efae8b6944702b1996bec6b50722e5.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-88a866637b2bf3c870443bbd77ad3472cd668bcc.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Newton's 3<sup>rd</sup> law</b> <i>only</i> describes the action-reaction pair of forces exerted by and on <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;one&#x20;&lt;i&gt;single&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;entity&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;i&#x2E;e&#x2E;&#x20;just&#x20;the&#x20;table&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Newton's 3<sup>rd</sup> law</b> <i>only</i> describes the action-reaction pair of forces exerted by and on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>one <i>single</i> entity</u> (i.e. just the table)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-9f11b2a219a939a514eb64dec2f53837c3c547f0.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div>Even though gravity exerts a force on a book resting on a table, and that table exerts a force on that book, these are not action-reaction pairs since it's describing the forces from two separate entities (earth &amp; table).</div>"
"<div>Difference between the uses of these equations...</div><div><br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>[A]<sub>t</sub> = [A]<sub style="""">0</sub>e<sup style="""">-</sup><sup>λ</sup><sup style="""">t</sup></b>: used to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""left&#x2D;over&#x20;&lt;u&gt;concentration&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&lt;i&gt;after&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;decay"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div>- <b>Δn/t = -λn</b>: used to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;rate&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;decay"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""e341f4b11043b7942de48db6cbeff2ca.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""99af145d4bbc4bf8b8c3c8e775138c49.png""><br></div>"	"<div>Difference between the uses of these equations...</div><div><br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>[A]<sub>t</sub> = [A]<sub style="""">0</sub>e<sup style="""">-</sup><sup>λ</sup><sup style="""">t</sup></b>: used to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">left-over <u>concentration</u> <i>after</i> decay</span>.</div><div>- <b>Δn/t = -λn</b>: used to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>rate</u> of decay</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""e341f4b11043b7942de48db6cbeff2ca.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""99af145d4bbc4bf8b8c3c8e775138c49.png""><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<div>Difference between the uses of these equations...</div><div><br></div><div>- <b>Δn/t = -λn</b>: used to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;rate&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;decay"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>[A]<sub>t</sub> = [A]<sub>0</sub>e<sup>-</sup><sup>λ</sup><sup>t</sup></b>: used to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""left&#x2D;over&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;u&gt;concentration&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;i&gt;after&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;decay"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""e341f4b11043b7942de48db6cbeff2ca.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""99af145d4bbc4bf8b8c3c8e775138c49.png""><br></div>"	"<div>Difference between the uses of these equations...</div><div><br></div><div>- <b>Δn/t = -λn</b>: used to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>rate</u> of decay</span>.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>[A]<sub>t</sub> = [A]<sub>0</sub>e<sup>-</sup><sup>λ</sup><sup>t</sup></b>: used to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">left-over&nbsp;<u>concentration</u>&nbsp;<i>after</i>&nbsp;decay</span>.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""e341f4b11043b7942de48db6cbeff2ca.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""99af145d4bbc4bf8b8c3c8e775138c49.png""><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"In the equation for <b>specific gravity</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;density&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;of&#x20;water&#x20;&#x28;ρ&lt;sub&gt;water&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1000</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">kg/m<sup>3</sup></span> at 4°C."	"In the equation for <b>specific gravity</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>density </i>of water (ρ<sub>water</sub>)</span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1000</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">kg/m<sup>3</sup></span> at 4°C.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Compares the density of the material (ρ) to water's density (ρ<sub>water</sub>).<div>- Value greater than 1 (material's ρ is larger than water) means it sinks.<br><div><br></div><div><img src=""095b9246bf2c636849c6fd163144d69c.png""><br></div></div></div>"
"As an object is submerged <i>deeper</i> in a body of water, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>hydrostatic pressure</b></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[increases/decreases]</span> in a linear fashion."	"As an object is submerged <i>deeper</i> in a body of water, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>hydrostatic pressure</b></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> in a linear fashion.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Evident by the equation <b>P<sub>hydrostatic</sub></b> = ρgΔh.</div><div>- <b>Hydrostatic pressure</b> is the <u>weight of the fluid molecules</u> above some arbitrary point in a body of water.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-eb021bc5d1dc3b9c9e526925e945808a30a28e7d.jpg""></div>"
"As an object is submerged <i>deeper</i> in a body of water, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;hydrostatic&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> in a linear fashion."	"As an object is submerged <i>deeper</i> in a body of water, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>hydrostatic pressure</b></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> in a linear fashion.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Evident by the equation <b>P<sub>hydrostatic</sub></b> = ρgΔh.</div><div>- <b>Hydrostatic pressure</b> is the <u>weight of the fluid molecules</u> above some arbitrary point in a body of water.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-eb021bc5d1dc3b9c9e526925e945808a30a28e7d.jpg""></div>"
"Shortcut to calculate the <u>fraction of an original sample&nbsp;<i>remaining</i></u>&nbsp;after <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x27;n&#x27;&#x20;number&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;b&gt;half&#x2D;lives&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">(1/2)<sup>n </sup>= fraction remaining</span><br></div>"	"Shortcut to calculate the <u>fraction of an original sample&nbsp;<i>remaining</i></u>&nbsp;after <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">'n' number of <b>half-lives</b></span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">(1/2)<sup>n </sup>= fraction remaining</span><br></div>

"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;mechanical&#x20;advantage&#x20;&#x28;MA&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;inclined&#x20;plane&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">MA = (hypotenuse)/(height)</span></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>mechanical advantage (MA)</b></span> of an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>inclined plane</u></span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">MA = (hypotenuse)/(height)</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>Mechanical advantage</b> represents the reduction in force you'd need to put into a system to accomplish some amount of work.<div>- The equation for mechanical advantage is F<sub>out</sub>/F<sub>in</sub>.<br><div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7572860cb671571ec50fe98084dc1844752dc93e.jpg""><br></div></div></div></div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>mechanical advantage (MA)</b></span> of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>inclined plane</u></span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""MA&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;&#x28;hypotenuse&#x29;&#x2F;&#x28;height&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>mechanical advantage (MA)</b></span> of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>inclined plane</u></span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">MA = (hypotenuse)/(height)</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>Mechanical advantage</b> represents the reduction in force you'd need to put into a system to accomplish some amount of work.<div>- The equation for mechanical advantage is F<sub>out</sub>/F<sub>in</sub>.<br><div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7572860cb671571ec50fe98084dc1844752dc93e.jpg""><br></div></div></div></div>"
"A <i>light</i>&nbsp;object on an <b>inclined plane</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;equally&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;as"" data-ordinal=""2"">[less/more/equally]</span> likely as a <i>heavy</i>&nbsp;object to slide down the plane."	"A <i>light</i>&nbsp;object on an <b>inclined plane</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>equally</i> as</span> likely as a <i>heavy</i>&nbsp;object to slide down the plane.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>F<sub>net</sub> = (mgsinθ) - (µ<sub>s</sub>F<sub>N</sub>) → ma = (mgsinθ) - (µ<sub>s</sub>mg) → <u>mass cancels out</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-084c046a5fa09f5e5793f56833d5c8d4bbfb3b85.jpg""></div>"
"Equation to find the <u>factor</u> by which something was <i>increased</i>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x28;factor&#x20;increase&#x29;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;&#x28;decimal&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;i&gt;new&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x29;&#x20;&#x2F;&#x20;&#x28;decimal&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;i&gt;old&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"Equation to find the <u>factor</u> by which something was <i>increased</i>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">(factor increase) = (decimal of <i>new</i>) / (decimal of <i>old</i>)</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

What's the factor increase from (1/100,000) → (1/5)?<div><br></div><div>(factor increase) = (1/5)/(1/100,000) → (factor increase) = (0.2)/(1 x 10<sup>-5</sup>) → <b>20,000</b></div></div>"

<div>- The <u>greater the 's' character</u> of an atom, the harder it is to eject an electron close to its nucleus. Keep in mind π-electrons are located in orbits further away from the nuclei of the atoms.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""7531b9cbf1e71721d78a8ddd0b1bf92f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""Photoelectric-Effect.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>greater</i></span> the degree of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>s-character</b></span> in an atom, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;harder&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[easier/harder]</span> it is to eject an electron."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>greater</i></span> the degree of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>s-character</b></span> in an atom, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>harder</i></span> it is to eject an electron.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The ""left-over"" energy from the photon is imparted as kinetic energy to the electron.</div><div>- (E=hf) is the equation for the amount of energy carried by a photon of light... (E=hf<sub>T</sub>) represents the minimum amount of energy needed for the electron to get ejected (threshold).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""7531b9cbf1e71721d78a8ddd0b1bf92f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""Photoelectric-Effect.jpg""></div>"

"It requires <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> energy to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">eject</span> an <u>electron</u> from an atom than it does to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">excite</span> it.

<div>An object dropped from a height with a potential energy of 15 J will compress a perfectly elastic spring with exactly 15 J of energy.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-d8eb8faba35c7e7ca48e34f2f2bf9ff8942797b0.jpg""></div>"
"In a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>imperfectly</i></span> <b>elastic spring</b>, <u>potential energy</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;is&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[is/is not]</span>&nbsp;perfectly conserved - potential energy will always be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""slightly&#x20;less"" data-ordinal=""2"">[magnitude difference]</span> than expected."	"In a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>imperfectly</i></span> <b>elastic spring</b>, <u>potential energy</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is not</i></span>&nbsp;perfectly conserved - potential energy will always be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">slightly less</span> than expected.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>An object dropped from a height with a potential energy of 15 J will compress an imperfectly elastic spring with less than 15 J (~13 J) of energy.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-d8eb8faba35c7e7ca48e34f2f2bf9ff8942797b0.jpg""></div>"
"In a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;imperfectly&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>elastic spring</b>, <u>potential energy</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is not</i></span>&nbsp;perfectly conserved - potential energy will always be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">slightly less</span> than expected."	"In a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>imperfectly</i></span> <b>elastic spring</b>, <u>potential energy</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is not</i></span>&nbsp;perfectly conserved - potential energy will always be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">slightly less</span> than expected.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>Impulse</b> just represents the change in momentum over time (i.e. the amount of force applied to an object for a certain amount of time).&nbsp;<div>- <b>Δp</b> = change in momentum; <b>F</b> = force applied; <b>Δt</b> = time of contact.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""hqdefault-253b9a12038f925fc71a39a099badf288cc419da.jpg""><br></div></div>"
