"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Presbyopia</b></span> is age-related <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>far</u></span><u>sightedness</u> caused by a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""hardening&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;lens"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> - resulting in a projection of the image <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>behind</i></span> the retina."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Presbyopia</b></span> is age-related <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>far</u></span><u>sightedness</u> caused by a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">hardening of the lens</span> - resulting in a projection of the image <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>behind</i></span> the retina.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Hardening of the lens makes it harder to regulate the focal power.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Lens power (P)</b>&nbsp;is the reciprocal of the focal length (f), since it represents the ability of the lens to converge light at a point.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Converging</b>&nbsp;glasses help&nbsp;<u>offset</u>&nbsp;the weakness of the lens - allowing it to focus on the retina.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-153dc861cc4fde84a067591005c8255fbecaeba0.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-052808b4ccf4a5fd1049721eb517de4b659ac145.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-2ad048560a86a01cb77c9ef93f0f2e02101d7489.jpg""></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Presbyopia</b></span> is age-related <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;far&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><u>sightedness</u> caused by a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">hardening of the lens</span> - resulting in a projection of the image <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;behind&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[in front of/behind]</span> the retina."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Presbyopia</b></span> is age-related <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>far</u></span><u>sightedness</u> caused by a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">hardening of the lens</span> - resulting in a projection of the image <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>behind</i></span> the retina.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Hardening of the lens makes it harder to regulate the focal power.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Lens power (P)</b>&nbsp;is the reciprocal of the focal length (f), since it represents the ability of the lens to converge light at a point.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Converging</b>&nbsp;glasses help&nbsp;<u>offset</u>&nbsp;the weakness of the lens - allowing it to focus on the retina.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-153dc861cc4fde84a067591005c8255fbecaeba0.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-052808b4ccf4a5fd1049721eb517de4b659ac145.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-2ad048560a86a01cb77c9ef93f0f2e02101d7489.jpg""></div></div>"

"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Presbyopia</b></span> is age-related <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>far</u></span><u>sightedness</u> caused by a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">hardening of the lens</span> - resulting in a projection of the image <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>behind</i></span> the retina.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Hardening of the lens makes it harder to regulate the focal power.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Lens power (P)</b>&nbsp;is the reciprocal of the focal length (f), since it represents the ability of the lens to converge light at a point.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Converging</b>&nbsp;glasses help&nbsp;<u>offset</u>&nbsp;the weakness of the lens - allowing it to focus on the retina.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-153dc861cc4fde84a067591005c8255fbecaeba0.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-052808b4ccf4a5fd1049721eb517de4b659ac145.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-2ad048560a86a01cb77c9ef93f0f2e02101d7489.jpg""></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Elastic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[elastic/inelastic]</span> <b>collisions</b> are collisions where <u>energy</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span> conserved &amp; <u>momentum</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span>&nbsp;conserved - no heat is lost to the surroundings."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Elastic</b></span> <b>collisions</b> are collisions where <u>energy</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span> conserved &amp; <u>momentum</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span>&nbsp;conserved - no heat is lost to the surroundings.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Momentum (mv)</b> is conserved in <u>both</u> elastic &amp; inelastic collisions, but kinetic energy is not.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-50740ee807aec7683566a2c68609238b0d6a3229.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Elastic</b></span> <b>collisions</b> are collisions where <u>energy</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;is&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[is/is not]</span> conserved &amp; <u>momentum</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;is&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[is/is not]</span>&nbsp;conserved - no heat is lost to the surroundings."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Elastic</b></span> <b>collisions</b> are collisions where <u>energy</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span> conserved &amp; <u>momentum</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span>&nbsp;conserved - no heat is lost to the surroundings.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Momentum (mv)</b> is conserved in <u>both</u> elastic &amp; inelastic collisions, but kinetic energy is not.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-50740ee807aec7683566a2c68609238b0d6a3229.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Inelastic</b></span> <b>collisions</b> are collisions where <u>energy</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;is&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[is/is not]</span> conserved &amp; <u>momentum</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;is&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[is/is not]</span>&nbsp;conserved - heat <i>is</i> lost to the surroundings."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Inelastic</b></span> <b>collisions</b> are collisions where <u>energy</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is not</i></span> conserved &amp; <u>momentum</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span>&nbsp;conserved - heat <i>is</i> lost to the surroundings.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Momentum (mv)</b> is conserved in <u>both</u> elastic &amp; inelastic collisions, but kinetic energy is not.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-50740ee807aec7683566a2c68609238b0d6a3229.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Inelastic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[elastic/inelastic]</span> <b>collisions</b> are collisions where <u>energy</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is not</i></span> conserved &amp; <u>momentum</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span>&nbsp;conserved - heat <i>is</i> lost to the surroundings."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Inelastic</b></span> <b>collisions</b> are collisions where <u>energy</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is not</i></span> conserved &amp; <u>momentum</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span>&nbsp;conserved - heat <i>is</i> lost to the surroundings.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Momentum (mv)</b> is conserved in <u>both</u> elastic &amp; inelastic collisions, but kinetic energy is not.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-50740ee807aec7683566a2c68609238b0d6a3229.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">distance</span> between two <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;connected&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[isolated/connected]</span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does</i></span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates.</div>"	"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">distance</span> between two <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>connected</i></span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does</i></span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The electric field will change, since the voltage would be the same (same battery supplying both) but the distance would change.</div><div>- Connected together → E changes with distance (since voltage is held constant).</div><div>- Isolated from each other → E does not change with distance, since voltage will also change.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""></div></div>"
"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""distance"" data-ordinal=""4"">[...]</span> between two <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>connected</i></span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does</i></span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates.</div>"	"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4"">distance</span> between two <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>connected</i></span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does</i></span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The electric field will change, since the voltage would be the same (same battery supplying both) but the distance would change.</div><div>- Connected together → E changes with distance (since voltage is held constant).</div><div>- Isolated from each other → E does not change with distance, since voltage will also change.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""></div></div>"
"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">distance</span> between two <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>connected</i></span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[does/does not]</span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates.</div>"	"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">distance</span> between two <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>connected</i></span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does</i></span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The electric field will change, since the voltage would be the same (same battery supplying both) but the distance would change.</div><div>- Connected together → E changes with distance (since voltage is held constant).</div><div>- Isolated from each other → E does not change with distance, since voltage will also change.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""></div></div>"
"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">distance</span> between two <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>isolated</i></span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[does/does not]</span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates, since the plates would not be connected to one central voltage source.</div>"	"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">distance</span> between two <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>isolated</i></span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does not</i></span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates, since the plates would not be connected to one central voltage source.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The electric field will change, since the voltage would be the same (same battery supplying both) but the distance would change.</div><div>- Connected together → E changes with distance (since voltage is held constant).</div><div>- Isolated from each other → E does not change with distance, since voltage will also change.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">distance</span> between two <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;isolated&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[isolated/connected]</span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does not</i></span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates, since the plates would not be connected to one central voltage source.</div>"	"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">distance</span> between two <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>isolated</i></span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does not</i></span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates, since the plates would not be connected to one central voltage source.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The electric field will change, since the voltage would be the same (same battery supplying both) but the distance would change.</div><div>- Connected together → E changes with distance (since voltage is held constant).</div><div>- Isolated from each other → E does not change with distance, since voltage will also change.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""distance"" data-ordinal=""4"">[...]</span> between two <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>isolated</i></span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does not</i></span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates, since the plates would not be connected to one central voltage source.</div>"	"<div>Changing the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4"">distance</span> between two <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>isolated</i></span> plates of a <b>capacitor</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does not</i></span> change the <u>electric field (E)</u>&nbsp;between the plates, since the plates would not be connected to one central voltage source.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The electric field will change, since the voltage would be the same (same battery supplying both) but the distance would change.</div><div>- Connected together → E changes with distance (since voltage is held constant).</div><div>- Isolated from each other → E does not change with distance, since voltage will also change.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>doubles</i></span> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;isolated&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;from"" data-ordinal=""2"">[connected/isolated]</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>"	"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>doubles</i></span> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i> from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because according to the E = ΔV/d equation, voltage is directly proportional to distance. Doubling distance cuts the capacitance in half but doubles the voltage. Since voltage is being squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Connected plates</u> → doubling distance cuts energy in half, since capacitance is halved but voltage is unchanged (since both plates are connected to the same battery source regardless).</div><div>- <u>Isolated plates</u> → doubling distance doubles the voltage but decreases capacitance. Since voltage is squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7bf590021238fd8bde556e627ccdc4c192315e6b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;doubles&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[halves/doubles]</span> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i> from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>"	"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>doubles</i></span> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i> from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because according to the E = ΔV/d equation, voltage is directly proportional to distance. Doubling distance cuts the capacitance in half but doubles the voltage. Since voltage is being squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Connected plates</u> → doubling distance cuts energy in half, since capacitance is halved but voltage is unchanged (since both plates are connected to the same battery source regardless).</div><div>- <u>Isolated plates</u> → doubling distance doubles the voltage but decreases capacitance. Since voltage is squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7bf590021238fd8bde556e627ccdc4c192315e6b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>doubles</i></span> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i> from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;halve&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[change]</span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increase&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;by&#x20;a&#x20;factor&#x20;of&#x20;4"" data-ordinal=""3"">[change]</span>.</div>"	"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>doubles</i></span> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i> from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because according to the E = ΔV/d equation, voltage is directly proportional to distance. Doubling distance cuts the capacitance in half but doubles the voltage. Since voltage is being squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Connected plates</u> → doubling distance cuts energy in half, since capacitance is halved but voltage is unchanged (since both plates are connected to the same battery source regardless).</div><div>- <u>Isolated plates</u> → doubling distance doubles the voltage but decreases capacitance. Since voltage is squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7bf590021238fd8bde556e627ccdc4c192315e6b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>halves</i></span> its <u>stored energy (PE)</u> when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>connected </i>to</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;halve&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""remain&#x20;constant"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>halves</i></span> its <u>stored energy (PE)</u> when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>connected </i>to</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">remain constant</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because according to the E = ΔV/d equation, voltage is directly proportional to distance. Doubling distance cuts the capacitance in half but doubles the voltage. Since voltage is being squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Connected plates</u> → doubling distance cuts energy in half, since capacitance is halved but voltage is unchanged (since both plates are connected to the same battery source regardless).</div><div>- <u>Isolated plates</u> → doubling distance doubles the voltage but decreases capacitance. "<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;halves&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[halves/doubles]</span> its <u>stored energy (PE)</u> when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>connected </i>to</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">remain constant</span>.</div>"	"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>halves</i></span> its <u>stored energy (PE)</u> when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>connected </i>to</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">remain constant</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because according to the E = ΔV/d equation, voltage is directly proportional to distance. Doubling distance cuts the capacitance in half but doubles the voltage. Since voltage is being squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Connected plates</u> → doubling distance cuts energy in half, since capacitance is halved but voltage is unchanged (since both plates are connected to the same battery source regardless).</div><div>- <u>Isolated plates</u> → doubling distance doubles the voltage but decreases capacitance. Since voltage is squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7bf590021238fd8bde556e627ccdc4c192315e6b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>halves</i></span> its <u>stored energy (PE)</u> when the plates are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;connected&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;to"" data-ordinal=""2"">[connected/isolated]</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">remain constant</span>.</div>"	"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>halves</i></span> its <u>stored energy (PE)</u> when the plates are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>connected </i>to</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">remain constant</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because according to the E = ΔV/d equation, voltage is directly proportional to distance. Doubling distance cuts the capacitance in half but doubles the voltage. Since voltage is being squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Connected plates</u> → doubling distance cuts energy in half, since capacitance is halved but voltage is unchanged (since both plates are connected to the same battery source regardless).</div><div>- <u>Isolated plates</u> → doubling distance doubles the voltage but decreases capacitance. Since voltage is squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7bf590021238fd8bde556e627ccdc4c192315e6b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Halving the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;doubles&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[halves/doubles]</span> its <u>stored energy (PE)</u> when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>connected </i>to</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>double</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">remain constant</span>.</div>"	"<div>Halving the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>doubles</i></span> its <u>stored energy (PE)</u> when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>connected </i>to</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>double</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">remain constant</span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because according to the E = ΔV/d equation, voltage is directly proportional to distance. Halving distance doubles the capacitance but voltage remains constant (since both plates are connected to the same battery).</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Connected plates</u> → doubling distance cuts energy in half, since capacitance is halved but voltage is unchanged (since both plates are connected to the same battery source regardless).</div><div>- <u>Isolated plates</u> → doubling distance doubles the voltage but decreases capacitance. Since voltage is squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7bf590021238fd8bde556e627ccdc4c192315e6b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because according to the E = ΔV/d equation, voltage is directly proportional to distance. Halving distance doubles the capacitance but voltage remains constant (since both plates are connected to the same battery).</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Connected plates</u> → doubling distance cuts energy in half, since capacitance is halved but voltage is unchanged (since both plates are connected to the same battery source regardless).</div><div>- <u>Isolated plates</u> → doubling distance doubles the voltage but decreases capacitance. Since voltage is squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7bf590021238fd8bde556e627ccdc4c192315e6b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Halving the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;halves&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[halves/doubles]</span> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i>&nbsp;from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">double</span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decrease</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>"	"<div>Halving the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>halves</i></span> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i>&nbsp;from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">double</span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decrease</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because according to the E = ΔV/d equation, voltage is directly proportional to distance. Halving distance doubles the capacitance but decreases voltage by a factor of 4. This decrease in voltage ""overpowers"" the increase in capacitance - halving the total energy.</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Connected plates</u> → doubling distance cuts energy in half, since capacitance is halved but voltage is unchanged (since both plates are connected to the same battery source regardless).</div><div>- <u>Isolated plates</u> → doubling distance doubles the voltage but decreases capacitance. Since voltage is squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7bf590021238fd8bde556e627ccdc4c192315e6b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Halving the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>halves</i></span> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;isolated&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;from"" data-ordinal=""2"">[connected/isolated]</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">double</span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decrease</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>"	"<div>Halving the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>halves</i></span> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i>&nbsp;from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">double</span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decrease</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- To find the total&nbsp;<b>focal length</b>,&nbsp;<u>add</u>&nbsp;the reciprocal of everything up.</div><div>- To find the total&nbsp;<b>power</b>,&nbsp;<u>add</u>&nbsp;everything up (no reciprocals).</div><div>- To find the total&nbsp;<b>magnification</b>,&nbsp;<u>multiply</u>&nbsp;each image's magnification (M<sub>tot</sub>&nbsp;= M<sub>1</sub>M<sub>2</sub>M<sub>3</sub>...).</div><div><sub><br></sub></div><div>&nbsp;<img src=""paste-b4b967021063a24874c63b0299ef1a64d9052a66.jpg""></div></div>"

<div>- Travelling exactly equal to the speed of sound results in the greatest sound intensity.</div><div>- Travelling any faster or slower than the speed of sound will decrease its sound intensity.</div><div><br></div><img src=""clipboard_e4533666e1df42a5c0da289aefe14a615.png""></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Travelling exactly equal to the speed of sound results in the greatest sound intensity.</div><div>- Travelling any faster or slower than the speed of sound will decrease its sound intensity.</div><div><br></div><img src=""clipboard_e4533666e1df42a5c0da289aefe14a615.png""></div>"
"<div>Do these two situations produce <i>equal</i>&nbsp;<b>Doppler shifts</b>?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;No&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x2C;&#x20;the&#x20;Doppler&#x20;shift&#x20;would&#x20;be&#x20;different&#x20;due&#x20;to&#x20;the&#x20;difference&#x20;in&#x20;relative&#x20;velocities&#x20;&#x28;plug&#x20;it&#x20;in&#x20;to&#x20;the&#x20;equation&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><div>- You approach a stationary source of sound, like a radio, at 5 m/s.</div><div>- A source of sound moves towards you at 5 m/s while you're stationary.</div>"	"<div>Do these two situations produce <i>equal</i>&nbsp;<b>Doppler shifts</b>?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>No</u>, the Doppler shift would be different due to the difference in relative velocities (plug it in to the equation).</span></div><div><br></div><div>- You approach a stationary source of sound, like a radio, at 5 m/s.</div><div>- A source of sound moves towards you at 5 m/s while you're stationary.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The <b>Doppler shift (Δf)</b> would be different. This is because the <u>v/c term</u> would be different in each case, depending on which person (v or c) is the one standing still.</div><div>- Δf = frequency shift; f = original source's frequency; Δv = velocity difference between the source &amp; observer; c = speed of sound in the medium.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ac4e8bc33109f1a54c23b5506ceabd25b9c685c0.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c82370dbcc23ea6731f5157a0e77d1e946201b66.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>doubles</i></span> its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;stored&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;PE&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""4"">[property]</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i> from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>"	"<div>Doubling the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>doubles</i></span> its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i> from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>halve</i></span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because according to the E = ΔV/d equation, voltage is directly proportional to distance. Doubling distance cuts the capacitance in half but doubles the voltage. Since voltage is being squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Connected plates</u> → doubling distance cuts energy in half, since capacitance is halved but voltage is unchanged (since both plates are connected to the same battery source regardless).</div><div>- <u>Isolated plates</u> → doubling distance doubles the voltage but decreases capacitance. Since voltage is squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7bf590021238fd8bde556e627ccdc4c192315e6b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Halving the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>halves</i></span> its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;stored&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;PE&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""4"">[property]</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i>&nbsp;from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">double</span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decrease</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>"	"<div>Halving the distance between two plates of a <b>capacitor</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>halves</i></span> its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>stored energy</u> (PE)</span>&nbsp;when the plates are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>isolated</i>&nbsp;from</span> each other, since the capacitance will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">double</span> but voltage will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decrease</i> by a factor of 4</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because according to the E = ΔV/d equation, voltage is directly proportional to distance. Halving distance doubles the capacitance but decreases voltage by a factor of 4. This decrease in voltage ""overpowers"" the increase in capacitance - halving the total energy.</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Connected plates</u> → doubling distance cuts energy in half, since capacitance is halved but voltage is unchanged (since both plates are connected to the same battery source regardless).</div><div>- <u>Isolated plates</u> → doubling distance doubles the voltage but decreases capacitance. Since voltage is squared, this ""overpowers"" the loss of capacitance.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""331f075a39e1afa3b742355ca0ce09cf.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7bf590021238fd8bde556e627ccdc4c192315e6b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7d0b75a1005e9c59677e239dbd88a50e98914bcd.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c7e78d5e954653a6c826d15c0eab07c450344d70.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8305a43293ba05ad0a9edca91ce9406df6f869c4.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>net charge (C)</b></span> of a material <i>after</i> a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">'n' number of <u>electrons</u> were <i>added</i> or <i>removed</i></span>:<div><br><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""C&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;&#x28;n&#x29;&#x28;1&#x2E;6&#x20;x&#x20;10&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;19&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>net charge (C)</b></span> of a material <i>after</i> a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">'n' number of <u>electrons</u> were <i>added</i> or <i>removed</i></span>:<div><br><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">C = (n)(1.6 x 10<sup>-19</sup>)</span><br></div></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>C</b> = net charge; <b>n</b> = number of electrons added or removed.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-56e1b72a1a066e8c0f9ee823e9f32c6f3ca670af.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;net&#x20;charge&#x20;&#x28;C&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of a material <i>after</i> a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x27;n&#x27;&#x20;number&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;u&gt;electrons&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;were&#x20;&lt;i&gt;added&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;or&#x20;&lt;i&gt;removed&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:<div><br><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">C = (n)(1.6 x 10<sup>-19</sup>)</span><br></div></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>net charge (C)</b></span> of a material <i>after</i> a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">'n' number of <u>electrons</u> were <i>added</i> or <i>removed</i></span>:<div><br><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">C = (n)(1.6 x 10<sup>-19</sup>)</span><br></div></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>C</b> = net charge; <b>n</b> = number of electrons added or removed.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-56e1b72a1a066e8c0f9ee823e9f32c6f3ca670af.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>1 <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Angstrom"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is 1 x&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">10<sup style="""">-10</sup></span>&nbsp;m.<br></div>"	"<div>1 <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Angstrom</span>&nbsp;is 1 x&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">10<sup style="""">-10</sup></span>&nbsp;m.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;focal&#x20;length&#x20;&#x28;f&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>mirror</u></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span> an intrinsic property that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>cannot</i></span> change - it is calculated as <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">f = r/2</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>focal length (f)</b></span> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>mirror</u></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span> an intrinsic property that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>cannot</i></span> change - it is calculated as <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">f = r/2</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>f</b> = focal length; <b>r</b> = radius of the mirror's curvature.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1e54f2f0d29538ba74aa3001ec2e0c3943ff750a.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>focal length (f)</b></span> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>mirror</u></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;is&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[is/is not]</span> an intrinsic property that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[can/cannot]</span> change - it is calculated as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""f&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;r&#x2F;2"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>focal length (f)</b></span> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>mirror</u></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span> an intrinsic property that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>cannot</i></span> change - it is calculated as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">f = r/2</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>f</b> = focal length; <b>r</b> = radius of the mirror's curvature.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1e54f2f0d29538ba74aa3001ec2e0c3943ff750a.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>focal length (f)</b></span> of a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;mirror&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[lens/mirror]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span> an intrinsic property that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>cannot</i></span> change - it is calculated as <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">f = r/2</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>focal length (f)</b></span> of a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>mirror</u></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>is</i></span> an intrinsic property that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>cannot</i></span> change - it is calculated as <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">f = r/2</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>f</b> = focal length; <b>r</b> = radius of the mirror's curvature.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1e54f2f0d29538ba74aa3001ec2e0c3943ff750a.jpg""></div>"
"Changing the object distance (o) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;will&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[will/will not]</span> change the <u>mirror's</u> <b>focal length (f)</b>, since that's an intrinsic property that depends <i>only</i> on the mirror's radius of curvature."	"Changing the object distance (o) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>will not</i></span> change the <u>mirror's</u> <b>focal length (f)</b>, since that's an intrinsic property that depends <i>only</i> on the mirror's radius of curvature.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- The focal length (f) of a mirror is calculated as f = r/2.<div>- The 1/f = 1/o + 1/i equation still applies, but treat f as an unchangeable constant and use the rest of the equation to find the other things, like image or object distance.<br><div>- <b>f</b> = focal length; <b>r</b> = radius of the mirror's curvature.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1e54f2f0d29538ba74aa3001ec2e0c3943ff750a.jpg""></div></div>"
"If the <b>focal length (f)</b> of a <u>mirror</u> is an <i>intrinsic</i> property unique to each mirror that depends only on its radius of curvature, why can this equation still be applied?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Treat&#x20;focal&#x20;length&#x20;&#x28;f&#x29;&#x20;like&#x20;a&#x20;constant&#x2E;&#x20;You&#x20;can&#x20;still&#x20;use&#x20;this&#x20;equation&#x20;to&#x20;find&#x20;the&#x20;other&#x20;variables&#x2C;&#x20;like&#x20;the&#x20;object&#x20;or&#x20;image&#x20;distance&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7f5bff09da3aa1fb015eecda60a98e7d4248aa5b.jpg""><br></div></div>"	"If the <b>focal length (f)</b> of a <u>mirror</u> is an <i>intrinsic</i> property unique to each mirror that depends only on its radius of curvature, why can this equation still be applied?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Treat focal length (f) like a constant. You can still use this equation to find the other variables, like the object or image distance.</span><br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7f5bff09da3aa1fb015eecda60a98e7d4248aa5b.jpg""><br></div></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- The focal length (f) of a mirror is calculated as f = r/2.<div>- The 1/f = 1/o + 1/i equation still applies, but treat f as an unchangeable constant and use the rest of the equation to find the other things, like image or object distance.<br><div>- <b>f</b> = focal length; <b>r</b> = radius of the mirror's curvature.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1e54f2f0d29538ba74aa3001ec2e0c3943ff750a.jpg""></div></div>"
"<b>Mirrors</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;do&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[do/do not]</span> have <u>refractive indices</u> (n)."	"<b>Mirrors</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>do not</i></span> have <u>refractive indices</u> (n).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is because light reflects off of mirrors - it doesn't pass through it and get refracted.</div><div><br></div>- The focal length (f) of a mirror is calculated as <b>f = r/2</b>.<div>- The 1/f = 1/o + 1/i equation still applies, but treat f as an unchangeable constant and use the rest of the equation to find the other things, like image or object distance.<br><div>- <b>f</b> = focal length; <b>r</b> = radius of the mirror's curvature.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1e54f2f0d29538ba74aa3001ec2e0c3943ff750a.jpg""></div></div>"
"<b>Light</b> travels <i>slower</i> in mediums with <i>high</i> <u>refractive indices</u> (n) because, in optically dense solutions (like water), its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""atoms&#x20;absorb&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;re&#x2D;emit&#x20;the&#x20;light"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - slowing it down."	"<b>Light</b> travels <i>slower</i> in mediums with <i>high</i> <u>refractive indices</u> (n) because, in optically dense solutions (like water), its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">atoms absorb &amp; re-emit the light</span> - slowing it down.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because more optically dense mediums (like water) have more atoms that absorb &amp; re-emit the light - slowing it down.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-eb0a500b565960f12493865dbbb9663639e3e642.jpg""></div></div>"
"<b>Light</b> travels <i>faster</i> in mediums with <i>low</i> <u>refractive indices</u> (n) because, in a vacuum, there <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""aren&#x27;t&#x20;any&#x20;atoms&#x20;that&#x20;absorb&#x20;and&#x20;re&#x2D;emit&#x20;the&#x20;light"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - allowing it to travel ""uninterrupted."""	"<b>Light</b> travels <i>faster</i> in mediums with <i>low</i> <u>refractive indices</u> (n) because, in a vacuum, there <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">aren't any atoms that absorb and re-emit the light</span> - allowing it to travel ""uninterrupted.""
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-eb0a500b565960f12493865dbbb9663639e3e642.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"On a <b>capacitor</b> connected to a flowing current, the <u>charge</u> (Q) on each plate will <i>eventually</i> level off due to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""gradual&#x20;accumulation&#x20;of&#x20;charge"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - creating <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""repulsions"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"On a <b>capacitor</b> connected to a flowing current, the <u>charge</u> (Q) on each plate will <i>eventually</i> level off due to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">gradual accumulation of charge</span> - creating <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">repulsions</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""6db5d2cadd819cc40fb7936cfffeaa4f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0d3784bf6be2b302637cbd2563ee8c88.png""></div>"
"When a <b>capacitor</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>discharging</i></span>, its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;voltage&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;V&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> gradually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decreases</i></span> due to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i style="""">dissipation</i> of separated charge</span>."	"When a <b>capacitor</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>discharging</i></span>, its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>voltage</u> (V)</span> gradually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decreases</i></span> due to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i style="""">dissipation</i> of separated charge</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because the current allows for the flow of charge, so there isn't a clearly established separation of charge anymore.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6db5d2cadd819cc40fb7936cfffeaa4f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0d3784bf6be2b302637cbd2563ee8c88.png""></div>"
"When a <b>capacitor</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>discharging</i></span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>voltage</u> (V)</span> gradually <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[increases/decreases]</span> due to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;dissipation&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;separated&#x20;charge"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>."	"When a <b>capacitor</b> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>discharging</i></span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>voltage</u> (V)</span> gradually <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decreases</i></span> due to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i style="""">dissipation</i> of separated charge</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because the current allows for the flow of charge, so there isn't a clearly established separation of charge anymore.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6db5d2cadd819cc40fb7936cfffeaa4f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0d3784bf6be2b302637cbd2563ee8c88.png""></div>"
"When a <b>capacitor</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;discharging&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[charging/discharging]</span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>voltage</u> (V)</span> gradually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decreases</i></span> due to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i style="""">dissipation</i> of separated charge</span>."	"When a <b>capacitor</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>discharging</i></span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>voltage</u> (V)</span> gradually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decreases</i></span> due to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i style="""">dissipation</i> of separated charge</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because the current allows for the flow of charge, so there isn't a clearly established separation of charge anymore.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6db5d2cadd819cc40fb7936cfffeaa4f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0d3784bf6be2b302637cbd2563ee8c88.png""></div>"
"When a <b>capacitor</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;charging&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[charging/discharging]</span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>voltage</u> (V)</span> gradually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span> due to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i style="""">increase</i>&nbsp;in separated charge</span>."	"When a <b>capacitor</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>charging</i></span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>voltage</u> (V)</span> gradually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span> due to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i style="""">increase</i>&nbsp;in separated charge</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because charging clearly establishes a separation of charge.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6db5d2cadd819cc40fb7936cfffeaa4f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0d3784bf6be2b302637cbd2563ee8c88.png""></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because charging clearly establishes a separation of charge.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6db5d2cadd819cc40fb7936cfffeaa4f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0d3784bf6be2b302637cbd2563ee8c88.png""></div>"
"The index of refraction (n) of <u>air</u> can be <i>assumed</i> to be approximately <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span>."	"The index of refraction (n) of <u>air</u> can be <i>assumed</i> to be approximately <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Technically only vacuums have refractive indices of 1, but air is close enough.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-3dc98e3fa2ad6c73c1f89a3fb704febe661dfe5f.jpg""></div>"
"In the presence of an external&nbsp;<b>magnetic field (B)</b>, <u>positive</u> &amp; <u>negative</u> charges move in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;opposite&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[the same/opposite]</span> <u>direction(s)</u> from each other, which can be determined by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">either the right-hand (+) or left-hand (-) rule</span>."	"In the presence of an external&nbsp;<b>magnetic field (B)</b>, <u>positive</u> &amp; <u>negative</u> charges move in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> <u>direction(s)</u> from each other, which can be determined by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">either the right-hand (+) or left-hand (-) rule</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Similar to how positive &amp; negative charges move in opposite directions in <u>electric fields</u>, except in magnetic fields, you have to use the right/left-hand rule to figure out exactly where that direction is.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br></div></div>"
"In the presence of an external&nbsp;<b>magnetic field (B)</b>, <u>positive</u> &amp; <u>negative</u> charges move in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> <u>direction(s)</u> from each other, which can be determined by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""either&#x20;the&#x20;right&#x2D;hand&#x20;&#x28;&#x2B;&#x29;&#x20;or&#x20;left&#x2D;hand&#x20;&#x28;&#x2D;&#x29;&#x20;rule"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"In the presence of an external&nbsp;<b>magnetic field (B)</b>, <u>positive</u> &amp; <u>negative</u> charges move in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> <u>direction(s)</u> from each other, which can be determined by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">either the right-hand (+) or left-hand (-) rule</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Similar to how positive &amp; negative charges move in opposite directions in <u>electric fields</u>, except in magnetic fields, you have to use the right/left-hand rule to figure out exactly where that direction is.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""97a86fbf74a9c18d878a3bff62413b64.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation to determine the <u>velocity</u> of a liquid <i>exiting</i> a punctured hole in the side of a fluid-filled container:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""v&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;√&#x28;2gh&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-4550eebf89453be4d1b399e993e5755705d06680.jpg"">"	"<div>Equation to determine the <u>velocity</u> of a liquid <i>exiting</i> a punctured hole in the side of a fluid-filled container:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">v = √(2gh)</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-4550eebf89453be4d1b399e993e5755705d06680.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Height (h) in this case refers to the height of the fluid above the hole.</div><div>- <b>√(2gh)</b> is also used to find the final velocity of an object before it hits the ground (i.e. when gravitational PE is fully converted into KE).</div><div>- This is a simplification of <b>Bernoulli's equation</b> (P + ρgh + 1/2ρv<sup>2</sup> = P + ρgh + 1/2ρv<sup>2</sup>)</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-474f951e4b64fab4f9bd63d03a0613540d86b606.jpg""></div>"
"Equation for the round-trip <i>time</i> of an object thrown directly upwards (to return to the ground) in projectile motion:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""t&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;2v&#x2F;g"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Equation for the round-trip <i>time</i> of an object thrown directly upwards (to return to the ground) in projectile motion:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">t = 2v/g</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>t</b> = round-trip time; <b>v </b>= velocity; <b>g</b> = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-3b745d848519c27d4988cc173f417124800008df.jpg""></div>"
"In a <u>standing wave</u>, the distance between <i>adjacent</i> <b>nodes</b>&nbsp;or <i>adjacent</i> <b>anti-nodes</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""λ&#x2F;2"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In a <u>standing wave</u>, the distance between <i>adjacent</i> <b>nodes</b>&nbsp;or <i>adjacent</i> <b>anti-nodes</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">λ/2</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- 1 wavelength (λ) is the ""peak to peak"" distance. The distance between two nodes or two anti-nodes is half of that.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-12fce9e0c834f23f078c22bd82b619d250305e11.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation to find the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""amount&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;b&gt;work&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x20;done"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> due to an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;elastic&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;force&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">W = F<sub>avg</sub>(distance)</span></div>"	"<div>Equation to find the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">amount of <b>work</b> done</span> due to an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>elastic</i> force</u></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">W = F<sub>avg</sub>(distance)</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-d8eb8faba35c7e7ca48e34f2f2bf9ff8942797b0.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Equation to find the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">amount of <b>work</b> done</span> due to an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>elastic</i> force</u></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""W&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;F&lt;sub&gt;avg&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x28;distance&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Equation to find the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">amount of <b>work</b> done</span> due to an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>elastic</i> force</u></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">W = F<sub>avg</sub>(distance)</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-d8eb8faba35c7e7ca48e34f2f2bf9ff8942797b0.jpg""></div>"
"<div>The <u>area under the curve</u> of a <b>force</b> vs. <b>displacement</b> graph is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""work"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-37a9ad1be595b904943ff057e1694a0d2434a700.jpg"">"	"<div>The <u>area under the curve</u> of a <b>force</b> vs. <b>displacement</b> graph is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">work</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-37a9ad1be595b904943ff057e1694a0d2434a700.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The work done due to an elastic force is <b>W = F<sub>avg</sub>(distance)</b>. Since the area under the curve is always the product of the x &amp; y-axis, F(distance) = W.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-d8eb8faba35c7e7ca48e34f2f2bf9ff8942797b0.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In the equation for the <b>electric field (E)</b>&nbsp;on the <u>perpendicular bisector of a dipole</u>, the <i>direction</i> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;electric&#x20;field&#x27;s&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x20;&lt;b&gt;vector&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> to the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""dipole&#x20;moment"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><div>E = (kp)/r<sup>3</sup></div>"	"<div>In the equation for the <b>electric field (E)</b>&nbsp;on the <u>perpendicular bisector of a dipole</u>, the <i>direction</i> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>electric field's</b> <b>vector</b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> to the direction of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">dipole moment</span>.</div><div><br></div><div>E = (kp)/r<sup>3</sup></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- <b>k</b> = 9 x 10<sup>9</sup>; <b>p</b> = dipole moment; <b>r</b> = radius (distance) between the two charges and the point.</div><div>- Perpendicualr bisector is shown as the line perpendicular between the two charges.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8f5b13f3675a5efdc41254c8c18f187213fbf73f.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"On an electromagnetic wave, <i>doubling</i> the <u>frequency</u> causes the <u>wavelength</u> to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""halve"" data-ordinal=""1"">[double/halve]</span> - the velocity of an electromagnetic wave must <i>always</i> be 3 x 10<sup>8</sup>."	"On an electromagnetic wave, <i>doubling</i> the <u>frequency</u> causes the <u>wavelength</u> to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">halve</span> - the velocity of an electromagnetic wave must <i>always</i> be 3 x 10<sup>8</sup>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>c = fλ</b>. Frequency and wavelength are inversely related.</div><div><br></div><img src=""80b27b2c3bce88d071f97cf63a01ca90.png""></div>"

"Relationship between <b>conductivity (c)</b> &amp; <b>resistivity (ρ)</b>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""c&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;1&#x2F;ρ"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ρ&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;1&#x2F;c"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Relationship between <b>conductivity (c)</b> &amp; <b>resistivity (ρ)</b>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">c = 1/ρ</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ρ = 1/c</span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Inverses of each other.</div>"
"<div>As a <u>sound wave</u> passes from one medium to another, its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;frequency&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[property]</span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does not</i></span> change.<br></div>"	"<div>As a <u>sound wave</u> passes from one medium to another, its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>frequency</b></span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does not</i></span> change.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Its velocity will change depending on the medium, according to the equation for the speed of sound below.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""90fb590216e39b3ddef373ec34676b8d.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-09f322a7b669c981097f55ea6cf42cbb46f011ef.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"As a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>sound</b></span> <b>wave</b> passes from one medium to the next, its velocity <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[does/does not]</span> change - depending on the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;bulk&#x20;modulus&#x20;&#x28;B&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[property]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;density&#x20;&#x28;ρ&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[property]</span> of the other medium."	"As a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>sound</b></span> <b>wave</b> passes from one medium to the next, its velocity <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does</i></span> change - depending on the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>bulk modulus (B)</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>density (ρ)</u></span> of the other medium.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>v</b> = sound wave velocity; <b>B</b> = bulk modulus; <b>ρ</b> = density.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""90fb590216e39b3ddef373ec34676b8d.png""><br></div></div>"
"As a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;sound&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[light/sound]</span> <b>wave</b> passes from one medium to the next, its velocity <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does</i></span> change - depending on the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>bulk modulus (B)</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>density (ρ)</u></span> of the other medium."	"As a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>sound</b></span> <b>wave</b> passes from one medium to the next, its velocity <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does</i></span> change - depending on the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>bulk modulus (B)</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>density (ρ)</u></span> of the other medium.

"As the air <u>temperature</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span>, the <b>speed of</b> <b>sound</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[increases/decreases]</span>, because the individual air molecules have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>greater</i> kinetic energy</span> and can thus vibrate faster."	"As the air <u>temperature</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span>, the <b>speed of</b> <b>sound</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span>, because the individual air molecules have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>greater</i> kinetic energy</span> and can thus vibrate faster.

"The <u>refractive index (n)</u> of a <b>lens</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;is&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[is/is not]</span> an intrinsic property that you should assume <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> change."	"The <u>refractive index (n)</u> of a <b>lens</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>is</i></span> an intrinsic property that you should assume <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">cannot</i></span> change.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

DO NOT confuse this with the focal length, which is NOT an intrinsic property of the lens. The focal length (f) depends on the distance between the lens and the focal point, and that can vary if the difference in relative refractive indices between the two mediums are altered (like a lens in water vs. a lens in air).</div>"
"At the air-water interface on the surface of a liquid, all <u>hydrogen bonds</u> face <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>downward</i></span> - creating a bonding force between surface molecules known as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;surface&#x20;tension&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"At the air-water interface on the surface of a liquid, all <u>hydrogen bonds</u> face <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>downward</i></span> - creating a bonding force between surface molecules known as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>surface tension</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"At the air-water interface on the surface of a liquid, all <u>hydrogen bonds</u> face <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;downward&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[direction]</span> - creating a bonding force between surface molecules known as <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>surface tension</b></span>."	"At the air-water interface on the surface of a liquid, all <u>hydrogen bonds</u> face <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>downward</i></span> - creating a bonding force between surface molecules known as <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>surface tension</b></span>.

"<b>Conductors</b> contain <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;both&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;free&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;atom&#x2D;bound&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[free/atom-bound/both]</span> electrons."	"<b>Conductors</b> contain <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>both</i> <u>free</u> &amp; <u>atom-bound</u></span> electrons.

"The free electrons in <b>conductors</b> arrange themselves on the material's <i>surface</i> and produce an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;internal&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;electric&#x20;field&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cancels out</span> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;external&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;electric&#x20;field&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - resulting in an electric field of net <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">zero</span>."	"The free electrons in <b>conductors</b> arrange themselves on the material's <i>surface</i> and produce an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>internal</i> <u>electric field</u></span> that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cancels out</span> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>external</i> <u>electric field</u></span> - resulting in an electric field of net <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">zero</span>.

"The free electrons in <b>conductors</b> arrange themselves on the material's <i>surface</i> and produce an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>internal</i> <u>electric field</u></span> that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">cancels out</span> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>external</i> <u>electric field</u></span> - resulting in an electric field of net <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">zero</span>.
